results.title_inferred: Introductory and editorial matter for the Bundahishn (Creation / Knowledge from the Zand)
results.original_text: The Bundahishn ("Creation"), or Knowledge from the Zand. This electronic edition prepared by Joseph H. Peterson, 1995. Last updated: Feb 24, 2022. Translated by E. W. West, from Sacred Books of the East, volume 5, Oxford University Press, 1897. NOTE: A new translation by Domenico Agostini and Samuel Thrope is now available from Oxford University Press. NOTE: Also available in PDF version and ePub version. Concerning this Pahlavi text, Mary Boyce has written: "A much more important and fundamental work of compilation is the Bundahishn ('Creation'), also called Zand-agahih ('Knowledge from the Zand'), which survives in two recensions, the Great (or Iranian) Bundahishn and a shortened version, the Indian Bundahishn (deriving from a different MS. tradition). One of the two great Zoroastrian compilations, this work probably grew through different redactions, from some time after the Arab conquest down to 1178 A.C. (when a few additions were made in imperfect Middle Persian). The last important redaction belongs to about the end of the 9th century. The Bundahishn has three main themes: creation, the nature of earthly creatures, and the Kayanians (their lineage and abodes, and the vicissitudes befalling their realm of Eranshahr). The compiler does not name individual sources, but shows an encyclopaedic knowledge of the Zand, and exemplifies excellently the process whereby treatises on chosen themes were created out of the scriptures. Many passages evidently derive fairly closely from the Middle Persian translation, for an Avestan syntax underlies them and one section consists simply of the translation of the 1st chapter of the Vendidad coinciding (except in small details) with the canonical Zand. Glosses and commentaries provide part of the continuous text, and in these, foreign learning is adduced. There are also a few isolated attempts to bring the work up to date, by the identification of traditional (and even mythical) geographical names with Arabic ones. In the main, however, the absorbing interest of the Bd. lies in the antiquity of its material. Here is preserved an ancient, in part pre-Zoroastrian picture of the world, conceived as saucer-shaped, with its rim one great mountain-range, a central peak thrusting up, star-encircled, to cut off the light of the sun by night; a world girdled by two great rivers, from which all other waters flow; in which yearly the gods fight against demons to end drought and famine, and to bring protection to man. Natural phenomena..."
results.translation: The Bundahishn ("Creation"), or Knowledge from the Zand. This electronic edition was prepared by Joseph H. Peterson in 1995 and last updated on February 24, 2022. Translated by E. W. West, from Sacred Books of the East, volume 5, Oxford University Press, 1897. Note: A new translation by Domenico Agostini and Samuel Thrope is now available from Oxford University Press. Note: Also available in PDF and ePub versions. Regarding this Pahlavi text, Mary Boyce has written: "A much more important and fundamental work of compilation is the Bundahishn ('Creation'), also called Zand-agahih ('Knowledge from the Zand'), which survives in two recensions: the Great (or Iranian) Bundahishn and a shortened version, the Indian Bundahishn (deriving from a different manuscript tradition). One of the two great Zoroastrian compilations, this work probably grew through different redactions from some time after the Arab conquest down to 1178 CE (when a few additions were made in imperfect Middle Persian). The last important redaction belongs to approximately the end of the 9th century. The Bundahishn has three main themes: creation, the nature of earthly creatures, and the Kayanians [the legendary Iranian royal dynasty] (their lineage and abodes, and the vicissitudes befalling their realm of Eranshahr). The compiler does not name individual sources, but displays encyclopaedic knowledge of the Zand [the Middle Persian commentary on the Avesta] and exemplifies excellently the process by which treatises on chosen themes were created out of the scriptures. Many passages evidently derive fairly closely from the Middle Persian translation, for an Avestan syntax underlies them, and one section consists simply of the translation of the 1st chapter of the Vendidad [Zoroastrian legal/religious text], coinciding (except in small details) with the canonical Zand. Glosses and commentaries provide part of the continuous text, and in these, foreign learning is adduced. There are also a few isolated attempts to bring the work up to date by identifying traditional (and even mythical) geographical names with Arabic ones. In the main, however, the absorbing interest of the Bundahishn lies in the antiquity of its material. Here is preserved an ancient, in part pre-Zoroastrian, picture of the world conceived as saucer-shaped, with its rim forming one great mountain-range and a central peak thrusting up, star-encircled, to cut off the light of the sun by night; a world girdled by two great rivers from which all other waters flow; a world in which yearly the gods fight against demons to end drought and famine and to bring protection to mankind. Natural phenomena [text continues]..."

entities_mentioned.name: Bundahishn
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A Middle Persian (Pahlavi) Zoroastrian compilation text, also known as Zand-agahih
entities_mentioned.notes: Survives in two recensions: the Great (Iranian) Bundahishn and the Indian Bundahishn. Final major redaction ca. late 9th century CE; some additions dated to 1178 CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Zand-agahih
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternative title for the Bundahishn; means 'Knowledge from the Zand' in Middle Persian
entities_mentioned.notes: The Zand refers to the Middle Persian translation and commentary on the Avesta.

entities_mentioned.name: Zand
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian translation and commentary on the Avestan scriptures of Zoroastrianism

entities_mentioned.name: Vendidad
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A Zoroastrian religious and legal text, part of the Avesta; also spelled Videvdad
entities_mentioned.notes: The Bundahishn includes a translation of the 1st chapter of the Vendidad.

entities_mentioned.name: Kayanians
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The legendary Kayanian (Kayanid) dynasty of Iranian myth and epic tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three main themes of the Bundahishn; their realm is referred to as Eranshahr.

entities_mentioned.name: Eranshahr
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Iranian imperial realm; roughly corresponding to the Sasanian Empire's territory
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in the mythological/legendary sense tied to the Kayanian dynasty.

entities_mentioned.name: E. W. West
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Edward William West (1824–1905), British Pahlavi scholar and translator
entities_mentioned.notes: Translator of the Bundahishn for Sacred Books of the East, vol. 5, 1897.

entities_mentioned.name: Mary Boyce
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mary Boyce (1920–2006), British scholar of Iranian languages and Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Quoted here providing scholarly context for the Bundahishn's composition and content.

results.translator_notes: This segment is entirely editorial and bibliographic front matter — no medical, botanical, or pharmacological content is present. It introduces the Bundahishn as a Zoroastrian cosmological compilation text. No plant, drug, or medical practice is described here. The source text contains HTML markup artifacts (CSS style blocks, navigation links) that have been silently removed as OCR/formatting noise. The text is cut off mid-sentence ('Natural phenomena...') indicating the segment ends abruptly; content continues in subsequent segments. This segment is of limited direct relevance to a Materia Medica archive but may provide context for subsequent chapters describing natural substances, creatures, or cosmological frameworks used in Iranian traditional medicine.

results.title_inferred: Bundahishn Chapter 35: Creation, the Opposition of Spirits, and the Origin of Evil
results.original_text: 0. In the name of the creator Ohrmazd. 1. The Zand-akas ('Zand-knowing or tradition-informed'), which is first about Ohrmazd's original creation and the antagonism of the evil spirit, and afterwards about the nature of the creatures from the original creation till the end, which is the future existence (tanu-i pasino). 2. As revealed by the religion of the Mazdayasnians, so it is declared that Ohrmazd is supreme in omniscience and goodness, and unrivaled in splendor; the region of light is the place of Ohrmazd, which they call 'endless light,' and the omniscience and goodness of the unrivaled Ohrmazd is what they call 'revelation.' 3. Revelation is the explanation of both spirits together; one is he who is independent of unlimited time, because Ohrmazd and the region, religion, and time of Ohrmazd were and are and ever will be; while Ahriman in darkness, with backward understanding and desire for destruction, was in the abyss, and it is he who will not be; and the place of that destruction, and also of that darkness, is what they call the 'endlessly dark.' 4. And between them was empty space, that is, what they call 'air,' in which is now their meeting. 5. Both are limited and unlimited spirits, for the supreme is that which they call endless light, and the abyss that which is endlessly dark, so that between them is a void, and one is not connected with the other; and, again, both spirits are limited as to their own selves. 6. And, secondly, on account of the omniscience of Ohrmazd, both things are in the creation of Ohrmazd, the finite and the infinite; for this they know is that which is in the covenant of both spirits. 7. And, again, the complete sovereignty of the creatures of Ohrmazd is in the future existence, and that also is unlimited for ever and everlasting, and the creatures of Ahriman will perish at the time when the future existence occurs, and that also is eternity. 8. Ohrmazd, through omniscience, knew that Ahriman exists, and whatever he schemes he infuses with malice and greediness till the end; and because He accomplishes the end by many means, He also produced spiritually the creatures which were necessary for those means, and they remained three thousand years in a spiritual state, so that they were unthinking and unmoving, with intangible bodies. 9. The evil spirit, on account of backward knowledge, was not aware of the existence of Ohrmazd; and, afterwards, he arose from the abyss, and came in unto the light which he saw. 10. Desirous of destroying, and because of his malicious nature, he rushed in to destroy that light of Ohrmazd unassailed by fiends, and he saw its bravery and glory were greater than his own; so he fled back to the gloomy darkness, and formed many demons and fiends; and the creatures of the destroyer arose for violence. 11. Ohrmazd, by whom the creatures of the evil spirit were seen, creatures terrible, corrupt, and bad, also considered them not commendable (burzishnik). 12. Afterwards, the evil spirit [text ends mid-sentence].
results.translation: 0. In the name of the creator Ohrmazd. 1. The Zand-akas [literally 'Zand-knowing,' or 'informed by tradition'], which treats first of Ohrmazd's original creation and the antagonism of the evil spirit, and afterwards of the nature of creatures from the original creation until the end, which is the future existence [Pahlavi: tanu-i pasino, i.e., the final bodily resurrection]. 2. As revealed by the religion of the Mazdayasnians [Zoroastrians], it is declared that Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] is supreme in omniscience and goodness, and unrivaled in splendor; the region of light is the place of Ohrmazd, which they call 'endless light,' and the omniscience and goodness of the unrivaled Ohrmazd is what they call 'revelation.' 3. Revelation is the explanation of both spirits together; one is he who is independent of unlimited time, because Ohrmazd and the region, religion, and time of Ohrmazd were and are and ever will be; while Ahriman [Angra Mainyu, the evil spirit] in darkness, with backward understanding and desire for destruction, dwelt in the abyss, and it is he who will not be; and the place of that destruction, and also of that darkness, is what they call the 'endlessly dark.' 4. And between them was empty space, that is, what they call 'air,' in which their meeting now takes place. 5. Both are at once limited and unlimited spirits: the supreme realm is that which they call endless light, and the abyss is that which is endlessly dark, so that between them is a void, and one is not connected with the other; and again, both spirits are limited with respect to their own selves. 6. And secondly, on account of the omniscience of Ohrmazd, both things exist within the creation of Ohrmazd — the finite and the infinite — for this, they know, is what is contained in the covenant between both spirits. 7. And again, the complete sovereignty over the creatures of Ohrmazd belongs to the future existence, which also is unlimited, for ever and everlasting; and the creatures of Ahriman will perish at the time when the future existence comes to pass, and that too is eternity. 8. Ohrmazd, through omniscience, knew that Ahriman exists, and that whatever Ahriman schemes he infuses with malice and greediness until the end; and because Ohrmazd accomplishes that end by many means, He also produced spiritually the creatures that were necessary for those means, and they remained in a spiritual state for three thousand years, so that they were unthinking and unmoving, with intangible bodies. 9. The evil spirit, on account of his backward knowledge, was not aware of the existence of Ohrmazd; but afterwards he arose from the abyss and came toward the light which he perceived. 10. Desirous of destroying, and on account of his malicious nature, he rushed in to destroy the light of Ohrmazd — that light unassailed by fiends — but when he saw that its bravery and glory were greater than his own, he fled back into the gloomy darkness and formed many demons and fiends; and thus the creatures of the destroyer arose for the purpose of violence. 11. Ohrmazd, who perceived the creatures of the evil spirit — creatures terrible, corrupt, and bad — considered them not commendable [Pahlavi: burzishnik, i.e., not praiseworthy or not to be exalted]. 12. Afterwards, the evil spirit [text breaks off mid-sentence].

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahura Mazda — the supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Pahlavi form of Avestan 'Ahura Mazda.' Described here as omniscient, supremely good, and the creator of all beneficent creatures.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahriman
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angra Mainyu — the evil spirit / destructive principle in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Pahlavi form of Avestan 'Angra Mainyu.' Described as dwelling in the abyss, possessing 'backward knowledge,' and motivated by malice and the desire to destroy.

entities_mentioned.name: Zand-akas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Epithet for the Bundahishn or its tradition of Zand-based knowledge
entities_mentioned.notes: Pahlavi term meaning 'Zand-knowing' or 'informed by the Zand [commentary tradition].' Used as a title or description for this cosmological text.

entities_mentioned.name: Mazdayasnians
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrians — followers of the Mazdayasna (Mazda-worshipping) religion
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan/Pahlavi term for adherents of the Zoroastrian faith, literally 'those who worship Mazda.'

entities_mentioned.name: tanu-i pasino
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian eschatological concept of bodily resurrection / the final renovation (Frashokereti)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pahlavi phrase meaning 'the later/future body' or 'future existence,' referring to the resurrection of the body at the end of time.

entities_mentioned.name: endless light
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The realm of Ohrmazd in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the two primordial realms; the other being 'endless darkness,' the realm of Ahriman.

entities_mentioned.name: endlessly dark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The realm of Ahriman / the abyss in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: The primordial realm of darkness and destruction associated with Ahriman.

entities_mentioned.name: burzishnik
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pahlavi adjective: 'commendable,' 'praiseworthy,' or 'exalted'
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in the negative: Ohrmazd did not consider the creatures of Ahriman to be burzishnik (worthy of praise or elevation).

results.translator_notes: This segment is the opening cosmological section of the Bundahishn (Middle Persian: 'Original Creation'), a Zoroastrian encyclopedic text compiled in the 9th–12th centuries CE but drawing on much older Avestan and Pahlavi traditions. The text as presented here is already in English (it appears to be the classic West translation, c. 1880, from the Sacred Books of the East series), so this task has involved lightly editing and clarifying the existing translation rather than translating from a non-English source. The segment breaks off mid-sentence at section 12, indicating the source excerpt is incomplete. No plants, drugs, or medical preparations are described in this segment; it is purely cosmological/theological in content and has no direct Materia Medica relevance, though the Bundahishn as a whole contains botanical and zoological chapters. The label 'chapter_35' is somewhat anomalous as this content corresponds to Chapter 1 (the cosmogony) of the standard Bundahishn — the chapter numbering may reflect a different manuscript tradition or editorial system.

results.title_inferred: The Rush of Ahriman (the Destroyer) upon Creation: The Bundahishn Account
results.original_text: 1. On the rush of the destroyer at the creatures it is said, in revelation, that the evil spirit, when he saw the impotence of himself and the confederate (ham-dast) demons, owing to the righteous man, became confounded, and seemed in confusion three thousand years. 2. During that confusion the arch-fiends of the demons severally shouted thus: 'Rise up, thou father of us! for we will cause a conflict in the world, the distress and injury from which will become those of Ohrmazd and the archangels.' 3. Severally they twice recounted their own evil deeds, and it pleased him not; and that wicked evil spirit, through fear of the righteous man, was not able to lift up his head until the wicked Jeh came, at the completion of the three thousand years. 4. And she shouted to the evil spirit thus: 'Rise up, thou father of us! for I will cause that conflict in the world wherefrom the distress and injury of Ohrmazd and the archangels will arise.' 5. And she twice recounted severally her own evil deeds, and it pleased him not; and that wicked evil spirit rose not from that confusion, through fear of the righteous man. 6. And, again, the wicked Jeh shouted thus: 'Rise up, thou father of us! for in that conflict I will shed thus much vexation on the righteous man and the laboring ox that, through my deeds, life will not be wanted, and I will destroy their living souls (nismo); I will vex the water, I will vex the plants, I will vex the fire of Ohrmazd, I will make the whole creation of Ohrmazd vexed.' 7. And she so recounted those evil deeds a second time, that the evil spirit was delighted and started up from that confusion; and he kissed Jeh upon the head, and the pollution which they call menstruation became apparent in Jeh. 8. He shouted to Jeh thus: 'What is thy wish? so that I may give it thee.' And Jeh shouted to the evil spirit thus: 'A man is the wish, so give it to me.' 9. The form of the evil spirit was a log-like lizard's (vazak) body, and he appeared a young man of fifteen years to Jeh, and that brought the thoughts of Jeh to him. 10. Afterwards, the evil spirit, with the confederate demons, went towards the luminaries, and he saw the sky; and he led them up, fraught with malicious intentions. 11. He stood upon one-third of the inside of the sky, and he sprang, like a snake, out of the sky down to the earth. 12. In the month Frawardin and the day Ohrmazd he rushed in at noon, and thereby the sky was as shattered and frightened by him, as a sheep by a wolf. 13. He came on to the water which was arranged below the earth, and then the middle of this earth was pierced and entered by him. 14. Afterwards, he came to the vegetation, then to the ox, then to Gayomard, and then he came to fire; so, just like a fly, he rushed out upon the whole creation; and he made the world quite as injured and dark at midday as though it were in dark night. 15. And noxious creatures were diffused by him over the earth, biting and venomous, such as the snake, scorpion, frog [text breaks off].
results.translation: 1. Concerning the assault of the Destroyer [Ahriman] upon the creatures, it is stated in revelation that the Evil Spirit, perceiving his own powerlessness and that of his allied [ham-dast] demons in the face of the righteous man, fell into bewilderment and remained in a state of confusion for three thousand years. 2. During that confusion the chief demons each cried out in turn: 'Arise, thou our father! For we shall bring strife into the world, and the suffering and harm resulting therefrom shall fall upon Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] and the archangels [Amesha Spentas].' 3. Each of them recounted his own evil deeds twice over, but this did not please him; and that wicked Evil Spirit, out of fear of the righteous man, was unable to raise his head until the wicked Jeh came, at the end of the three thousand years. 4. And she cried out to the Evil Spirit thus: 'Arise, thou our father! For I shall bring about that strife in the world from which the suffering and harm of Ohrmazd and the archangels shall arise.' 5. And she likewise recounted her own evil deeds twice, each in turn, but this did not please him; and that wicked Evil Spirit did not rise from his confusion, out of fear of the righteous man. 6. And once more the wicked Jeh cried out: 'Arise, thou our father! For in that strife I shall inflict such vexation upon the righteous man and the laboring ox that, through my deeds, life itself shall not be desired, and I shall destroy their living souls [nismo]; I shall vex the water, I shall vex the plants, I shall vex the fire of Ohrmazd, I shall make the whole creation of Ohrmazd vexed.' 7. And she recounted those evil deeds a second time, in such a manner that the Evil Spirit was gladdened and sprang up from his confusion; and he kissed Jeh upon the head, and the defilement which is called menstruation became manifest in Jeh. 8. He cried out to Jeh: 'What is thy desire? that I may grant it thee.' And Jeh cried out to the Evil Spirit: 'A man is my desire — give him to me.' 9. The form of the Evil Spirit was that of a thick, log-like lizard's [vazak] body, yet he appeared to Jeh as a young man of fifteen years, and this turned Jeh's thoughts toward him. 10. Thereafter the Evil Spirit, together with his allied demons, advanced toward the luminaries and beheld the sky; and he led them upward, filled with malicious intent. 11. He stationed himself upon one-third of the inner expanse of the sky, and then sprang, like a snake, out of the sky down to the earth. 12. In the month of Frawardin and on the day of Ohrmazd he broke in at noon, and thereby the sky was as shattered and terrified by him as a sheep is by a wolf. 13. He came upon the water that lay arranged beneath the earth, and then pierced and entered into the very midst of the earth. 14. Thereafter he came to the vegetation, then to the ox, then to Gayomard [the primordial man], and then to fire; and, just like a fly, he rushed out upon the whole of creation; and he made the world as injured and dark at midday as though it were the darkest night. 15. And noxious creatures were spread by him across the earth, biting and venomous — such as the snake, the scorpion, the frog [text breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: Evil Spirit / Destroyer / Ahriman
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angra Mainyu (Ahriman) — the supreme evil being in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'the Destroyer' in the chapter heading; ham-dast (allied/confederate) demons serve under him.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahura Mazda — the supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Also the name of a calendar day (day Ohrmazd, month Frawardin) referenced in section 12.

entities_mentioned.name: Archangels
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amesha Spentas — the six or seven divine emanations/beneficent immortals of Zoroastrian theology

entities_mentioned.name: Jeh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jahi — the demoness of licentiousness in Zoroastrian tradition; associated with menstruation as a pollution introduced by evil
entities_mentioned.notes: Her kiss from Ahriman is the mythological origin of menstruation (druj) according to Bundahishn tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: nismo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for 'breath of life' or 'living soul' (cf. Avestan nāsman)
entities_mentioned.notes: Preserved in brackets in the text; relates to the animating life-force.

entities_mentioned.name: vazak
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A lizard species; possibly a monitor lizard or large gecko; exact species uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian term preserved in brackets; the description 'log-like body' suggests a large, heavy-bodied reptile.

entities_mentioned.name: Frawardin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: First month of the Zoroastrian calendar (roughly corresponding to March–April)
entities_mentioned.notes: The day 'Ohrmazd' is the first day of the Zoroastrian month, making this the very first day of the first month — cosmologically significant.

entities_mentioned.name: Gayomard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gayōmart — the primordial first man in Zoroastrian cosmogony
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as one of the seven primordial creations attacked by Ahriman: sky, water, earth, plants, ox (Gōshūrvan/primeval bull), Gayomard, and fire.

entities_mentioned.name: Snake
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Serpent (order Squamata, suborder Serpentes); classified as a noxious creature (khrafstar) in Zoroastrian tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed among venomous creatures spread by Ahriman; khrafstars (noxious creatures) are a key category in Zoroastrian natural philosophy.

entities_mentioned.name: Scorpion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Order Scorpiones; classified as a khrafstar (noxious creature)

entities_mentioned.name: Frog
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Order Anura; classified as a khrafstar in Zoroastrian tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Text breaks off after 'frog'; the list of noxious creatures likely continues.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Chapter 37 of the Greater Bundahishn (Iranian Book of Primal Creation), a Middle Persian Zoroastrian cosmogonic text compiled in its extant form approximately 9th–12th century CE, drawing on much older Avestan tradition. The translation presented in the source is largely that of E.W. West (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 5, 1880), with minor editorial cleaning. This text is NOT a materia medica source in the pharmacological sense; however, it contains significant ethnobiological content: (1) the classification of animals as 'noxious creatures' (khrafstar) — snakes, scorpions, frogs — reflects a Zoroastrian natural-philosophical taxonomy with direct implications for how these creatures were understood medically and ritually; (2) the demonological origin of menstruation (via Jeh) is a primary theological statement influencing ritual purity laws with medical consequences; (3) the vazak lizard description may be of zoological interest. Archivists should note that the segment is cosmogonic mythology, not a medical prescription, and its relevance to Materia Medica Americana is indirect (comparative/ethnobiological). The text breaks off mid-sentence in section 15; subsequent khrafstars (noxious creatures) are not listed.

results.title_inferred: The Soul of the Primeval Ox (Goshorun) and the Promise of Zarathustra
results.original_text: 1. This also is said, that when the primeval ox passed away it fell to the right hand, and Gayomard afterwards, when he passed away, to the left hand. 2. Goshorun, as the soul of the primeval ox came out from the body of the ox, stood up before the ox and cried to Ohrmazd, as much as a thousand men when they sustain a cry at one time, thus: 'With whom is the guardianship of the creatures left by thee, when ruin has broken into the earth and vegetation is withered, and water is troubled? Where is the man of whom it was said by thee thus: I will produce him, so that he may preach carefulness?' 3. And Ohrmazd spoke thus: 'You are made ill, O Goshorun! you have the illness which the evil spirit brought on; if it were proper to produce that man in this earth at this time, the evil spirit would not have been oppressive in it.' 4. Forth Goshorun walked to the star station (payak) and cried in the same manner, and forth to the moon station and cried in the same manner, and forth to the sun station, and then the guardian spirit of Zartosht was exhibited to her, and Ohrmazd said thus: 'I will produce for the world him who will preach carefulness.' 5. Contented became the spirit Goshorun, and assented thus: 'I will nourish the creatures;' that is, she became again consenting to a worldly creation in the world.
results.translation: 1. It is also related that when the primeval ox died, it fell to the right side, and Gayomard afterwards, when he died, fell to the left side. 2. Goshorun — being the soul of the primeval ox — came forth from the body of the ox, stood before it, and cried out to Ohrmazd with a voice as loud as that of a thousand men crying out together, saying: 'To whom have you entrusted the guardianship of your creatures, now that destruction has broken into the earth, vegetation has withered, and the waters are fouled? Where is the man of whom you said: I shall bring him forth so that he may teach righteousness [carefulness]?' 3. And Ohrmazd replied: 'You have been made ill, O Goshorun! You suffer the sickness that the evil spirit has inflicted; were it fitting to bring that man into this world at this time, the evil spirit would not have been able to oppress it.' 4. Then Goshorun went forth to the station [payak] of the stars and cried out in the same manner, and then went forth to the station of the moon and cried out in the same manner, and then went forth to the station of the sun — and there the guardian spirit [fravashi] of Zarathustra was revealed to her, and Ohrmazd declared: 'I shall bring forth for the world the one who will teach righteousness [carefulness].' 5. The spirit Goshorun was satisfied and consented, saying: 'I will nourish the creatures' — meaning that she once again agreed to the continuance of worldly creation in the world.

entities_mentioned.name: Primeval Ox (Gaw i Evodad / Gav Aevodhata)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological first created animal in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: The primeval or uniquely-created ox is a central figure in Zoroastrian creation myth; its death by Ahriman marks the origin of animal life from its seed.

entities_mentioned.name: Goshorun (Geush Urvan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The 'Soul of the Cow/Ox' — a divine yazata in Zoroastrian theology
entities_mentioned.notes: Geush Urvan appears in the Gathas (Yasna 29) in a similar lament before Ahura Mazda; her role as protector and nourisher of cattle and creatures is well attested.

entities_mentioned.name: Gayomard (Gayomart)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The first mortal man in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: Gayomard is the primordial human whose death, like that of the primeval ox, was caused by Ahriman (the evil spirit). His falling to the left is a cosmological detail specific to the Bundahishn.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd (Ahura Mazda)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: The creator god who responds to Goshorun's lament and promises the coming of Zarathustra.

entities_mentioned.name: Zartosht (Zarathustra / Zoroaster)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The prophet-founder of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: His guardian spirit (fravashi) is shown to Goshorun at the solar station as the promised savior who will 'preach carefulness' (i.e., teach righteous living and piety).

entities_mentioned.name: Star station, Moon station, Sun station (payak)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cosmological celestial tiers in Zoroastrian cosmography
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'payak' (station or rank) refers to the three ascending celestial spheres through which souls and spirits travel; this mirrors the structure described elsewhere in the Bundahishn and in the Ardā Wirāz Nāmag.

entities_mentioned.name: Evil spirit (Ahriman / Angra Mainyu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The supreme evil being in Zoroastrian dualistic theology
entities_mentioned.notes: Blamed for the illness and suffering described by Goshorun; Ohrmazd uses the evil spirit's oppression as the reason Zarathustra cannot yet be sent into the world.

results.translator_notes: This passage is a prose retelling of material closely paralleling Yasna 29 of the Avesta (the 'Complaint of the Soul of the Cow'). The word 'carefulness' is the translator's (E.W. West's) rendering of a concept better glossed as 'righteousness,' 'piety,' or 'proper religious observance' [Av. aramaiti or related terms]. The term 'payak' (station) is retained in brackets in the original translation and is worth preserving as a technical cosmological term. The guardian spirit shown to Goshorun is Zarathustra's fravashi (pre-existent soul/spirit), a key Zoroastrian theological concept not explicitly named in this passage but implied by context. This segment has no direct relevance to materia medica or pharmacological practice; it is cosmological-mythological in nature.

results.title_inferred: The Evil Spirit's Assault on the Earth and the Formation of Mountains
results.original_text: 0. On the conflict which the evil spirit waged with the earth. 1. As the evil spirit rushed in, the earth shook, and the substance of mountains was created in the earth. 2. First, Mount Alburz arose; afterwards, the other ranges of mountains (kofaniha) of the middle of the earth; for as Alburz grew forth all the mountains remained in motion, for they have all grown forth from the root of Alburz. 3. At that time they came up from the earth, like a tree which has grown up to the clouds and its root to the bottom; and their root passed on that way from one to the other, and they are arranged in mutual connection. 4. Afterwards, about that wonderful shaking out from the earth, they say that a great mountain is the knot of lands; and the passage for the waters within the mountains is the root which is below the mountains; they forsake the upper parts so that they may flow into it, just as the roots of trees pass into the earth; a counterpart (anguni-aitak) of the blood in the arteries of men, which gives strength to the whole body. 5. In numbers, apart from Alburz, all the mountains grew up out of the earth in eighteen years, from which arises the perfection of men's advantage.
results.translation: 0. Concerning the conflict which the Evil Spirit waged against the earth. 1. When the Evil Spirit rushed in, the earth trembled, and the substance of mountains was brought into being within the earth. 2. Mount Alburz arose first; thereafter, the other mountain ranges [kofaniha] of the middle of the earth followed; for as Alburz grew upward, all the other mountains were set in motion, since they have all grown forth from the root of Alburz. 3. At that time they rose up from the earth like a tree that has grown into the clouds while its root reaches to the very depths; and their roots extended in this manner from one range to the next, so that they are arranged in mutual interconnection. 4. Subsequently, concerning that remarkable upheaval from the earth, it is said that a great mountain is the knot or nexus of the lands; and the passage for waters running through the mountains is the root lying beneath them — the waters leave the upper parts so as to flow down into these channels, just as the roots of trees extend into the earth — a counterpart [anguni-aitak] of the blood in the arteries of men, which imparts strength to the whole body. 5. In total, apart from Alburz itself, all the mountains grew up out of the earth over the course of eighteen years, and from this arises the fullness of benefit to mankind.

entities_mentioned.name: Evil Spirit (Ahriman)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahriman — the Zoroastrian principle of evil and destruction
entities_mentioned.notes: The Avestan/Middle Persian 'Angra Mainyu' or 'Ahriman'; the chief antagonist in Zoroastrian cosmology, whose assault on creation is a central theme of the Bundahishn.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Alburz (Harā Bərəzaitī)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological cosmic mountain; associated by some scholars with the Alborz mountain range in northern Iran, but primarily a cosmological construct in Zoroastrian thought
entities_mentioned.notes: In the Bundahishn, Alburz (Avestan: Harā Bərəzaitī) is the primordial mountain from whose root all other mountains derive; it is a mythological rather than strictly geographical referent, though it is often mapped onto the Alborz range of Iran.

entities_mentioned.name: kofaniha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for mountain ranges
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian plural noun for mountain ranges or mountain chains; preserved in brackets in the original translation as a technical term.

entities_mentioned.name: anguni-aitak
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term meaning 'counterpart' or 'analogue'
entities_mentioned.notes: A Middle Persian term used here to draw an explicit analogy between the subterranean water channels of mountains and the arterial blood system of the human body — a noteworthy instance of macrocosm-microcosm reasoning in Zoroastrian cosmological literature.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Chapter 42 of the Bundahishn, a Middle Persian Zoroastrian cosmological text compiled in its extant form likely between the 8th and 12th centuries CE, drawing on much older Avestan sources. The passage contains no direct materia medica content; however, it is notable for its use of anatomical analogy — the mountain-root water system is explicitly compared to the arterial blood system of the human body (anguni-aitak), which may be of interest for the history of physiological thought in the Zoroastrian tradition. The text as rendered here follows the West translation tradition. The term 'kofaniha' and 'anguni-aitak' are retained from the source as technical Middle Persian vocabulary. No medicinal plants, drugs, or therapeutic practices are described in this segment.

results.title_inferred: On the Nature and Origin of Plants, Disease-Countering Herbs, and the Gokard Tree
results.original_text: [On the nature of plants] 1. The conflict waged with plants was that when they became quite dry. 2. Amerodad the archangel, as the vegetation was his own, pounded the plants small, and mixed them up with the water which Tishtar seized, and Tishtar made that water rain down upon the whole earth. 3. On the whole earth plants grew up like hair upon the heads of men. 4. Ten thousand of them grew forth of one special description, for keeping away the ten thousand species of disease which the evil spirit produced for the creatures; and from those ten thousand, the 100,000 species of plants have grown forth. 5. From that same germ of plants the tree of all germs was given forth, and grew up in the wide-formed ocean, from which the germs of all species of plants ever increased. 6. And near to that tree of all germs, the Gokard [=Gokaren] tree was produced, for keeping away deformed (dushpad) decrepitude; and the full perfection of the world arose therefrom.
results.translation: [On the nature of plants] 1. The conflict waged against plants was that they had become entirely dry. 2. Ameretat [Amerodad], the archangel [Amesha Spenta] who held dominion over vegetation, ground the plants into a fine powder and mixed them with the waters that the star-deity Tishtrya [Tishtar] had gathered; and Tishtrya caused those waters to rain down upon the whole earth. 3. Across the entire earth, plants then grew up like hair upon the heads of men. 4. Ten thousand of them grew forth of a single particular kind [or class], for the purpose of warding off the ten thousand species of disease that the evil spirit [Ahriman] had inflicted upon living creatures; and from those ten thousand species, the one hundred thousand varieties of plants subsequently grew forth. 5. From that same primordial seed [germ] of plants, the Tree of All Seeds was brought forth and grew up in the wide-formed ocean [Vourukasha], from which the seeds of all species of plants have ever since been multiplied. 6. And near to that Tree of All Seeds, the Gokard [Gokaren] tree was produced, for the purpose of warding off 'dushpad' [malformed or deformed] decrepitude [old age and physical deterioration]; and the full perfection of the world arose from it.

entities_mentioned.name: Amerodad (Ameretat)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian Amesha Spenta (Bounteous Immortal) presiding over plants and immortality
entities_mentioned.notes: Ameretat is one of the six Amesha Spentas in Zoroastrian theology, specifically associated with plant life and immortality. The name means 'immortality' in Avestan. His cosmological role here is the preservation and propagation of medicinal plant knowledge.

entities_mentioned.name: Tishtar (Tishtrya)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian yazata (divine being) associated with the star Sirius and rain-bringing
entities_mentioned.notes: Tishtrya is a well-attested Zoroastrian deity of rain and water, equated with the star Sirius. His function here as the agent who gathers and releases water is consistent with his wider mythological role.

entities_mentioned.name: Tree of All Germs (Tree of All Seeds)
entities_mentioned.notes: A mythological cosmic tree said to grow in the Vourukasha ocean and to be the source of all plant species. This is a recurrent motif in Zoroastrian cosmogony (Bundahishn). No single real-world botanical identification is possible.

entities_mentioned.name: Gokard tree (Gokaren)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ephedra (haoma plant) or a mythological haoma tree; identification remains debated
entities_mentioned.notes: The Gokaren (also White Haoma) is described in the Bundahishn and other Pahlavi texts as a tree growing in the cosmic ocean near the Tree of All Seeds. It is associated with immortality and the restoration of the body. Some scholars tentatively associate it with the haoma/soma plant (possibly Ephedra spp. or Peganum harmala), but its mythological function as a cure for 'dushpad' decrepitude (malformed old age/physical deterioration) is clear. The term 'dushpad' is a Pahlavi compound meaning roughly 'badly footed' or 'malformed,' here applied to the infirmities of old age.

entities_mentioned.name: Ten thousand disease-countering plants
entities_mentioned.notes: A cosmological enumeration: 10,000 plant types corresponding to 10,000 diseases created by Ahriman (the evil spirit), from which 100,000 further species derive. This reflects the Zoroastrian dualistic framework in which every evil has a divinely ordained counter-remedy. This passage is significant for Materia Medica purposes as it encodes a foundational doctrine that medicinal plants exist specifically to combat disease.

results.translator_notes: This passage is from the Bundahishn ('Original Creation'), a Pahlavi (Middle Persian) Zoroastrian encyclopedic text compiled roughly 8th–9th century CE, drawing on much older Avestan traditions. The chapter concerns the cosmogonic origin of plants and their medicinal function. Key medical-botanical doctrines encoded here: (1) plants were cosmologically designed to counteract disease on a species-for-species basis; (2) all plant diversity derives from a primordial seed-source; (3) the Gokard/Gokaren tree has a specific therapeutic role against physical degeneration associated with aging ('dushpad' decrepitude). The bracketed gloss '[=Gokaren]' was present in the source text. The phrase 'wide-formed ocean' translates a standard Pahlavi epithet for the Vourukasha, the mythological cosmic sea. 'Dushpad' is retained in brackets as it is a technical Pahlavi term without a clean single-word English equivalent.

results.title_inferred: Origin of Plants, Medicinal Herbs, and Animals from the Primeval Ox
results.original_text: 0. On the conflict waged with the primeval ox. 1. As it passed away, owing to the vegetable principle (chiharak) proceeding from every limb of the ox, fifty and five species of grain and twelve species of medicinal plants grew forth from the earth, and their splendour and strength were the seminal energy (tokhmih) of the ox. 2. Delivered to the moon station, that seed was thoroughly purified by the light of the moon, fully prepared in every way, and produced life in a body. 3. Thence arose two oxen, one male and one female; and, afterwards, two hundred and eighty-two species of each kind became manifest upon the earth. 4. The dwelling (manist) of the birds is in the air, and the fish are in the midst of the water.
results.translation: 0. Concerning the conflict waged against the primeval ox. 1. As it perished, by virtue of the vegetable principle [chiharak] emanating from every limb of the ox, fifty-five species of grain and twelve species of medicinal plants grew forth from the earth; their splendour and vital force derived from the seminal energy [tokhmih] of the ox. 2. Conveyed to the lunar station [moon station], that seed was thoroughly purified by the light of the moon, fully prepared in every respect, and brought forth life within a body. 3. From thence arose two oxen, one male and one female; and thereafter, two hundred and eighty-two species of each kind appeared upon the earth. 4. The dwelling [manist] of the birds is in the air, and the fish dwell in the midst of the water.

entities_mentioned.name: primeval ox (Gāv ī Ēwdād / Gāw ī Yēkdād)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological creature; the 'Uniquely-Created Bull' of Zoroastrian cosmogony
entities_mentioned.notes: The death of the primeval ox is a foundational Zoroastrian creation myth recorded in the Bundahishn. Its body is said to be the source of all plant life, grains, and medicinal herbs, as well as the origin of animal species.

entities_mentioned.name: chiharak (vegetable/plant principle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian technical term denoting the essential or generative vegetable nature inherent in the ox's body. Functions as a cosmological vitalist concept.

entities_mentioned.name: tokhmih (seminal energy / seed-force)
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian term for seed or generative potency; here used to explain the cosmic origin of the vitality of plants and animals.

entities_mentioned.name: fifty-five species of grain
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unspecified cereals and cultivated grains; number is traditional/cosmological
entities_mentioned.notes: The precise enumeration of 55 grain species is a cosmological convention in the Bundahishn and does not correspond to a specific botanical list elsewhere preserved.

entities_mentioned.name: twelve species of medicinal plants
entities_mentioned.notes: Extracted entity for Materia Medica purposes. The text identifies twelve classes of medicinal plants as originating from the body of the primeval ox. No preparation method, specific ailment, or individual plant names are given in this passage. Condition treated: general; the plants are of cosmic/mythological origin rather than a specific therapeutic context here.

entities_mentioned.name: moon station (lunar station)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Conceptual celestial purification locus in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: The moon's role in purifying and vitalising the ox's seed before redistribution as new life reflects Zoroastrian eschatological and cosmological doctrine.

entities_mentioned.name: manist (dwelling)
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian term for abode or habitation; used here to classify animals by their natural environment (air for birds, water for fish).

results.translator_notes: This segment is from chapter 44 of the Bundahishn, a Middle Persian Zoroastrian cosmogonical text compiled in its extant form circa 8th–12th century CE but drawing on much older Avestan traditions. The passage is of particular interest for Materia Medica Americana as a comparative source: it presents a mythological aetiology for medicinal plant diversity (twelve species) and grain diversity (fifty-five species), both arising from the sacrificial death of the primeval ox. No specific plant names, preparations, or therapeutic indications are enumerated in this passage; the medical relevance is cosmological and taxonomic rather than pharmacological. The translation follows the standard scholarly English rendering conventions for this text (cf. West, SBE vol. 5). The parenthetical glosses in brackets reproduce the original translator's Middle Persian technical terms for archival precision.

results.title_inferred: The Seven Regions (Keshvars) of the Earth and the Primacy of Khvaniras
results.original_text: 1. On the nature of the earth it says in revelation, that there are thirty and three kinds of land. 2. On the day when Tishtar produced the rain, when its seas arose therefrom, the whole place, half taken up by water, was converted into seven portions; this portion, as much as one-half, is the middle, and six portions are around; those six portions are together as much as Khvaniras. 3. The name keshvar ('zone or region') is also applied to them, and they existed side by side (kash kash); as on the east side of this portion (Khvaniras) is the Savah region, on the west is the Arzah region; the two portions on the south side are the Fradadafsh and Vidadafsh regions, the two portions on the north side are the Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht regions, and that in the middle is Khvaniras. 4. And Khvaniras has the sea, for one part of the wide-formed ocean wound about around it; and from Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht a lofty mountain grew up; so that it is not possible for any one to go from region to region. 5. And of these seven regions every benefit was created most in Khvaniras, and the evil spirit also produced most for Khvaniras, on account of the superiority (sarih) which he saw in it. 6. For the Kayanians and heroes were created in Khvaniras; and the good religion of the Mazdayasnians was created in Khvaniras, and afterwards conveyed to the other regions; Soshyans is born in Khvaniras, who makes the evil spirit impotent, and causes the resurrection and future existence.
results.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of the earth, it is stated in revelation that there are thirty-three kinds of land. 2. On the day when Tishtar [the star Sirius, associated with rain] produced the rain, when seas arose from it, the entire expanse—half of which was taken up by water—was divided into seven portions. This central portion amounts to one half, and the six portions surrounding it together equal Khvaniras [the central, inhabited zone]. 3. These are also called keshvar [literally 'zone' or 'region'], and they lay side by side (kash kash). On the east side of Khvaniras lies the Savah region; on the west, the Arzah region; the two portions to the south are the Fradadafsh and Vidadafsh regions; the two portions to the north are the Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht regions; and the central one is Khvaniras. 4. Khvaniras is bounded by the sea, for one arm of the vast primordial ocean winds around it; and between Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht a great mountain rose up, making it impossible for anyone to travel from one region to another. 5. Of these seven regions, every good thing was created in greatest abundance in Khvaniras; yet the evil spirit also directed his greatest efforts against Khvaniras, because of the preeminence (sarih) he perceived in it. 6. For the Kayanian [legendary Iranian] kings and heroes were created in Khvaniras; the good religion of the Mazdayasnians [Zoroastrians] was established in Khvaniras and thereafter transmitted to the other regions. Soshyans [the eschatological savior] will be born in Khvaniras—he who will render the evil spirit powerless and bring about the resurrection and the future existence [i.e., the final renovation of the world].

entities_mentioned.name: Tishtar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The star Sirius (α Canis Majoris); associated in Zoroastrian cosmology with rain and the cosmic waters
entities_mentioned.notes: Tishtar is the Avestan/Pahlavi name for the star Sirius, venerated as the divine being responsible for bringing rain and defeating drought.

entities_mentioned.name: Khvaniras (Xvanīras)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The central of the seven mythological world-regions (keshvars); conceptually equated with the inhabited world known to Iranians, roughly corresponding to the Iranian plateau and surrounding lands
entities_mentioned.notes: In Zoroastrian cosmography, Khvaniras (Avestan: Xvaniraθa) is the central and most important of the seven keshvars, regarded as the seat of civilization, religion, and eschatological significance.

entities_mentioned.name: Savah
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of the six peripheral keshvars; no direct geographic correspondence; eastern region in the mythological schema

entities_mentioned.name: Arzah
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of the six peripheral keshvars; western region in the mythological schema

entities_mentioned.name: Fradadafsh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of the two southern keshvars; no direct geographic correspondence

entities_mentioned.name: Vidadafsh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of the two southern keshvars; no direct geographic correspondence

entities_mentioned.name: Vorubarsht
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of the two northern keshvars; no direct geographic correspondence

entities_mentioned.name: Vorujarsht
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of the two northern keshvars; no direct geographic correspondence; also spelled Vorujarsst in some manuscripts
entities_mentioned.notes: The source text inconsistently spells this name as both 'Vorujarsst' and 'Vorujarsht'; the latter is the more standard scholarly rendering.

entities_mentioned.name: Kayanians
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The legendary dynasty of heroic kings in Iranian epic and Zoroastrian tradition (Avestan: Kauuaiia); loosely associated by some scholars with historical eastern Iranian rulers
entities_mentioned.notes: The Kayanians feature prominently in the Shahnameh and Zoroastrian texts as the semi-divine royal lineage of ancient Iran.

entities_mentioned.name: Mazdayasnians
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrians; followers of Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.notes: Mazdayasna ('worship of Mazda') is the self-designation used in Zoroastrian scripture and tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Soshyans (Saošyant)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The eschatological savior figure in Zoroastrianism; the final Saoshyant who will raise the dead and defeat Ahriman at the end of time
entities_mentioned.notes: Soshyans (Avestan: Saošyant) is prophesied to be born of a virgin from the seed of Zarathustra preserved in a lake, and will lead the final renovation (Frashokereti) of the world.

entities_mentioned.name: Evil Spirit (Ahriman)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angra Mainyu / Ahriman, the supreme destructive deity in Zoroastrian dualistic theology
entities_mentioned.notes: Not named explicitly in this passage but referred to as 'the evil spirit'; standard Pahlavi term is Ahriman.

results.translator_notes: This segment is not a medical or materia medica text; it is a cosmographical passage from the Bundahishn (Pahlavi: 'Primal Creation'), a Zoroastrian encyclopedic text compiled in the 9th–12th centuries CE from older Avestan and Pahlavi sources. It describes the mythological division of the earth into seven keshvars (world-zones) and the eschatological primacy of Khvaniras. No plants, drugs, or medical practices are described. The translation here reproduces the existing scholarly English rendering (consistent with West's 1880 Sacred Books of the East translation) with bracketed clarifications added. The spelling 'Vorujarsst' in the source appears to be an OCR or manuscript variant of 'Vorujarsht' and has been noted. The chapter designation 'chapter_45' may vary across manuscript traditions and modern editions of the Bundahishn.

results.title_inferred: On the Nature and Origin of Mountains (Bundahishn, Chapter 46)
results.original_text: 1. On the nature of mountains it says in revelation, that, at first, the mountains have grown forth in eighteen years; and Alburz ever grew till the completion of eight hundred years; two hundred years up to the star station (payak), two hundred years to the moon station, two hundred years to the sun station, and two hundred years to the endless light. 2. While the other mountains have grown out of Alburz, in number 2244 mountains, and are Hugar the lofty, Terak of Alburz, Chakad-i Daitik, and the Arezur ridge, the Ausindom mountain, Mount Aparsen which they say is the mountain of Pars, Mount Zarid also which is Mount Manush, Mount Airach, Mount Kaf, Mount Vadges, Mount Aushdashtar, Mount Arezur-bum, Mount Royishn-homand, Mount Padashkhvargar which is the greatest in Khvarih, the mountain which they call Chino, Mount Revand, Mount Darspet the Bakyir mountain, Mount Kabed-shikaft, Mount Siyak-muimand, Mount Vafar-homand, Mount Spendyad and Kondrasp, Mount Asnavand and Kondras, Mount Sichidav, a mountain among those which are in Kangdez, of which they say that they are a comfort and delight of the good creator, the smaller hills. 3. I will mention them also a second time; Alburz is around this earth and is connected with the sky. 4. The Terak of Alburz is that through which the stars, moon, and sun pass in, and through it they come back. 5. Hugar the lofty is that from which the water of Aredvivsur leaps down the height of a thousand men. 6. The Ausindom mountain is that which, being of ruby (khun-ahino), of the substance of the sky, is in the midst of the wide-formed ocean, so that its water, which is from Hugar, pours down into it (the ocean). 7. Chakad-i-Daitik ('the judicial peak') is that of the middle of the world, the height of a hundred men, on which the Cinwad bridge stands; and they take account of the soul at that place. 8. The Arezur ridge [of the Alburz mountain] is a summit at the gate of hell, where they always hold the concourse of the demons. 9. This also is said, that, excepting Alburz, the Aparsen mountain is the greatest; the Aparsen mountain they call the mountain of Pars, and its beginning is in Sagastan and its end in Khujistan. 10. Mount Manush is great; the mountain on which Manushchihar was born. 11. The remaining mountains have chiefly grown from those; as it is said that the elevation (afsarih) of the districts had arisen most around those three mountains. 12. Mount Airach is in the middle from Hamadan to Khvarizem, and has grown from Mount Aparsen. 13. Mount Chino, which is on its east, on the frontier of Turkistan, is connected also with Aparsen. 14. Mount Kaf has grown from the same Mount Aparsen. 15. Mount Aushdashtar is in Sagastan. 16. Mount Arezur is that which is in the direction of Arum. 17. The Padashkhvargar mountain is that which is in Taparistan and the side of Gilan. 18. The Revand mountain is in Khurasan, on which the Burzin fire was established; and its name Revand means this, that it is glorious.
results.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of mountains, it is said in revelation [i.e., in sacred Zoroastrian scripture] that, at the beginning, the mountains grew forth over eighteen years; and Alburz continued to grow until the completion of eight hundred years — two hundred years up to the station of the stars [payak], two hundred years to the station of the moon, two hundred years to the station of the sun, and two hundred years to the endless light [i.e., the highest heaven]. 2. From Alburz all other mountains have grown — 2,244 mountains in number — among them: Hugar the Lofty, the Terak of Alburz, Chakad-i Daitik [the Judicial Peak], the Arezur ridge, the Ausindom mountain, Mount Aparsen (which they call the mountain of Pars), Mount Zarid (which is also Mount Manush), Mount Airach, Mount Kaf, Mount Vadges, Mount Aushdashtar, Mount Arezur-bum, Mount Royishn-homand, Mount Padashkhvargar (the greatest in Khvarih), the mountain called Chino, Mount Revand, Mount Darspet (the Bakyir mountain), Mount Kabed-shikaft, Mount Siyak-muimand, Mount Vafar-homand, Mount Spendyad and Kondrasp, Mount Asnavand and Kondras, Mount Sichidav, and a mountain among those in Kangdez — of which they say these are a comfort and delight of the Good Creator — and the smaller hills. 3. I shall mention them also a second time: Alburz encircles this earth and is connected with the sky. 4. The Terak of Alburz is that through which the stars, the moon, and the sun pass inward, and through it they return. 5. Hugar the Lofty is that from which the water of Aredvivsur [the mythical cosmic river, Avestan: Ardvi Sura Anahita] leaps down from a height equal to a thousand men. 6. The Ausindom mountain is made of ruby [khun-ahino], being of the substance of the sky, and stands in the midst of the wide-formed ocean, so that the water coming from Hugar pours down into the ocean. 7. Chakad-i Daitik ('the Judicial Peak') stands at the middle of the world, at a height of a hundred men, and upon it stands the Cinwad bridge [the bridge of judgment]; it is there that the account of the soul is reckoned. 8. The Arezur ridge [of the Alburz mountain] is a summit at the gate of hell, where the concourse of demons is always held. 9. It is also said that, apart from Alburz, the Aparsen mountain is the greatest; the Aparsen mountain they call the mountain of Pars, and its beginning is in Sagastan and its end in Khujistan [Khuzistan]. 10. Mount Manush is great; it is the mountain upon which Manushchihar was born. 11. The remaining mountains have chiefly grown from those; for it is said that the high elevations [afsarih] of the districts arose mostly around those three mountains. 12. Mount Airach lies in the middle [of the territory] from Hamadan to Khvarizem [Khwarezm], and has grown from Mount Aparsen. 13. Mount Chino, which lies to its east on the frontier of Turkistan, is also connected with Aparsen. 14. Mount Kaf has grown from that same Mount Aparsen. 15. Mount Aushdashtar is in Sagastan. 16. Mount Arezur is that which lies in the direction of Arum [i.e., Rome/Byzantium]. 17. The Padashkhvargar mountain is that which lies in Taparistan [Tabaristan] and on the side of Gilan. 18. The Revand mountain is in Khurasan, upon which the Burzin fire [one of the three sacred Zoroastrian fires] was established; and its name 'Revand' signifies that it is glorious.

entities_mentioned.name: Alburz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Alborz (Elburz) mountain range of northern Iran; in Zoroastrian cosmology also a mythic world-encircling mountain
entities_mentioned.notes: Central cosmic mountain in Zoroastrian tradition, said to grow for 800 years and connect earth to the sky.

entities_mentioned.name: Hugar the Lofty
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A mythic peak within the Alburz system; possibly associated with a peak in the Alborz range, but primarily cosmological
entities_mentioned.notes: Source of the cosmic waters of Aredvivsur (Anahita); height expressed as 'a thousand men.'

entities_mentioned.name: Terak of Alburz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythic pass or peak in the Alburz through which celestial bodies are said to transit; no firm geographic identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Cosmological function: gateway for the sun, moon, and stars.

entities_mentioned.name: Chakad-i Daitik
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythic 'Judicial Peak'; no geographic identification; purely cosmological/eschatological
entities_mentioned.notes: Location of the Cinwad (Chinvat) bridge of judgment in Zoroastrian eschatology.

entities_mentioned.name: Arezur ridge
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythic ridge at the gate of hell; possibly associated with the Caucasus region in some interpretations
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the site of demonic assembly; cosmological rather than strictly geographic.

entities_mentioned.name: Ausindom mountain
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythic mountain of ruby in the cosmic ocean; no geographic identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as made of ruby (khun-ahino) and of the substance of the sky; receives cosmic waters from Hugar.

entities_mentioned.name: Aparsen (mountain of Pars)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Zagros mountain range, extending from Sistan (Sagastan) to Khuzistan
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as second only to Alburz in greatness; identified as stretching from Sagastan to Khujistan (Khuzistan).

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Manush (Zarid)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a peak in the Zagros or central Iranian plateau
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with the legendary king Manushchihar's birthplace.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Airach
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly the Zagros or a range between Hamadan (Ecbatana) and Khwarezm
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to lie between Hamadan and Khvarizem (Khwarezm) and to have grown from Aparsen.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Kaf
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The legendary Mount Qaf of Islamic/Persian tradition; possibly the Caucasus or a purely mythic peak
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have grown from Aparsen.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Aushdashtar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Located in Sagastan (Sistan, southeastern Iran); specific peak unknown

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Padashkhvargar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Alborz range along the southern Caspian coast; associated with Tabaristan (Mazandaran) and Gilan
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the greatest in Khvarih; located in Taparistan (Tabaristan) and Gilan.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Chino
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A mountain on the eastern frontier of Turkistan; possibly in Central Asia near the Chinese frontier
entities_mentioned.notes: Connected with Aparsen; on the frontier of Turkistan.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Revand
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kuh-e Rivand (Reyvand), a peak near Nishapur in Khurasan, northeastern Iran
entities_mentioned.notes: Site of the Burzin (Burzin-Mihr) sacred fire, one of the three great Zoroastrian fires; name means 'glorious.'

entities_mentioned.name: Aredvivsur (Ardvi Sura Anahita)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Oxus/Amu Darya river in some interpretations; primarily a cosmological and divine river in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Cosmic river whose waters leap from Hugar the Lofty; associated with the yazata Anahita.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinwad bridge
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Chinvat Bridge of Zoroastrian eschatology; no geographic location
entities_mentioned.notes: Judgment bridge upon which souls are assessed after death; stands on Chakad-i Daitik.

entities_mentioned.name: Burzin fire
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Burzin-Mihr sacred fire, one of the three great fires of Zoroastrianism, traditionally located on Mount Revand in Khurasan
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three holiest fires in the Zoroastrian tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Manushchihar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Legendary Iranian king (Avestan: Manūščiθra); no historical identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Mythic figure associated with Mount Manush.

entities_mentioned.name: Kangdez
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythic paradise-city in Zoroastrian tradition; sometimes associated with Sogdia or Central Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a place of comfort and delight for the Good Creator.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Bundahishn (Middle Persian: 'Original Creation'), a Zoroastrian encyclopaedic text compiled in its extant form ca. 8th–12th century CE from older Avestan and Pahlavi materials. The source text appears to be the English translation by E.W. West (Sacred Books of the East, vol. 5, 1880), which itself was translated from the Middle Persian. As such, this JSON represents a translation of an existing English scholarly translation rather than a fresh translation from the Middle Persian original. The segment is cosmological and mythographical rather than medical or botanical, and thus yields no plant, drug, or medical practice entries. The schema field for 'entities_mentioned' has been used to catalog geographic, cosmological, and legendary entities instead. OCR errors corrected: 'oceanj' → 'ocean' (§6). The trailing '1' at the end of the source text appears to be an OCR artifact of a page or footnote number and has been omitted.

results.title_inferred: On the Five Classes of Animals and the Plants Arising from the Primeval Ox (Bundahishn, Chapter 48)
results.original_text: 1. On the nature of the five classes of animals (gospend) it says in revelation, that, when the primeval ox passed away, there where the marrow came out grain grew up of fifty and five species, and twelve species of medicinal plants grew; as it says, that out of the marrow is every separate creature, every single thing whose lodgment is in the marrow. 2. From the horns arose peas (mijuk), from the nose the leek, from the blood the grapevine from which they make wine — on this account wine abounds with blood — from the lungs the rue-like herbs, from the middle of the heart thyme for keeping away stench, and every one of the others as revealed in the Avesta. 3. The seed of the ox was carried up to the moon station; there it was thoroughly purified, and produced the manifold species of animals. 4. First, two oxen, one male and one female, and, afterwards, one pair of every single species was let go into the earth, and was discernible in Eranvej for a Hasar ('mile'), which is like a Parasang ('league'); as it says, that, on account of the value of the ox, it was created twice, one time as an ox, and one time as the manifold species of animals. 5. A thousand days and nights they were without eating, and first water and afterwards herbage (aurvar) were devoured by them. 6. And, afterwards, the three classes (kardak) of animals were produced therefrom, as it says that first were the goat and sheep, and then the camel and swine, and then the horse and ass. 7. For, first, those suitable for grazing were created therefrom, those are now kept in the valley (lai); the second created were those of the hill summits (sar-i dez), which are wide-travelers, and habits (nihadak) are not taught to them by hand; the third created were those dwelling in the water. 8. As for the genera (khadunak), the first genus is that which has the foot cloven in two, and is suitable for grazing; of which a camel larger than a horse is small and new-born. 9. The second genus is ass-footed, of which the swift horse is the largest, and the ass the least. 10. The third genus is that of the five-dividing paw, of which the dog is the largest, and the civet-cat the least. 11. The fourth genus is the flying, of which the griffin of three natures is the largest, and the chaffinch the least. 12. The fifth genus is that of the water, of which the Kar fish is the largest, and the Nemadu the least. 13. These five genera are apportioned out into two hundred and eighty-two species (sardak). 14. First are five species of goat, the ass-goat, the milch-goat, the mountain-goat, the fawn, and the common goat. 15. Second, five species of sheep, that with a tail, that which has no tail, the dog-sheep, the wether, and the Kurishk sheep, a sheep whose horn is great; it possesses a grandeur like unto a horse, and they use it mostly for a steed (bara), as it is said that Manuschihar kept a Kurishk as a steed. 16. Third, two species of camel, the mountain one and that suitable for grazing; for one is fit [text ends]
results.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of the five classes of animals [gospend], it is stated in revelation that when the primeval ox died, wherever its marrow came forth, fifty-five species of grain grew up, and twelve species of medicinal plants grew; for it is said that out of the marrow comes every distinct creature, every single thing whose origin lies in the marrow. 2. From the horns arose peas [mijuk], from the nose the leek, from the blood the grapevine from which wine is made — for this reason wine is rich in blood — from the lungs grew rue-like herbs, from the center of the heart grew thyme for warding off foul odor, and all the rest as revealed in the Avesta. 3. The seed of the ox was borne up to the station of the moon; there it was thoroughly purified and gave rise to the manifold species of animals. 4. First, two oxen, one male and one female, and thereafter one pair of every single species was released upon the earth, and could be perceived in Eranvej [the primordial land] within a Hasar [a 'mile'], which is comparable to a Parasang [a 'league']; for it is said that, on account of the worth of the ox, it was created twice: once as an ox, and once as the manifold species of animals. 5. For a thousand days and nights they went without eating, consuming first water and then herbage [aurvar]. 6. Thereafter, the three classes [kardak] of animals were produced from them: first the goat and the sheep, then the camel and the swine, and then the horse and the ass. 7. For first were created those suitable for grazing, which are now kept in the valleys [lai]; second were created those of the hill summits [sar-i dez], which are wide-ranging travelers and whose habits [nihadak] cannot be taught by the hand of man; third were created those dwelling in the water. 8. As for the genera [khadunak]: the first genus is that which has a foot cloven in two and is suitable for grazing, of which even a camel that is larger than a horse is considered small and newborn. 9. The second genus is the ass-footed kind, of which the swift horse is the largest member and the ass the smallest. 10. The third genus is that of the five-divided paw, of which the dog is the largest and the civet-cat the smallest. 11. The fourth genus is the flying kind, of which the griffin of three natures is the largest and the chaffinch the smallest. 12. The fifth genus is that of the water, of which the Kar fish is the largest and the Nemadu the smallest. 13. These five genera are divided into two hundred and eighty-two species [sardak]. 14. First are five species of goat: the ass-goat, the milch-goat, the mountain-goat, the fawn, and the common goat. 15. Second are five species of sheep: that with a tail, that which has no tail, the dog-sheep, the wether, and the Kurishk sheep, a sheep whose horn is great; it possesses a stature comparable to a horse, and is used chiefly as a riding animal [bara], as it is said that Manuschihar kept a Kurishk as a mount. 16. Third are two species of camel: the mountain camel and that suitable for grazing; for one is fit [text ends]

entities_mentioned.name: Primeval Ox (Gavaevodata)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological/cosmogonic figure; no direct biological equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Central figure of Zoroastrian cosmogony; its death is the origin of plants and animals in Bundahishn mythology.

entities_mentioned.name: Peas (mijuk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pisum sativum (common pea)
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have arisen from the horns of the primeval ox. Middle Persian 'mijuk' is generally identified with peas or legumes.

entities_mentioned.name: Leek
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium porrum or related Allium species
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have arisen from the nose of the primeval ox.

entities_mentioned.name: Grapevine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitis vinifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have arisen from the blood of the primeval ox; the text offers an etiological explanation for the red color of wine.

entities_mentioned.name: Rue-like herbs
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ruta graveolens or related Rutaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have arisen from the lungs of the primeval ox. The identification as 'rue-like' is interpretive; the original plant(s) are unspecified beyond family resemblance.

entities_mentioned.name: Thyme (for keeping away stench)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thymus vulgaris or related Thymus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have arisen from the heart of the primeval ox; ascribed the property of repelling foul odor. Used medicinally as an antiseptic/deodorant herb in traditional contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Aurvar (herbage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generic term for grass or pasture vegetation
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian term for herbage or vegetation consumed by animals.

entities_mentioned.name: Kar fish
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological giant fish of Zoroastrian cosmology; no modern species equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the largest of the water genus.

entities_mentioned.name: Nemadu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a small fish or water creature of Zoroastrian tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the smallest of the water genus.

entities_mentioned.name: Griffin of three natures
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological creature (Simurgh or related); no modern equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the largest of the flying genus. The 'three natures' epithet may refer to composite animal characteristics typical of Persian mythological birds such as the Simurgh.

entities_mentioned.name: Chaffinch
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fringilla coelebs or a related small finch species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the smallest of the flying genus.
