entities_mentioned.name: Civet-cat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Viverra zibetha (large Indian civet) or related viverrid
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the smallest of the five-clawed paw genus. Identification as civet-cat is the traditional scholarly rendering; the original term may refer to a different small carnivore.

entities_mentioned.name: Kurishk sheep
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a large-horned breed of sheep used as a riding or draft animal
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as horse-sized with a large horn, used as a riding animal [bara] by the legendary king Manuschihar. No confident modern breed identification; may be legendary or exaggerated.

entities_mentioned.name: Eranvej
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological primordial homeland of Zoroastrian tradition; no geographic modern equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: The place where the first animals were released; a paradisal region in Zoroastrian cosmogony.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from the Bundahishn ('Original Creation'), a Middle Persian Zoroastrian encyclopedic text compiled ca. 8th–9th century CE from older Avestan and Pahlavi sources. The source text used here appears to be the West translation (19th century) of the Indian Bundahishn. The passage is cosmogonic rather than strictly medical, but it contains explicit ethnobotanical content: the origin of 55 grain species and 12 medicinal plant species from the body of the primeval ox, with specific plant-to-body-part correspondences. These correspondences function as etiological myths explaining the properties of individual plants (e.g., thyme from the heart → deodorizing property; grapevine from the blood → red wine). The 12 medicinal plant species are mentioned collectively but only a subset is individually named in this passage. The taxonomic system (five genera, 282 species) reflects a sophisticated Zoroastrian natural-philosophical tradition. Transliterated Middle Persian terms [gospend, mijuk, aurvar, kardak, khadunak, sardak, lai, sar-i dez, nihadak, bara] have been preserved in brackets. The text ends mid-sentence (section 16), indicating the segment is incomplete.

results.title_inferred: The Origin of Humanity: Gayomard's Seed, Mashye and Mashyane, and the First Corruption
results.original_text: 1. On the nature of men it says in revelation, that Gayomard, in passing away, gave forth seed; that seed was thoroughly purified by the motion of the light of the sun, and Neryosang kept charge of two portions, and Spandarmad received one portion. 2. And in forty years, with the shape of a one-stemmed Rivas-plant, and the fifteen years of its fifteen leaves, Matro [Mashye] and Matroyao [Mashyane] grew up from the earth in such a manner that their arms rested, behind on their shoulders (dosh), and one joined to the other they were connected together and both alike. 3. And the waists of both of them were brought close and so connected together that it was not clear which is the male and which the female, and which is the one whose living soul (nismo) of Ohrmazd is not away. 4. As it is said thus: Which is created before, the soul (nismo) or the body? And Ohrmazd said that the soul is created before, and the body after, for him who was created; it is given into the body that it may produce activity, and the body is created only for activity; hence the conclusion is this, that the soul (ruban) is created before and the body after. 5. And both of them changed from the shape of a plant into the shape of man, and the breath (nismo) went spiritually into them, which is the soul (ruban); and now, moreover, in that similitude a tree had grown up whose fruit was the ten varieties of man. 6. Ohrmazd spoke to Mashye and Mashyane thus: 'You are man, you are the ancestry of the world, and you are created perfect in devotion by me; perform devotedly the duty of the law, think good thoughts, speak good words, do good deeds, and worship no demons!' 7. Both of them first thought this, that one of them should please the other, as he is a man for him; and the first deed done by them was this, when they went out they washed themselves thoroughly; and the first words spoken by them were these, that Ohrmazd created the water and earth, plants and animals, the stars, moon, and sun, and all prosperity whose origin and effect are from the manifestation of righteousness. 8. And, afterwards, antagonism rushed into their minds, and their minds were thoroughly corrupted, and they exclaimed that the evil spirit created the water and earth, plants and animals, and the other things as aforesaid. 9. That false speech was spoken through the will of the demons, and the evil spirit possessed himself of this first enjoyment from them; through that false speech they both became wicked, and their souls are in hell until the future existence. 10. And they had gone thirty days without food, covered with clothing of herbage (giyah); and after the thirty days they went forth into the wilderness, came to a white-haired goat, and milked the milk from the udder with their mouths. 11. When they had devoured the milk Mashye said to Mashyane thus: 'My delight was owing to it when I had not devoured the milk, and my delight is more delightful now when it is devoured by my vile body.' 12. That [text ends]
results.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of men, it is stated in revelation that Gayomard, at the moment of his death, emitted his seed; that seed was thoroughly purified by the movement of the light of the sun, and Neryosang kept charge of two portions of it, while Spandarmad received one portion. 2. After forty years, in the form of a single-stemmed Rivas-plant [rhubarb], with fifteen leaves corresponding to fifteen years of growth, Mashye and Mashyane [the first human pair, here called Matro and Matroyao] grew up from the earth in such a manner that their arms rested behind on their shoulders [dosh], and joined one to the other they were conjoined and alike in every respect. 3. The waists of both were drawn together and so conjoined that it was not discernible which was male and which female, nor which was the one from whom the living soul [nismo] of Ohrmazd had not yet departed. 4. As it is stated: Which was created first, the soul [nismo] or the body? And Ohrmazd replied that the soul was created first and the body afterward, for the one who was created; the soul is given into the body so that the body may produce activity, and the body is created for the sole purpose of activity. Hence the conclusion is that the soul [ruban] is created before the body. 5. And both of them changed from the form of a plant into the form of human beings, and the breath [nismo] entered them spiritually, which is the soul [ruban]; and furthermore, in that similitude a tree had grown whose fruit comprised the ten varieties of humankind. 6. Ohrmazd spoke to Mashye and Mashyane thus: 'You are human; you are the progenitors of the world, and you have been created by me in perfect devotion. Perform the duty of the law devotedly, think good thoughts, speak good words, do good deeds, and worship no demons!' 7. Their first thought was that each should please the other, as one is a companion to the other; and the first act they performed was to wash themselves thoroughly when they went out; and the first words they spoke were these: that Ohrmazd created water and earth, plants and animals, the stars, the moon, and the sun, and all prosperity whose origin and effect derive from the manifestation of righteousness. 8. Afterward, however, antagonism rushed into their minds, their minds were thoroughly corrupted, and they cried out that the evil spirit had created water and earth, plants and animals, and the other things aforementioned. 9. That false speech was uttered through the will of the demons, and the evil spirit thereby seized upon this first act of enjoyment from them; through that false speech they both became wicked, and their souls remain in hell until the future existence [the final renovation]. 10. For thirty days they went without food, clothed in garments of herbage [giyah]; and after those thirty days they went out into the wilderness, encountered a white-haired goat, and drew milk from its udder with their mouths. 11. When they had consumed the milk, Mashye said to Mashyane: 'My delight was greater before I had consumed the milk than it is now that it has been consumed by my vile body.' 12. That [text ends mid-sentence]

entities_mentioned.name: Gayomard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primordial man of Zoroastrian cosmology; Avestan Gaya Marətan
entities_mentioned.notes: The first mortal in Zoroastrian tradition; his death and the purification of his seed leads to the origin of humanity.

entities_mentioned.name: Neryosang
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan yazata (divine being) associated with fire and the sun; messenger deity
entities_mentioned.notes: Acts as guardian of two portions of Gayomard's seed.

entities_mentioned.name: Spandarmad
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spenta Armaiti; Zoroastrian divinity of earth and devotion
entities_mentioned.notes: Receives one portion of Gayomard's seed; associated with the earth in which the first human pair gestates.

entities_mentioned.name: Matro / Mashye
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: First man of Zoroastrian tradition; equivalent to Adam in Abrahamic traditions
entities_mentioned.notes: Name appears in two forms in the source: 'Matro' appears to be a variant or scribal rendering of 'Mashye'; the translator has bracketed the standard form.

entities_mentioned.name: Matroyao / Mashyane
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: First woman of Zoroastrian tradition; counterpart to Mashye
entities_mentioned.notes: Similarly, 'Matroyao' is a variant rendering of 'Mashyane'.

entities_mentioned.name: Rivas-plant
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum ribes or Rheum rhabarbarum (rhubarb); Persian 'rīvās'
entities_mentioned.notes: The first human pair grew in the form of a single-stemmed rivas plant with fifteen leaves. Rivas in Persian literature commonly refers to a species of Rheum (rhubarb) or Rheum ribes. Relevant to Materia Medica as a medicinally significant plant in Persian/Zoroastrian tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahura Mazda; supreme deity of Zoroastrianism

entities_mentioned.name: giyah (herbage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generic term for grass/plant material used as clothing
entities_mentioned.notes: The word 'giyah' (Persian/Middle Persian for 'plant' or 'herbage') is preserved in the original; Mashye and Mashyane used it as clothing for thirty days.

entities_mentioned.name: white-haired goat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Domestic goat (Capra hircus)
entities_mentioned.notes: The first animal food consumed by the first humans; milk taken directly from the udder by mouth.

entities_mentioned.name: nismo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for breath/living soul
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from 'ruban' (soul proper); nismo relates to the vital breath or animating principle.

entities_mentioned.name: ruban
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for soul/spirit
entities_mentioned.notes: The immortal soul as distinct from the vital breath (nismo).

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Bundahishn (also Bundahišn), a Middle Persian Zoroastrian cosmological text. The translation appears to be that of E.W. West (Sacred Books of the East, vol. 5, 1880), with the bracketed identifications of 'Mashye' and 'Mashyane' inserted editorially. The segment is of primary interest for Materia Medica Americana only insofar as it references the Rivas-plant (rhubarb/Rheum sp.), a plant of considerable medical importance in the ancient Near East and which entered European pharmacopoeias via Persian and Arabic medical traditions. The text also references milk from a goat as the first food of humanity, and herbage (giyah) as clothing — neither of which constitutes a medical preparation, but both reflect the ethnobotanical context of the source tradition. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence at section 12, indicating the source text was truncated. The cosmological and theological content is not directly relevant to materia medica but provides cultural context for plant and animal symbolism in Zoroastrian-influenced medical traditions.

results.title_inferred: On the Nature of Generation, Seed, and Biological Classification of Male and Female
results.original_text: 1. On the nature of generation it says in revelation, that a woman when she comes out from menstruation, during ten days and nights, when they go near unto her, soon becomes pregnant. 2. When she is cleansed from her menstruation, and when the time for pregnancy has come, always when the seed of the man is the more powerful a son arises from it; when that of the woman is the more powerful, a daughter; when both seeds are equal, twins and triplets. 3. If the male seed comes the sooner, it adds to the female, and she becomes robust; if the female seed comes the sooner, it becomes blood, and the leanness of the female arises therefrom. 4. The female seed is cold and moist, and its flow is from the loins, and the color is white, red, and yellow; and the male seed is hot and dry, its flow is from the brain of the head, and the color is white and mud-colored (hashgun). 5. All the seed of the females which issues beforehand, takes a place within the womb, and the seed of the males will remain above it, and will fill the space of the womb; whatever refrains therefrom becomes blood again, enters into the veins of the females, and at the time any one is born it becomes milk and nourishes him, as all milk arises from the seed of the males, and the blood is that of the females. 6. These four things, they say, are male, and these female: the sky, metal, wind, and fire are male, and are never otherwise; the water, earth, plants, and fish are female, and are never otherwise; the remaining creation consists of male and female. 7. As regards the fish it says that, at the time of excitement, they go forwards and come back in the water, two and two, the length of a mile (hasar), which is one-fourth of a league (parasang), in the running water; in that coming and going they then rub their bodies together, and a kind of sweat drops out betwixt them, and both become pregnant.
results.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of generation, it is stated in revelation [i.e., the sacred Zoroastrian scriptures] that a woman, upon emerging from her menstruation, may become pregnant if approached during the following ten days and nights. 2. When she is purified from her menstruation and the time for conception has arrived: whenever the seed [semen] of the man is the more potent, a son results; when that of the woman is the more potent, a daughter; when both seeds are equal in power, twins or triplets are produced. 3. If the male seed arrives first, it augments the female seed and the woman becomes robust [in body]; if the female seed arrives first, it turns to blood, and the thinness [leanness] of the female body results from this. 4. The female seed is cold and moist in nature; it flows from the loins, and its color is white, red, and yellow. The male seed is hot and dry in nature; it flows from the brain of the head, and its color is white and mud-colored [hashgun]. 5. All of the female seed that is emitted first settles within the womb, and the male seed remains above it and fills the cavity of the womb; whatever of the male seed is not used in this way reverts to blood, enters the veins of the female, and at the time of birth becomes milk and nourishes the infant — for all milk arises from the seed of males, and blood is that of the females. 6. These four things are said to be inherently male, and these inherently female: the sky, metal, wind, and fire are male, and are never otherwise; water, earth, plants, and fish are female, and are never otherwise; the remainder of creation consists of both male and female. 7. Concerning fish, it is said that at the time of their sexual excitement they swim forward and back in running water, two by two, over the length of a hasar [mile], which is one-fourth of a parasang [league]; in this to-and-fro swimming they rub their bodies together, and a kind of sweat exudes between them, and both thereby become pregnant.

entities_mentioned.name: female seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Conceptualized female reproductive secretion; roughly analogous in Galenic humoral terms to female 'seed' or menstrual material
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as cold, moist, flowing from the loins, and white/red/yellow in color. This reflects pre-modern humoralist and Zoroastrian cosmological physiology, paralleling Galenic two-seed theory.

entities_mentioned.name: male seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Semen
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as hot, dry, flowing from the brain, and white/mud-colored (hashgun). The idea that semen originates in the brain is a widespread ancient medical belief found in Hippocratic, Galenic, and Zoroastrian traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: hasar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Persian unit of distance, approximately one mile
entities_mentioned.notes: Glossed in text as one-fourth of a parasang.

entities_mentioned.name: parasang
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Persian unit of distance (farsakh), roughly 3.5–6 km depending on era and region
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Persian itinerary measure; here used to calibrate the hasar.

entities_mentioned.name: fish
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fish (Pisces, general)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified cosmologically as female. The description of fish reproduction via mutual bodily rubbing and 'sweat' reflects ancient misunderstanding of external fertilization.

entities_mentioned.name: hashgun
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian term given as the color descriptor for male seed, rendered as 'mud-colored' by the translator. Exact hue is uncertain; possibly grayish-brown.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Bundahishn (Middle Persian: 'Primal Creation'), a Zoroastrian cosmological and encyclopedic text compiled in its extant form around the 9th–12th centuries CE, though drawing on much older Avestan and Pahlavi traditions. The passage reflects a humoral physiological theory of reproduction closely analogous to the Galenic two-seed doctrine and Hippocratic writings, suggesting either parallel development or cross-cultural transmission. The claim that male seed originates in the brain is a well-attested ancient belief (cf. Alcmaeon, Hippocrates). The cosmological gendering of natural elements (sky=male, earth=female, etc.) is a widespread Indo-Iranian mythological motif. No plant drugs or medical preparations are described in this segment; the content is theoretical/physiological rather than pharmacological. The translator (likely West, from the 19th-century Sacred Books of the East series) has preserved original Persian technical terms in parentheses where helpful.

results.title_inferred: On the Gokaren Tree, the Kar Fish, the Vas of Panchasadvaran, and the Tree of Many Seeds
results.original_text: 1. On the nature of the tree they call Gokaren it says in revelation, that it was the first day when the tree they call Gokaren grew in the deep mud within the wide-formed ocean; and it is necessary as a producer of the renovation of the universe, for they prepare its immortality therefrom. 2. The evil spirit has formed therein, among those which enter as opponents, a lizard as an opponent in that deep water, so that it may injure the Haoma. 3. And for keeping away that lizard, Ohrmazd has created there ten Kar fish which, at all times, continually circle around the Haoma, so that the head of one of those fish is continually towards the lizard. 4. And together with the lizard those fish are spiritually fed, that is, no food is necessary for them; and till the renovation of the universe they remain in contention. 5. There are places where that fish is written of as 'the Ariz of the water;' as it says that the greatest of the creatures of Ohrmazd is that fish, and the greatest of those proceeding from the evil spirit is that lizard; with the jaws of their bodies, moreover, they snap in two whatever of the creatures of both spirits has entered between them, except that one fish which is the Vas of Panchasadvaran. 6. This, too, is said, that those fish are so serpent-like in that deep water, they know the scratch (malishn) of a needle's point by which the water shall increase, or by which it is diminishing. 7. Regarding the Vas of Panchasadvaran it is declared that it moves within the wide-formed ocean, and its length is as much as what a man, while in a swift race, will walk from dawn till when the sun goes down; so much that it does not itself move the length of the whole of its great body. 8. This, too, is said, that the creatures of the waters live also specially under its guardianship. 9. The tree of many seeds has grown amid the wide-formed ocean, and in its seed are all plants; some say it is the proper-curing, some the energetic-curing, some the all-curing. 10. Between these trees of such kinds is formed the mountain with cavities, 9999 thousand myriads in number, each myriad being ten thousand. 11. Unto that mountain is given the protection of the waters, so that water streams forth from there, in the rivulet channels, to the land of the seven regions, as the source of all the sea-water in the land of the seven regions is from there.
results.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of the tree called Gokaren, it is stated in revelation that on the first day the tree called Gokaren grew in the deep mud within the wide-formed ocean; it is indispensable as the agent of the renovation of the universe, for immortality is prepared from it. 2. The evil spirit has placed among its opponents a lizard in that deep water, so that it may harm the Haoma. 3. To ward off that lizard, Ohrmazd created ten Kar fish there, which at all times continually circle around the Haoma, such that the head of one of those fish is always directed toward the lizard. 4. Both the lizard and those fish are sustained spiritually—that is, no physical food is necessary for them—and until the renovation of the universe they remain in contention. 5. In some passages that fish is referred to as 'the Ariz of the water.' It is stated that the greatest of Ohrmazd's creatures is that fish, and the greatest of those originating from the evil spirit is that lizard; with the jaws of their bodies they snap in two whatever creatures of either spirit come between them, with the exception of that one fish which is the Vas of Panchasadvaran. 6. It is also said that those fish are so serpent-like in that deep water that they can detect the disturbance [malishn, lit. 'scratch'] of a needle's point, by which they know whether the water is rising or diminishing. 7. Concerning the Vas of Panchasadvaran, it is declared that it moves within the wide-formed ocean, and its length is equal to the distance a man running swiftly would cover from dawn until sunset; indeed, it does not itself travel a distance equal to the full length of its great body. 8. It is also said that the creatures of the waters live especially under its protection. 9. The tree of many seeds has grown in the midst of the wide-formed ocean, and within its seed are contained all plants; some call it the 'proper-curing' [tree], some the 'energetic-curing,' and some the 'all-curing.' 10. Between these various trees there is formed a mountain filled with cavities, 9,999 thousand myriads in number, each myriad being ten thousand. 11. The protection of the waters is entrusted to that mountain, so that water flows forth from it, through rivulet channels, to the land of the seven regions; for the source of all sea-water in the land of the seven regions originates there.

entities_mentioned.name: Gokaren (also Gaokerena)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Ephedra or a mythic white Haoma plant (Hom); sometimes associated with Ephedra species in Zoroastrian botany
entities_mentioned.notes: The Gokaren (Gaokerena) is a sacred cosmological tree in Zoroastrian scripture, closely linked to Haoma and to the eschatological renovation (Frashegird). It grows in the cosmic ocean Vourukaša. Its connection to immortality and the 'renovation of the universe' is a central Avestan/Pahlavi theme. Some scholars equate the white Haoma with Ephedra.

entities_mentioned.name: Haoma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra species (especially Ephedra sinica or E. intermedia) or possibly Peganum harmala; identification debated
entities_mentioned.notes: Haoma is the sacred ritual plant-deity of Zoroastrianism, cognate with Vedic Soma. Its botanical identity remains contested. The text presents Haoma as requiring protection from demonic forces.

entities_mentioned.name: Kar fish (ten Kar fish)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mythological fish created by Ohrmazd to guard the Haoma/Gokaren tree from the demonic lizard in the cosmic ocean. Not a real species; a cosmological construct in Zoroastrian mythology.

entities_mentioned.name: Lizard (opponent of Haoma)
entities_mentioned.notes: A creature of Ahriman (the evil spirit) placed in the cosmic ocean to injure the Haoma. Purely mythological.

entities_mentioned.name: Ariz of the water
entities_mentioned.notes: An alternative name for the Kar fish found in some textual variants. Meaning and etymology uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Vas of Panchasadvaran
entities_mentioned.notes: A colossal mythological fish of extraordinary size, guardian of aquatic creatures. 'Panchasadvaran' may relate to a Pahlavi term; its precise etymology is uncertain. Not identical with the ten Kar fish.

entities_mentioned.name: Tree of many seeds (all-curing / proper-curing / energetic-curing tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly the Avestan Vīspō.bīš ('all-healing') tree; conceptually a pan-botanical archetype
entities_mentioned.notes: This tree contains all plant seeds and is described with three epithets relating to healing. It corresponds to the Avestan concept of the healing tree Vīspō.bīš. The three alternative names—'proper-curing,' 'energetic-curing,' and 'all-curing'—may reflect different textual traditions or scribal glosses. Relevant to Materia Medica as a mythological source of all medicinal plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd (Ahura Mazda)
entities_mentioned.notes: The supreme deity of Zoroastrianism, creator of the beneficent cosmic creatures described in this passage.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text is the Bundahishn (Middle Persian: 'Original Creation'), a Zoroastrian cosmological compendium, here chapter 52 (chapter numbering may vary by edition). The text used appears to be E.W. West's 19th-century translation from Pahlavi, lightly revised. 2. The word 'malishn' (scratch/sensation) is retained from the original as a technical Pahlavi gloss. 3. 'Deer water' in sentence 6 of the source is an OCR error for 'deep water,' silently corrected. 4. The healing epithets of the Tree of Many Seeds ('proper-curing,' 'energetic-curing,' 'all-curing') are significant for Materia Medica purposes: this passage represents a mythological origin narrative for all medicinal plants, asserting that all curative herbs derive their seeds from this one cosmic tree in the primordial ocean. 5. The numeral '9999 thousand myriads' is preserved as in the source; it represents an astronomical cosmological quantity rather than a literal count.

results.title_inferred: The Three-Legged Ass, Ox Hadhayosh, and Bird Chamrosh: Mythical Creatures of the Bundahishn
results.original_text: 1. Regarding the three-legged ass they say, that it stands amid the wide-formed ocean, and its feet are three, eyes six, mouths nine, ears two, and horn one, body white, food spiritual, and it is righteous. 2. And two of its six eyes are in the position of eyes, two on the top of the head, and two in the position of the hump; with the sharpness of those six eyes it overcomes and destroys. 3. Of the nine mouths three are in the head, three in the hump, and three in the inner part of the flanks; and each mouth is about the size of a cottage, and it is itself as large as Mount Alvand. 4. Each one of the three feet, when it is placed on the ground, is as much as a flock (gird) of a thousand sheep comes under when they repose together; and each pastern is so great in its circuit that a thousand men with a thousand horses may pass inside. 5. As for the two ears it is Mazendaran which they will encompass. 6. The one horn is as it were of gold and hollow, and a thousand branch horns have grown upon it, some befitting a camel, some befitting a horse, some befitting an ox, some befitting an ass, both great and small. 7. With that horn it will vanquish and dissipate all the vile corruption due to the efforts of noxious creatures. 8. When that ass shall hold its neck in the ocean, its ears will terrify (asahmed), and all the water of the wide-formed ocean will shake with agitation, and the side of Ganavad will tremble (shivaned). 9. When it utters a cry all female water-creatures, of the creatures of Ohrmazd, will become pregnant; and all pregnant noxious water-creatures, when they hear that cry, will cast their young. 10. When it stales in the ocean all the sea-water will become purified, which is in the seven regions of the earth — it is even on that account when all asses which come into water stale in the water — as it says thus: 'If, O three-legged ass! you were not created for the water, all the water in the sea would have perished from the contamination which the poison of the evil spirit has brought into its water, through the death of the creatures of Ohrmazd.' 11. Tishtar seizes the water more completely from the ocean with the assistance of the three-legged ass. 12. Of ambergris also (ambar-ich) it is declared, that it is the dung of the three-legged ass; for if it has much spirit food, then also the moisture of the liquid nourishment goes through the veins pertaining to the body into the urine, and the dung is cast away. 13. Of the ox Hadhayosh, which they call Sarsaok, it says, that in the original creation men passed from region to region upon it, and in the renovation of the universe they prepare Hush (the beverage producing immortality) from it. 14. It is said, that life is in the hand of that foremost man, at the end of his years, who has constructed the most defenses around this earth, until the renovation of the universe is requisite. 15. Regarding the bird Chamrosh it says, that it is on the summit of Mount Alburz; and every three ye[ars...]
results.translation: 1. Regarding the three-legged ass, they say that it stands in the midst of the wide-formed ocean; its feet number three, its eyes six, its mouths nine, its ears two, and its horn one; its body is white, its sustenance spiritual, and it is a righteous creature. 2. Of its six eyes, two are in the normal position of eyes, two are on the top of the head, and two are in the position of the hump; by the keenness of those six eyes it overcomes and destroys [evil]. 3. Of the nine mouths, three are in the head, three in the hump, and three in the inner part of the flanks; each mouth is roughly the size of a cottage, while the creature itself is as large as Mount Alvand. 4. Each of the three feet, when set upon the ground, covers an area sufficient for a flock [gird] of a thousand sheep resting together; and each pastern is so vast in its circumference that a thousand men with a thousand horses could pass within it. 5. As for its two ears, they will encompass all of Mazendaran. 6. Its single horn is as if made of gold and is hollow, and upon it a thousand branch-horns have grown — some of a size befitting a camel, some a horse, some an ox, some an ass — both great and small. 7. With that horn it will vanquish and scatter all the foul corruption brought about by the strivings of noxious creatures. 8. When the ass plunges its neck into the ocean, its ears will strike terror [asahmed], and all the water of the wide-formed ocean will heave with agitation, and the region of Ganavad will tremble [shivaned]. 9. When it utters its cry, all female water-creatures among the creations of Ohrmazd will become pregnant; and all pregnant noxious water-creatures, upon hearing that cry, will miscarry their young. 10. When it urinates into the ocean, all the sea-water throughout the seven regions of the earth will be purified — it is on this very account that all asses which enter water urinate in the water — as the text says: 'If, O three-legged ass, you had not been created for the water, all the water in the sea would have perished from the contamination which the poison of the evil spirit introduced into it through the death of the creatures of Ohrmazd.' 11. The star Tishtar draws water more completely from the ocean with the aid of the three-legged ass. 12. Regarding ambergris [ambar-ich], it is declared that it is the dung of the three-legged ass; for since it consumes much spiritual food, the moisture of its liquid nourishment passes through the body's veins into the urine, and the solid dung is expelled. 13. Regarding the ox Hadhayosh, also called Sarsaok, it is said that in the original creation men traveled from region to region upon it, and at the renovation of the universe the drink Hush — the beverage that produces immortality — will be prepared from it. 14. It is said that life rests in the hands of that foremost man, at the end of his years, who has built the greatest defenses around this earth, pending the renovation of the universe. 15. Regarding the bird Chamrosh, it is said that it dwells upon the summit of Mount Alburz; and every three ye[ars... (text breaks off)]

entities_mentioned.name: Three-legged ass (Ass of Ohrmazd / Srisok)
entities_mentioned.notes: A cosmological creature from Zoroastrian mythology described in the Greater Bundahishn. Its urine purifies ocean water; its dung is identified as ambergris.

entities_mentioned.name: Ambergris (ambar-ich)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ambergris — waxy substance produced in the intestine of sperm whales (Physeter macrocephalus)
entities_mentioned.notes: The text offers a mythological etiology for ambergris, identifying it as the dung of the three-legged ass. The Middle Persian term 'ambar-ich' is cognate with Arabic 'anbar,' the standard term for ambergris in later Islamic materia medica. Ambergris was widely used in medieval medicine as a stimulant, aphrodisiac, and fragrance fixative.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Alvand
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mount Alvand (Alwand), a mountain near Hamadan, western Iran
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison for the three-legged ass.

entities_mentioned.name: Mazendaran
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mazandaran province, northern Iran (southern Caspian coast)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ears of the three-legged ass are said to encompass all of Mazandaran.

entities_mentioned.name: Ganavad
entities_mentioned.notes: A geographical or cosmological location mentioned in the Bundahishn; not securely identified with a modern place.

entities_mentioned.name: Tishtar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The star Sirius (Alpha Canis Majoris)
entities_mentioned.notes: In Zoroastrian cosmology, Tishtar is the yazata (divine being) associated with the star Sirius and with rainfall and water; it draws water from the cosmic ocean.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahura Mazda — supreme deity of Zoroastrianism

entities_mentioned.name: Ox Hadhayosh (Sarsaok)
entities_mentioned.notes: A mythical primordial ox from Zoroastrian eschatology. At the renovation of the universe (Frashokereti), the drink Hush (also called Haoma of immortality) is to be prepared from it.

entities_mentioned.name: Hush (beverage producing immortality)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eschatological Haoma / White Haoma
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with the Zoroastrian ritual drink Haoma; in its eschatological form it is said to confer immortality at the end of time.

entities_mentioned.name: Bird Chamrosh
entities_mentioned.notes: A mythical bird said to dwell atop Mount Alburz in Zoroastrian cosmology; the description is cut off in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Mount Alburz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Alborz mountain range, northern Iran; in Zoroastrian cosmology a cosmic mountain
entities_mentioned.notes: The mythological Mount Hara Berezaiti / Alburz is the cosmic mountain at the center of the world; not strictly identical to the physical Alborz range, though associated with it.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Greater Bundahishn (also known as the Iranian Bundahishn), a Middle Persian Zoroastrian encyclopedic text compiled probably in the 9th century CE though drawing on much older Avestan traditions. The translation follows the West (1880) rendering closely but corrects a minor OCR error ('if is itself' corrected to 'it is itself' in §3). The passage contains one text-critical note: the final sentence (§15) is truncated mid-word ('every three ye[ars...]'), reflecting either a page break or a manuscript lacuna in the source used. The identification of ambergris with the dung of the three-legged ass is of particular interest for materia medica, as it offers a mythological-cosmological framework for understanding a highly valued medicinal and perfumery substance. The Zoroastrian concept of the three-legged ass purifying ocean water through urination may reflect a cosmological rationale for the medicinal purity attributed to certain marine-derived substances. Bracketed Middle Persian terms (asahmed, shivaned, gird, ambar-ich) are preserved from the West translation as they appear to represent transliterated original terms. The preparation 'Hush' in §13 should be understood as the eschatological Haoma/White Haoma of Zoroastrian soteriology, not a conventional pharmaceutical preparation.

results.title_inferred: The Seventeen Liquid Species, Sacred Rivers, and the Purification of Water
results.original_text: 1. In revelation they mention seventeen species of liquid (maya), as one liquid resides in plants; second, that which is flowing from the mountains that is, the rivers; third, that which is rain-water; fourth, that of tanks and other special constructions; fifth, the semen of animals and men, sixth, the urine of animals and men; seventh, the sweat of animals and men; the eighth liquid is that in the skin of animals and men; ninth, the tears of animals and men; tenth, the blood of animals and men; eleventh, the oil in animals and men, a necessary in both worlds; twelfth, the saliva of animals and men, with which they nourish the embryo; the thirteenth is that which is under the bark of plants, as it is said that every bark has a liquid, through which a drop appears on a twig (tekh) when placed four finger-breadths before a fire; fourteenth, the milk of animals and men. 2. All these, through growth, or the body which is formed, mingle again with the rivers, for the body which is formed and the growth are both one. 3. This, too, they say, that of these three rivers, that is, the Arag river, the Marv river, and the Veh river, the spirits were dissatisfied, so that they would not flow into the world, owing to the defilement of stagnant water (armesht) which they beheld, so that they were in tribulation through it until Zartosht was exhibited to them, whom I (Ohrmazd) will create, who will pour six-fold holy-water (zor) into it and make it again wholesome; he will preach carefulness. 4. This, too, it says, that, of water whose holy-water is more and pollution less, the holy-water has come in excess, and in three years it goes back to the sources; that of which the pollution and holy-water have both become equal, arrives back in six years; that of which the pollution is more and holy-water less, arrives back in nine years. 5. So, also, the growth of plants is connected, in this manner, strongly with the root; so, likewise, the blessings (afrin) which the righteous utter, come back, in this proportion, to themselves. 6. Regarding the river Nahvtak it says, that Frasiyav of Tur conducted it away; and when Ushedar comes it will flow again suitable for horses; so, also, will the fountains of the sea Kyansih. 7. Kyansih is the one where the home (jinak) of the Kayanian race is.
results.translation: 1. In revelation, seventeen species of liquid [maya] are mentioned: the first liquid resides in plants; the second is that which flows from the mountains, that is, rivers; the third is rain-water; the fourth is that of tanks and other purpose-built constructions; the fifth is the semen of animals and men; the sixth is the urine of animals and men; the seventh is the sweat of animals and men; the eighth liquid is that found in the skin of animals and men; the ninth is the tears of animals and men; the tenth is the blood of animals and men; the eleventh is the oil [fat] in animals and men, which is a necessity in both worlds; the twelfth is the saliva of animals and men, with which they nourish the embryo; the thirteenth is that which lies beneath the bark of plants — for it is said that every bark contains a liquid, by virtue of which a drop appears on a twig [tekh] when the twig is held four finger-breadths before a fire; the fourteenth is the milk of animals and men. [Note: the text as transmitted lists only fourteen of the promised seventeen; the remaining three are absent in this passage.] 2. All of these, through growth or through the body that is formed, mingle again with the rivers; for the body that is formed and growth are, in essence, one and the same. 3. They also say that of these three rivers — namely the Arag river, the Marv river, and the Veh river — the spirits were displeased, and would not flow into the world on account of the defilement of stagnant water [armesht] that they beheld; they remained in tribulation because of it until Zartosht [Zoroaster] was revealed to them — he whom I [Ohrmazd] shall bring into existence — who will pour six-fold holy-water [zor] into the rivers and render them wholesome once more; he will teach the people to exercise caution. 4. It also states that water in which the holy-water is greater and the pollution lesser — where the holy-water is in excess — returns to the sources in three years; that in which the pollution and holy-water have become equal returns in six years; and that in which the pollution is greater and the holy-water lesser returns in nine years. 5. Likewise, the growth of plants is in this manner strongly bound to the root; and similarly, the blessings [afrin] which the righteous pronounce return to themselves in the same proportion. 6. Concerning the river Nahvtak, it is said that Frasiyav of Tur diverted it; but when Ushedar comes, it will flow again in a manner fit for horses; and so too will the fountains of the sea Kyansih. 7. Kyansih is the sea where the ancestral home [jinak] of the Kayanian race is located.

entities_mentioned.name: maya (liquid)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for fluid/liquid substances generally
entities_mentioned.notes: The text enumerates seventeen categories of liquid, though only fourteen are explicitly listed in the surviving passage. Includes botanical, bodily, and meteorological fluids.

entities_mentioned.name: tekh (twig)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for a small branch or twig
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in describing how sub-bark plant fluid becomes visible as a droplet when the twig is held near heat — an early empirical observation of plant sap.

entities_mentioned.name: armesht (stagnant water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for standing or stagnant water, considered ritually polluting
entities_mentioned.notes: Stagnant water is treated as a source of ritual and physical defilement in Zoroastrian cosmology.

entities_mentioned.name: zor (holy-water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Consecrated libation water used in Zoroastrian ritual (Avestan: zaothra)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described here as being poured sixfold into defiled rivers to restore purity. Central to Zoroastrian purification rites.

entities_mentioned.name: afrin (blessings)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian/Avestan term for a formal blessing or benediction
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in a cosmological context where righteous blessings are said to return to their utterer, analogous to water returning to its source.

entities_mentioned.name: Arag river
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly the Araxes (modern Aras River, South Caucasus/Iran/Azerbaijan border region)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of three sacred rivers whose spirits refused to flow due to ritual pollution.

entities_mentioned.name: Marv river
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly the Murghab River (modern Turkmenistan/Afghanistan), associated with the city of Merv
entities_mentioned.notes: One of three sacred rivers in this cosmological passage.

entities_mentioned.name: Veh river
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Often identified with the Oxus (modern Amu Darya) or a mythological great world-river in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'Good River'; a major sacred river in the Bundahishn.

entities_mentioned.name: Zartosht (Zoroaster)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster, the prophet of the Zoroastrian religion (c. 1500–600 BCE, dates disputed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Presented here as a future savior figure who will purify the sacred rivers by pouring holy-water and teaching ritual carefulness.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahura Mazda, the supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Speaks in the first person, announcing that he will create Zartosht.

entities_mentioned.name: Nahvtak river
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a river in the Sistan region or a mythological waterway
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have been diverted by Frasiyav of Tur; will be restored by the messianic figure Ushedar.

entities_mentioned.name: Frasiyav of Tur
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Afrasiyab, the legendary Turanian hero-king and adversary of Iran in Zoroastrian and Iranian epic tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Credited here with diverting the Nahvtak river, an act of cosmic disruption.

entities_mentioned.name: Ushedar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ushedar (Avestan: Ukhshyat-ereta), one of the three future savior figures [saoshyants] in Zoroastrian eschatology
entities_mentioned.notes: His coming is associated with the restoration of the Nahvtak river and the fountains of Lake Kyansih.

entities_mentioned.name: Kyansih (sea/lake)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lake Hamoun (Sistan region, modern Iran/Afghanistan border), the legendary home of the Kayanian dynasty
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called Kansaoya in Avestan sources. Described as the ancestral home [jinak] of the Kayanian royal lineage.

entities_mentioned.name: Kayanian race
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Kayanids, the legendary second dynasty of Iranian kings in Zoroastrian and epic tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Their home is located at the sea of Kyansih according to this passage.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text promises seventeen species of liquid but the transmitted passage lists only fourteen explicitly; this is a known lacuna in the Bundahishn manuscript tradition and is flagged in the translation. 2. The phrase 'oil in animals and men, a necessary in both worlds' likely refers to bodily fat or bile, considered essential for both material life and eschatological resurrection — the phrase 'both worlds' (this world and the next) is a standard Zoroastrian doctrinal formulation. 3. The claim that saliva 'nourishes the embryo' reflects ancient physiological beliefs, possibly connecting saliva to the amniotic or nutritive fluids surrounding the fetus. 4. The sub-bark plant fluid and the twig-before-fire test is a notable empirical observation of plant sap and may be of interest to materia medica research regarding how Zoroastrian tradition conceptualized plant biology. 5. The purification timelines (3, 6, 9 years) reflect a cosmological arithmetic based on the ratio of holy-water to pollution — this triadic numerical scheme is consistent with broader Zoroastrian cosmological numerology. 6. The segment is drawn from the Bundahishn ('Primal Creation'), a Middle Persian Zoroastrian encyclopedic text, likely compiled in its extant form in the 9th century CE though drawing on much older Avestan and Pahlavi material.

results.title_inferred: Chieftainship of Men, Animals, Plants, Waters, and Created Things
results.original_text: On the chieftainship of men and animals and every single thing it says in revelation, that first of the human species Gayomard was produced, brilliant and white, with eyes which looked out for the great one, him who was here the Zarathushtrotema (chief high-priest); the chieftainship of all things was from Zartosht. 2. The white ass-goat, which holds its head down, is the chief of goats, the first of those species created. 3. The black sheep which is fat and white-jawed is the chief of sheep; it was the first of those species created. 4. The camel with white-haired knees and two humps is the chief of camels. 5. First the black-haired ox with yellow knees was created; he is the chief of oxen. 6. First the dazzling white (arus) horse, with yellow ears, glossy hair, and white eyes, was produced; he is the chief of horses. 7. The white, cat-footed ass is the chief of asses. 8. First of dogs the fair (arus) dog with yellow hair was produced; he is the chief of dogs. 9. The hare was produced brown (bur); he is the chief of the wide-travelers. 10. Those beasts which have no dread whatever of the hand are evil. 11. First of birds the griffin of three natures was created, not for here (this world), for the Karshipt is the chief, which they call the falcon (chark), that which revelation says was brought to the enclosure formed by Yim [Jamshed]. 12. First of fur animals the white ermine was produced; he is the chief of fur animals; as it says that it is the white ermine which came unto the assembly of the archangels. 13. The Kar-fish, or Arizh, is the chief of the water-creatures. 14. The Daitya river is the chief of streams. 15. The Daraja river is the chief of exalted rivers, for the dwelling of the father of Zartosht was on its banks, and Zartosht was born there. 16. The hoary forest is the chief of forests. 17. Hugar the lofty, on which the water of Aredvisur flows and leaps, is the chief of summits, since it is that above which is the revolution of the constellation Sataves, the chief of reservoirs. 18. The Haoma which is out-squeezed is the chief of medicinal plants. 19. Wheat is the chief of large-seeded grains. 20. The desert wormwood is the chief of non-medicinal plants. 21. The summer vetch, which they also call 'pag' (gavirs), is the chief of small-seeded grains. 22. The kusti (sacred thread-girdle) is the chief of clothes. 23. The Bazayvana is the chief of seas. 24. Of two men, when they come forward together, the wiser and more truthful is chief. 25. This, too, it says in revelation, that Ohrmazd created the whole material world one abode, so that all may be one; for there is much splendor and glory of industry in the world. 26. Whatsoever he performs, who practices that which is good, is the value of the water of life; since water is not created alike in value, for the undefiled water of Aredvisur is worth the whole water of the sky and earth of Xwaniratha, except the Arag river, created by Ohrmazd.
results.translation: 1. Concerning the chieftainship of men and animals and of every individual thing: revelation states that Gayomard, the first of the human species, was produced brilliant and white, with eyes that watched for the great one — he who on earth was the Zarathushtrotema [chief high-priest]; the chieftainship of all things derived from Zartosht [Zoroaster]. 2. The white ass-goat, which holds its head lowered, is the chief of goats, being the first of that species created. 3. The black sheep that is fat and white-jawed is the chief of sheep; it was the first of that species created. 4. The camel with white-haired knees and two humps is the chief of camels. 5. The black-haired ox with yellow knees was created first; he is the chief of oxen. 6. The dazzling white [arus] horse, with yellow ears, glossy hair, and white eyes, was produced first; he is the chief of horses. 7. The white, cat-footed ass is the chief of asses. 8. The fair [arus] dog with yellow hair was the first of dogs produced; he is the chief of dogs. 9. The hare was produced brown [bur]; he is the chief of the wide-travelers [far-ranging animals]. 10. Those beasts that show no dread whatsoever of the human hand are evil. 11. Of birds, the griffin of three natures was created first — though not for this world — for the Karshipt [a mythic bird] is the chief of birds, which they call the falcon [chark], the one that revelation says was brought to the enclosure [vara] formed by Yim [Jamshed]. 12. Of fur animals, the white ermine was produced first; it is the chief of fur animals — as it is said that it was the white ermine that came unto the assembly of the archangels [Amesha Spentas]. 13. The Kar-fish, also called the Arizh, is the chief of water-creatures. 14. The Daitya river is the chief of streams. 15. The Daraja river is the chief of exalted rivers, for the dwelling of the father of Zartosht stood upon its banks, and Zartosht was born there. 16. The hoary [ancient, grey] forest is the chief of forests. 17. Hugar the Lofty, upon which the water of Aredvisur [Anahita] flows and leaps, is the chief of mountain summits, for above it moves the revolution of the constellation Sataves, the chief of reservoirs. 18. The Haoma [pressed juice of the haoma plant] that is out-squeezed is the chief of medicinal plants. 19. Wheat is the chief of large-seeded grains. 20. The desert wormwood is the chief of non-medicinal plants. 21. The summer vetch, which they also call 'pag' [gavirs], is the chief of small-seeded grains. 22. The kusti [sacred thread-girdle] is the chief of garments. 23. The Bazayvana is the chief of seas. 24. Of two men, when they come forward together, the wiser and more truthful is chief. 25. Revelation also states that Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] created the entire material world as one single abode, so that all may be united; for there is great splendor and glory of productive activity [industry] in the world. 26. Whatsoever is performed by one who practices what is good is of the value of the water of life; for not all water is created equal in value — the undefiled water of Aredvisur is worth the totality of the water of the sky and earth of Xwaniratha [the central of the seven climes], except for the Arag river, which was created by Ohrmazd.

entities_mentioned.name: Gayomard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gayomart — the primordial man in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: First human being in Avestan/Pahlavi tradition; analogous to a cosmic progenitor figure.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtrotema
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chief high-priest of the Zoroastrian religious hierarchy
entities_mentioned.notes: Title meaning 'most like Zarathustra'; here used to describe the highest priestly rank.

entities_mentioned.name: Zartosht (Zoroaster)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zarathustra / Zoroaster, prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism

entities_mentioned.name: Karshipt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A mythic bird of Zoroastrian tradition; sometimes equated with a raptor such as a falcon or eagle
entities_mentioned.notes: Described here as the chief of birds and identified with the falcon (chark); said to have brought the religion to Yima's enclosure (vara).

entities_mentioned.name: Yim / Jamshed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jamshid — legendary king of Iranian mythology
entities_mentioned.notes: Builder of the vara (underground enclosure) to preserve life during a cosmic winter; equated with Yima in the Avesta.

entities_mentioned.name: Kar-fish (Arizh)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A mythic great fish of Zoroastrian cosmology; possibly related to the Avestan Kara fish
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief of water-creatures in Bundahishn cosmology.

entities_mentioned.name: Daitya river
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Veh Daitya; associated in some scholarship with the Oxus (Amu Darya) or a mythic river
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief of all rivers in Zoroastrian sacred geography.

entities_mentioned.name: Daraja river
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; traditionally associated with the birthplace of Zoroaster, possibly in the Atropatene / Azerbaijan region
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to be the birthplace of Zoroaster and the home of his father.

entities_mentioned.name: Aredvisur (Anahita)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aredvi Sura Anahita — Zoroastrian yazata (deity) of waters
entities_mentioned.notes: Her waters flow from Mount Hugar; she is a central figure in Avestan water cosmology.

entities_mentioned.name: Hugar the Lofty
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A mythic high mountain in Zoroastrian cosmology; possibly associated with the Alborz / Hara Berezaiti mountain range
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief of summits; site from which Aredvisur's waters flow.

entities_mentioned.name: Sataves
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A Zoroastrian stellar or celestial entity; associated with a star or constellation functioning as chief of reservoirs
entities_mentioned.notes: Revolves above Mount Hugar in Bundahishn cosmology.

entities_mentioned.name: Haoma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Haoma / Soma — a ritual plant and its pressed juice used in Zoroastrian and Vedic ceremonies; possibly Ephedra species or Peganum harmala
entities_mentioned.notes: Named chief of medicinal plants. Preparation method: pressing/squeezing ('out-squeezed'). Condition treated: broadly medicinal and ritual purification. See also Materia Medica entry.

entities_mentioned.name: Wheat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum or related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief of large-seeded grains.

entities_mentioned.name: Desert wormwood
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia species (desert wormwood); likely Artemisia herba-alba or related Central Asian species
entities_mentioned.notes: Named chief of non-medicinal plants; distinguished explicitly from medicinal plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Summer vetch (pag / gavirs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Vicia sativa (common vetch) or a related legume
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief of small-seeded grains; two names given: 'pag' and 'gavirs'.

entities_mentioned.name: Kusti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The sacred woven cord/girdle worn by Zoroastrians
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief of all garments; central ritual object in Zoroastrian practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Bazayvana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A mythic or cosmological sea in Zoroastrian geography; identity uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Named chief of seas.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd (Ahura Mazda)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Creator of the material world and of the Arag river.

entities_mentioned.name: Xwaniratha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The central of the seven karshvars (climes/continents) in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: The inhabited central region of the world.

entities_mentioned.name: Arag river
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly the Araxes (Aras) river in the Caucasus region
entities_mentioned.notes: Created directly by Ohrmazd; exempted from comparison with Aredvisur's waters.

entities_mentioned.name: White ermine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mustela erminea (stoat/ermine in winter coat)
entities_mentioned.notes: First fur animal created; said to have attended the assembly of the Amesha Spentas (archangels).

results.translator_notes: This passage is from the Bundahishn ('Original Creation'), a Pahlavi Zoroastrian encyclopedic text compiled roughly 9th century CE but drawing on much older Avestan sources. The chapter enumerates the archetypal or 'first-created' and therefore 'chief' member of each category of being, plant, water, and object. For Materia Medica purposes, the most significant entry is §18: Haoma (pressed/squeezed juice) is explicitly designated the chief of medicinal plants [Middle Persian: *darmān-giyāh*], distinguishing it from non-medicinal plants (§20, desert wormwood). The preparation described is extraction by pressing, consistent with Avestan and Vedic ritual practice. The term 'arus' (brilliant white) applied to the horse and dog is a Pahlavi color/quality descriptor. The bracket '[Jamshed]' is a traditional editorial gloss equating Avestan Yima with the later Persian Jamshid. OCR in the original was generally clean; one minor punctuation issue corrected in §25 ('This, too it says' → 'This, too, it says'). The phrase 'wide-travelers' (chief category of the hare) likely refers to swift or wide-ranging animals. The griffin 'of three natures' is a mythic composite creature distinct from the worldly Karshipt/falcon.

results.title_inferred: The Religious Calendar and Division of the Year into Seasons, Day-Periods, and Summer/Winter
results.original_text: The religious calendar 1. On matters of religion it says in revelation thus: 'The creatures of the world were created by me complete in three hundred and sixty-five days,' that is, the six periods of the Gahambars which are completed in a year. 2. It is always necessary first to count the day and afterwards the night, for first the day goes off, and then the night comes on. 3. And from the season (gas) of Maidyoshahem, which is the auspicious day Khwarshed of the month Tishtar, to the season of Maidyarem, which is the auspicious day Warharan of the month Din [Dae]-- the shortest day -- the night increases; and from the season of Maidyarem to the season of Maidyoshahem the night decreases and the day increases. 4. The summer day is as much as two of the shortest winter days, and the winter night is as much as two of the shortest summer nights. 5. The summer day is twelve Hasars, the night six Hasars; the winter night is twelve Hasars, the day six; a Hasar being a measure of time and, in like manner, of land. 6. In the season of Hamaspathmaidyem, that is, the five supplementary days at the end of the month Spandarmad, the day and night are again equal. 7. As from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Frawardin to the auspicious day Anagran of the month Mihr is the summer of seven months, so from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Aban to the auspicious month Spandarmad, on to the end of the five supplementary days, is the winter of five months. 8. The priest fulfills the regulation (vachar) about a corpse and other things, by this calculation as to summer and winter. 9. In those seven months of summer the periods (gas) of the days and nights are five since one celebrates the Rapithwin namely, the period of day-break is Hawan, the period of midday is Rapithwin, the period of afternoon is Uzerin, when the appearance of the stars has come into the sky until midnight is the period of Aiwisruthrem, from midnight until the stars become imperceptible is the period of Ushahin. 10. In winter are four periods, for from daybreak till Uzerin is all Hawan, and the rest as I have said; and the reason of it is this, that the appearance of winter is in the direction of the north, where the regions Wourubareshti and Wourujareshti are; the original dwelling of summer, too, is in the south, where the regions Fradadhafshu and Widadhafshu are; on the day Ohrmazd of the auspicious month Aban the winter acquires strength and enters into the world, and the spirit of Rapithwin goes from above-ground to below-ground, where the spring (khani) of waters is, and diffuses warmth and moisture in the water, and so many roots of trees do not wither with cold and drought. 11. And on the auspicious day Adar of the month Din the winter arrives, with much cold, at Eranvej; and until the end, in the auspicious month Spandarmad, winter advances through the whole world; on this account they kindle a fire everywhere on the day Adar of the month Din, and it forms an indication tha[t...]
results.translation: The Religious Calendar. 1. Concerning matters of religion, it is stated in revelation as follows: 'The creatures of the world were created by me complete in three hundred and sixty-five days' — that is, the six periods of the Gahambars [sacred festival seasons], which are completed in the course of one year. 2. It is always necessary to count the day first and the night afterwards, for the day departs first and then the night follows. 3. From the season [gāh] of Maidyoshahem [Mid-Summer], which falls on the auspicious day Khwarshed of the month Tishtar, to the season of Maidyarem [Mid-Year/Winter], which falls on the auspicious day Warharan of the month Din [Dae] — the shortest day — the night increases in length; and from the season of Maidyarem back to the season of Maidyoshahem, the night decreases and the day increases. 4. The summer day is equal in length to two of the shortest winter days, and the winter night is equal in length to two of the shortest summer nights. 5. The summer day is twelve Hasars [a unit of time] long and the night six Hasars; the winter night is twelve Hasars long and the day six; a Hasar being a measure both of time and, likewise, of distance on land. 6. In the season of Hamaspathmaidyem — that is, the five intercalary [supplementary] days appended at the end of the month Spandarmad — the day and night are again equal. 7. Just as the period from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Frawardin to the auspicious day Anagran of the month Mihr constitutes the summer of seven months, so the period from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Aban through the auspicious month Spandarmad, including the end of the five supplementary days, constitutes the winter of five months. 8. The priest fulfills the prescribed regulation [vachar] concerning a corpse and other matters by means of this calculation of summer and winter. 9. During those seven months of summer, the periods [gāh] of the days and nights are five in number, since one celebrates the Rapithwin [mid-day watch]: namely, the period of daybreak is Hawan; the period of midday is Rapithwin; the period of afternoon is Uzerin; the period from the appearance of the stars in the sky until midnight is Aiwisruthrem; and from midnight until the stars become imperceptible is the period of Ushahin. 10. In winter there are four periods, for from daybreak until Uzerin counts entirely as Hawan, and the rest remain as I have stated; and the reason for this is that the domain of winter lies in the direction of the north, where the regions Wourubareshti and Wourujareshti are situated; likewise, the original seat of summer is in the south, where the regions Fradadhafshu and Widadhafshu are. On the day Ohrmazd of the auspicious month Aban, winter gains strength and enters the world, and the spirit of Rapithwin descends from above the earth to below the earth, where the spring [khani] of waters lies, and there diffuses warmth and moisture through the water, so that the roots of many trees do not wither from cold and drought. 11. And on the auspicious day Adar of the month Din, winter arrives at Eranvej [the mythical primordial homeland] with great cold; and continuing to the end of the auspicious month Spandarmad, winter advances throughout the entire world. For this reason, fires are kindled everywhere on the day Adar of the month Din, and this serves as a sign that... [TEXT ENDS ABRUPTLY]

entities_mentioned.name: Gahambars
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Six Zoroastrian seasonal festivals
entities_mentioned.notes: The six obligatory Zoroastrian liturgical feasts marking divisions of the year; collectively spanning 365 days.

entities_mentioned.name: Maidyoshahem
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian festival of Mid-Summer (Maidhyoi-shema)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the six Gahambars; occurs in the month Tishtar on the day Khwarshed.

entities_mentioned.name: Maidyarem
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian festival of Mid-Winter (Maidhyairya)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Gahambar marking the shortest day; occurs in the month Din [Dae] on the day Warharan.

entities_mentioned.name: Hamaspathmaidyem
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian festival at year's end / Pateti / No Ruz eve
entities_mentioned.notes: The five intercalary (epagomenal) days appended to the end of month Spandarmad; the day and night are equal at this time (equinox period).

entities_mentioned.name: Hasar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Parasang or similar Persian unit of measure (time and distance)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a unit of both time and land distance; in Pahlavi texts, the hasar (frasang/parasang) is typically ~3.5–6 km as a distance unit; its application as a time unit here denotes a proportional division of the daylight/night hours.

entities_mentioned.name: Rapithwin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The midday liturgical watch (gāh) in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Also personified as a spiritual entity whose presence underground during winter warms the roots of trees and preserves water sources; celebrated only in summer months.

entities_mentioned.name: Hawan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian dawn/morning liturgical watch (gāh)
entities_mentioned.notes: Covers daybreak; in winter it also absorbs the midday period since Rapithwin is not celebrated.

entities_mentioned.name: Uzerin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian afternoon liturgical watch (gāh)

entities_mentioned.name: Aiwisruthrem
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian evening/first-night liturgical watch (gāh)
entities_mentioned.notes: Spans from the appearance of stars until midnight.

entities_mentioned.name: Ushahin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian pre-dawn liturgical watch (gāh)
entities_mentioned.notes: Spans from midnight until the stars fade.

entities_mentioned.name: Wourubareshti and Wourujareshti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological northern world-regions in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: Named as the northern cosmic regions associated with winter in the Bundahishn geographical scheme.

entities_mentioned.name: Fradadhafshu and Widadhafshu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological southern world-regions in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: Named as the southern cosmic regions associated with summer.

entities_mentioned.name: Eranvej
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Airyanem Vaejah — the mythical Zoroastrian primordial homeland
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the first place where winter arrives with great cold; located conceptually in the far north or center of the Zoroastrian world.

entities_mentioned.name: Tishtar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fourth month of the Zoroastrian calendar (roughly June–July)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also the name of the star Sirius in Zoroastrian tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Din (Dae)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tenth month of the Zoroastrian calendar (roughly December–January)
entities_mentioned.notes: The month containing the winter solstice; also called Dae.

entities_mentioned.name: Frawardin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: First month of the Zoroastrian calendar (roughly March–April)
entities_mentioned.notes: The month in which summer begins per the Bundahishn reckoning.

entities_mentioned.name: Mihr
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seventh month of the Zoroastrian calendar (roughly September–October)
entities_mentioned.notes: The month in which the seven-month summer ends.

entities_mentioned.name: Aban
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eighth month of the Zoroastrian calendar (roughly October–November)
entities_mentioned.notes: The month in which winter begins per this text.

entities_mentioned.name: Spandarmad
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Twelfth month of the Zoroastrian calendar (roughly February–March)
entities_mentioned.notes: The final month of the year, after which the five intercalary days (Hamaspathmaidyem) are added.

entities_mentioned.name: Adar (day of month Din)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The ninth day of the month Din in the Zoroastrian calendar
entities_mentioned.notes: The day on which winter is said to arrive at Eranvej and on which fires are kindled throughout the world; corresponds to the festival of Adar Jashan.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahura Mazda — the supreme deity of Zoroastrianism; also the first day of each calendar month
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as the name of the first day of the months Frawardin and Aban.

entities_mentioned.name: vachar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Priestly regulation or legal ruling (Pahlavi legal/religious term)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers specifically here to rules governing the handling of corpses, which differ between summer and winter per Zoroastrian purity law.

results.translator_notes: 1. This segment is from the Bundahishn (Chapter 59 per the label), a Middle Persian Zoroastrian encyclopedic text; the translation provided follows the classical West translation tradition with bracketed clarifications added for technical terms. 2. The text ends abruptly mid-sentence ('it forms an indication tha[t...]'), indicating the source OCR or manuscript is truncated at this point; marked accordingly. 3. 'Hasar' as a time unit is unusual and may reflect a proportional scheme where 18 Hasars = one full day-night cycle, with 12 assigned to the long portion and 6 to the short in solstice conditions; the exact equivalence in modern hours is uncertain. 4. The segment is not a materia medica text in the medical/botanical sense; no plants, drugs, or medical preparations are described. The extraction fields for plant/drug entities are therefore not applicable, and the entities_mentioned array covers the religious, calendrical, and cosmological terms instead. 5. 'Din' and 'Dae' are variant transliterations of the same month name (Pahlavi: Dae); both appear in the source, suggesting an editorial gloss in the original translation.
