results.title_inferred: On the Evil-Doing of Ahriman and the Demons (Bundahishn Chapter 62)
results.original_text: On the evil-doing of Ahriman and the demons 1. On the evildoing of Ahriman and the demons it says in revelation, that the evil which the evil spirit has produced for the creation of Ohrmazd it is possible to tell by this winter; and his body is that of a lizard (vazagh) whose place is filth (kalch). 2. He does not think, nor speak, nor act for the welfare (nadukih) of the creatures of Ohrmazd; and his business is unmercifulness and the destruction of this welfare, so that the creatures which Ohrmazd shall increase he will destroy; and his eyesight (chashm michihsn) does not refrain from doing the creatures harm. 3. As it says that, 'ever since a creature was created by us, I, who am Ohrmazd, have not rested at ease, on account of providing protection for my own creatures; and likewise not even he, the evil spirit, on account of contriving evil for the creatures.' 4. And by their devotion to witchcraft (yatuk-dinoih) he seduces mankind into affection for himself and disaffection to Ohrmazd, so that they forsake the religion of Ohrmazd, and practice that of Ahriman. 5. He casts this into the thoughts of men, that this religion of Ohrmazd is naught, and it is not necessary to be steadfast in it. 6. Whoever gives that man anything, in whose law (dad) this saying is established, then the evil spirit is propitiated by him, that is, he has acted by his pleasure. 7. The business of Akoman is this, that he gave vile thoughts and discord to the creatures. 8. The business of the demon Andar is this, that he constrains the thoughts of the creatures from deeds of virtue, just like a leader who has well-constrained (sardar-i khup afsardo); and he casts this into the thoughts of men, that it is not necessary to have the sacred shirt [sudre] and thread-girdle [kusti]. 9. The business of the demon Savar, that is a leader of the demons, is this, that is, misgovernment, oppressive anarchy, and drunkenness. 10. The business of the demon Naikiyas is this, that he gives discontent to the creatures; as it says, that should this one give anything to those men whose opinion (dad) is this, that it is not necessary to have the sacred shirt and thread-girdle, then Andar, Savar, and Naikiyas are propitiated by him. 11. The demon Taprev is he who mingles poison with plants and creatures, as it says thus: 'Taprev the frustrater, and Zairich the maker of poison.' 12. All those six, it is said, are arch-fiends of the demons; the rest are cooperating and confederate with them. 13. This, too, it says, that should one give anything to a man who says that it is proper to have one boot, and in his law walking with one boot is established, then the fiend Taprev is propitiated by him. 14. The demon Taromat is he who produces disobedience; the demon Mitrokht is the liar (drojan) of the evil spirit; the demon Arashk ('malice') is the spiteful fiend of the evil eye. 15. Theirs are the same appliances as the demon Eshm's, as it says that seven powers are given to Eshm, that he.
results.translation: On the Evil-Doing of Ahriman and the Demons. 1. Concerning the evil-doing of Ahriman and the demons, it is stated in revelation that the evil which the evil spirit has wrought against the creation of Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] may be known through this: his body is that of a lizard [vazagh], whose dwelling place is filth [kalch]. 2. He neither thinks, nor speaks, nor acts for the wellbeing [nadukih] of the creatures of Ohrmazd; his occupation is mercilessness and the destruction of that wellbeing, so that whatever creatures Ohrmazd causes to increase, he will destroy; and his gaze [chashm michihsn] does not refrain from doing harm to the creatures. 3. As it is said: 'Ever since a creature was created by us, I, who am Ohrmazd, have not rested at ease, on account of providing protection for my own creatures; and likewise, not even he—the evil spirit—on account of contriving evil for the creatures.' 4. And through devotion to witchcraft [yatuk-dinoih], he seduces mankind into love of himself and hatred of Ohrmazd, so that they forsake the religion of Ohrmazd and practice that of Ahriman. 5. He instils into the minds of men the idea that the religion of Ohrmazd is nothing, and that it is not necessary to remain steadfast in it. 6. Whoever gives anything to a man in whose law [dad] this saying is established thereby propitiates the evil spirit—that is, he has acted according to the evil spirit's will. 7. The work of Akoman [the demon of Evil Mind] is this: that he imparts vile thoughts and discord to the creatures. 8. The work of the demon Andar is this: that he constrains the thoughts of the creatures away from virtuous deeds, just as a leader who has bound his followers well [sardar-i khup afsardo]; and he instils into the minds of men that it is not necessary to wear the sacred shirt [sudre] and the sacred thread-girdle [kusti]. 9. The work of the demon Savar—who is a chief among the demons—is this: misgovernment, oppressive anarchy, and drunkenness. 10. The work of the demon Naikiyas is this: that he imparts discontent to the creatures; as it is said, should anyone give anything to those men who hold the opinion [dad] that it is not necessary to have the sacred shirt and thread-girdle, then Andar, Savar, and Naikiyas are propitiated by him. 11. The demon Taprev is he who mingles poison with plants and living creatures, as it is said: 'Taprev the frustrater, and Zairich the maker of poison.' 12. All these six, it is said, are arch-fiends among the demons; the remainder are cooperating with and allied to them. 13. It also says that should one give anything to a man who maintains that it is proper to wear only one boot, and in whose law walking with one boot is established, then the fiend Taprev is propitiated by him. 14. The demon Taromat is he who produces disobedience; the demon Mitrokht is the liar [drojan] of the evil spirit; the demon Arashk ('Malice') is the spiteful fiend of the evil eye. 15. Their instruments are the same as those of the demon Eshm [Wrath], as it is said that seven powers are given to Eshm, that he [text breaks off].

entities_mentioned.name: Ahriman (Angra Mainyu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme evil spirit in Zoroastrian theology
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having the body of a lizard (vazagh) dwelling in filth (kalch); a well-known Zoroastrian theological figure.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd (Ahura Mazda)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity in Zoroastrian theology
entities_mentioned.notes: Creator god opposed to Ahriman in Zoroastrian cosmology.

entities_mentioned.name: Akoman
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of Evil Mind (Aka Manah) in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the six arch-demons (archdivs); his function is to instil vile thoughts and discord.

entities_mentioned.name: Andar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon corresponding to Indra in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the six arch-demons; constrains creatures from virtuous deeds and discourages wearing of sudre and kusti.

entities_mentioned.name: Savar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of misgovernment and anarchy in Zoroastrian demonology (possibly corresponding to Vedic Shiva or a separate figure)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a chief demon associated with misgovernment, oppressive anarchy, and drunkenness.

entities_mentioned.name: Naikiyas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of discontent in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the six arch-demons; imparts discontent to creatures.

entities_mentioned.name: Taprev
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of poison/corruption in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly described as mingling poison with plants and creatures; paired with Zairich the poison-maker. Relevant to Materia Medica context as a personification of plant-based toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: Zairich
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of poison-making in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: Named 'maker of poison'; paired with Taprev the frustrater. Relevant to Materia Medica context as a personification of poisonous substances.

entities_mentioned.name: Taromat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of disobedience in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: Produces disobedience among creatures.

entities_mentioned.name: Mitrokht
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of falsehood/lying in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the 'liar (drojan) of the evil spirit.'

entities_mentioned.name: Arashk
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of malice and the evil eye in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: Name glossed as 'malice'; associated with the evil eye.

entities_mentioned.name: Eshm (Aeshma)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Demon of Wrath in Zoroastrian demonology
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to possess seven powers; text breaks off before these are enumerated.

entities_mentioned.name: sudre (sacred shirt)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Zoroastrian inner sacred garment worn by initiated believers
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to as a marker of religious adherence; demons work to dissuade the wearing of it.

entities_mentioned.name: kusti (thread-girdle)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Zoroastrian sacred cord worn around the waist by initiated believers
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with sudre as a fundamental Zoroastrian religious observance.

entities_mentioned.name: vazagh (lizard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generic lizard; possibly a specific species considered impure in Zoroastrian tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a description of Ahriman's bodily form; lizards were considered khrafstars (noxious creatures) in Zoroastrianism.

entities_mentioned.name: yatuk-dinoih (witchcraft/sorcery)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian term for demonic sorcery or witchcraft
entities_mentioned.notes: The means by which Ahriman seduces humans away from Ohrmazd's religion.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Bundahishn (Middle Persian: 'Primal Creation'), a Zoroastrian cosmological text compiled in the 9th century CE from earlier Avestan and Pahlavi sources. The chapter enumerates the six principal arch-demons (archdivs) and their specific evil functions. Of particular relevance to a Materia Medica context is the pairing of Taprev and Zairich as demons of plant-poisoning and poison-making respectively, reflecting Zoroastrian concern with toxic substances in the natural world. The Pahlavi technical terms (vazagh, kalch, nadukih, chashm michihsn, yatuk-dinoih, sardar-i khup afsardo, dad, drojan) are preserved from the source transcription. The text ends mid-sentence at section 15, indicating the segment is incomplete. The OCR error 'wag' (for 'was') in section 3 has been silently corrected. The reference to 'one boot' in section 13 is a known Zoroastrian religious transgression (associated with improper ritual behavior), though its precise meaning remains debated by scholars.

results.title_inferred: On the Resurrection and Future Existence (Bundahishn, Chapter 64)
results.original_text: On the resurrection and future existence. 1. On the nature of the resurrection and future existence it says in revelation, that, whereas Mashye and Mashyane, who grew up from the earth, first fed upon water, then plants, then milk, and then meat, men also, when their time of death has come, first desist from eating meat, then milk, then from bread, till when they shall die they always feed upon water. 2. So, likewise, in the millennium of Ushedarmah, the strength of appetite (az) will thus diminish, when men will remain three days and nights in superabundance (sirih) through one taste of consecrated food. 3. Then they will desist from meat food, and eat vegetables and milk; afterwards, they abstain from milk food and abstain from vegetable food, and are feeding on water; and for ten years before Soshyant comes they remain without food, and do not die. 4. After Soshyant comes they prepare the raising of the dead, as it says, that Zartosht asked of Ohrmazd thus: 'Whence does a body form again, which the wind has carried and the water conveyed (vazhid)? and how does the resurrection occur?' 5. Ohrmazd answered thus: 'When through me the sky arose from the substance of the ruby, without columns, on the spiritual support of far-compassed light; when through me the earth arose, which bore the material life, and there is no maintainer of the worldly creation but it; when by me the sun and moon and stars are conducted in the firmament (andarvai) of luminous bodies; when by me corn was created so that, scattered about in the earth, it grew again and returned with increase; when by me color of various kinds was created in plants; when by me fire was created in plants and other things without combustion; when by me a son was created and fashioned in the womb of a mother, and the structure (pishak) severally of the skin, nails, blood, feet, eyes, ears, and other things was produced; when by me legs were created for the water, so that it flows away, and the cloud was created which carries the water of the world and rains there where it has a purpose; when by me the air was created which conveys in one's eyesight, through the strength of the wind, the lowermost upwards according to its will, and one is not able to grasp it with the hand out-stretched; each one of them, when created by me, was herein more difficult than causing the resurrection, for it is an assistance to me in the resurrection that they exist, but when they were formed it was not forming the future out of the past. 6. Observe that when that which was not was then produced, why is it not possible to produce again that which was? for at that time one will demand the bone from the spirit of earth, the blood from the water, the hair from the plants, and the life from fire, since they were delivered to them in the original creation.' 7. First, the bones of Gayomard are roused up, then those of Mashye and Mashyane, then those of the rest of mankind; in the fifty-seven years of Soshyant they prepare [the raising of the dead].
results.translation: On the Resurrection and Future Existence. 1. Concerning the nature of the resurrection and future existence, it is stated in revelation that, just as Mashye and Mashyane [the primordial human pair], who grew up from the earth, first subsisted on water, then on plants, then on milk, and then on meat, so too will men, when their time of death has come, first cease eating meat, then milk, then bread, until at the moment of death they subsist on water alone. 2. Likewise, in the millennium of Ushedarmah [the second of the three saviors of the end times], the force of appetite [az] will so diminish that men will remain in a state of complete satiety [sirih] for three days and nights from a single taste of consecrated food. 3. Then they will abstain from meat and subsist on vegetables and milk; afterwards they will abstain from milk and then from vegetable food, living on water alone; and for ten years before the coming of Soshyant [the final savior] they will remain entirely without food, yet will not die. 4. After Soshyant comes, they will undertake the raising of the dead. As it is written, Zartosht [Zoroaster] asked Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda]: 'From where does a body take form again, once the wind has scattered it and the water has carried it away [vazhid]? And how does the resurrection come to pass?' 5. Ohrmazd answered thus: 'When through me the sky arose from the substance of the ruby, without columns, upheld by the spiritual support of far-reaching light; when through me the earth arose, which sustains material life and without which there is no maintainer of worldly creation; when through me the sun, moon, and stars were set in motion in the firmament [andarvai] of luminous bodies; when through me grain was created so that, scattered into the earth, it grew again and came back multiplied; when through me color of many kinds was created in plants; when through me fire was created within plants and other things without combustion; when through me a son was created and shaped in the womb of a mother, and the structure [pishak] of the skin, nails, blood, feet, eyes, ears, and all other parts was formed individually; when through me motion was given to water so that it flows away, and the cloud was created to carry the water of the world and to rain wherever it is purposed; when through me the air was created, which by the force of the wind carries even the lowest things upward as it wills, so that one cannot grasp it with an outstretched hand — each one of these acts of creation, when accomplished by me, was more difficult than bringing about the resurrection. For their very existence now assists me in the resurrection; but when they were first formed, there was no precedent — no past thing from which to fashion the future. 6. Consider: if that which did not exist was then brought into being, why should it not be possible to bring back into being that which once existed? For at that time the bone will be demanded back from the spirit of the earth, the blood from the water, the hair from the plants, and the life from fire, since these were entrusted to them at the original creation.' 7. First, the bones of Gayomard [the primordial man] are raised up, then those of Mashye and Mashyane, then those of the rest of humanity; and in the fifty-seven years of Soshyant's ministry they complete [the raising of the dead].

entities_mentioned.name: Mashye and Mashyane
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primordial human couple in Zoroastrian cosmology (cf. Adam and Eve in Abrahamic traditions)
entities_mentioned.notes: Said to have grown from the earth (from the seed of the slain primordial bull or Gayomard). Their dietary progression from water to plants to milk to meat mirrors the eschatological regression described here.

entities_mentioned.name: Ushedarmah
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ukhshyat-ereta / Hushedar-mah — second of the three eschatological saviors in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three posthumous sons of Zoroaster, associated with the second millennium of the end times.

entities_mentioned.name: az
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian concept of 'concupiscence' or 'greed/appetite' — a demonic force
entities_mentioned.notes: Rendered here as 'strength of appetite'; az is a key term in Zoroastrian eschatology and demonology, personified as a demon of greed.

entities_mentioned.name: sirih
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for satiety or superabundance
entities_mentioned.notes: Translated in the source as 'superabundance'; contextually means complete fullness or satiety.

entities_mentioned.name: Soshyant
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saoshyant — the final eschatological savior in Zoroastrianism, who will raise the dead and accomplish the renovation of the world (Frashegird)
entities_mentioned.notes: The third posthumous son of Zoroaster; his fifty-seven-year ministry corresponds to the period of resurrection.

entities_mentioned.name: Zartosht
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster / Zarathustra — the prophet-founder of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian form of the name Zarathustra.

entities_mentioned.name: Ohrmazd
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahura Mazda — the supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Middle Persian form of Avestan Ahura Mazda.

entities_mentioned.name: andarvai
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for the firmament or intermediate atmospheric/celestial space
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'in between'; used to denote the vault of the sky or firmament.

entities_mentioned.name: vazhid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian past participle meaning 'carried' or 'conveyed' (by water)
entities_mentioned.notes: Retained by the source translator as a technical term; from the verb vazidan.

entities_mentioned.name: pishak
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Middle Persian term for 'structure,' 'form,' or 'arrangement'
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here to describe the bodily structure or organization of anatomical parts.

entities_mentioned.name: Gayomard
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gayomard (Avestan: Gaya Maretan) — the primordial man in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: His bones are the first to be raised at the resurrection, consistent with his role as the archetypal first human.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a well-known passage from the Bundahishn ('Primal Creation'), a Middle Persian Zoroastrian encyclopedic text compiled in its extant form c. 8th–9th century CE, though drawing on older Avestan and Pahlavi traditions. The source text presented here is already in English translation (by E.W. West, Sacred Books of the East, vol. 5, 1880), so this task involved cleaning, annotating, and re-rendering West's Victorian translation into clearer modern scholarly English while preserving key technical terms in brackets. The passage is eschatological and cosmological in nature, not a materia medica source per se; however, it contains notable references to dietary practices (progression from meat to water) in an eschatological-medical context, and to plant fire (fire latent in plants without combustion), which may have relevance for understanding Zoroastrian theories of vital heat and plant properties. No plant drug identifications or preparation methods are present. The 'ruby' substance from which the sky is formed is a cosmological metaphor, not a pharmaceutical reference.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 1: The Sixteen Good Lands Created by Ahura Mazda and Their Counter-Plagues by Angra Mainyu
results.original_text: Sixteen perfect lands created by Ahura Mazda, and as many plagues created by Angra Mainyu. 1. Ahura Mazda spake unto Spitama Zarathushtra, saying: I have made every land dear (to its people), even though it had no charms whatever in it: had I not made every land dear (to its people), even though it had no charms whatever in it, then the whole living world would have invaded the Airyana Vaeja. Notes: 1. Or Spitamide. Zarathushtra was descended from Spitama at the fifth generation. 2. 'Everyone fancies that the land where he was born and has been brought up is the best and fairest land that I have created' (Comm.) 3. Greater Bundahish: 'It is said in the Sacred Book: had I not created the Genius of the native place, all mankind would have gone to Eran-Vej, on account of its pleasantness.' — On Airyanem Vaeja or Eran-Vej, see following note. 2. The first of the good lands and countries which I, Ahura Mazda, created, was the Airyana Vaeja, by the Vanguhi Daitya. Thereupon came Angra Mainyu, who is all death, and he counter-created the serpent in the river and Winter, a work of the Daevas. 4. Clause 2 in the Vendidad Sada is composed of Zend quotations in the Commentary that illustrate the alternative process of creation: 'First, Ahura Mazda would create a land of such kind that its dwellers might like it, and there could be nothing more delightful. Then he who is all death would bring against it a counter-creation.' 5. Airyanem Vaeja, Iran-Vej, is the holy land of Zoroastrianism: Zarathushtra was born and founded his religion there (Bund. 20.32; 32.3): the first animal couple appeared there (Bund. 14.4; Zadspram, 9.8). From its name, 'the Iranian seed,' it seems to have been considered as the original seat of the Iranian race. It has been generally supposed to belong to Eastern Iran, like the provinces which are enumerated after it, chiefly on account of the name of its river, the Vanguhi Daitya, which was in the Sassanian times (as Veh) the name of the Oxus. But the Bundahish distinctly states that Iran-Vej is 'bordering upon Adarbajan' (29.12); now, Adarbaijan is bordered by the Caspian Sea on the east, by the Rangha provinces on the west, by Media proper on the south, and by Arran on the north. The Rangha provinces are out of question, since they are mentioned at the end of the Fargard (verse 20), and the climatic conditions of Iran-Vej with its long winter likewise exclude Media and suit Arran, where the summer lasts hardly two months (cf. § 4, note 6). The very name agrees, as the country known as Arran seems to have been known to the Greeks as Ariania (Stephanus Byz.), which brings it close to our Airyanem. On the Vanguhi Daitya, see following note. 6. The Vanguhi Daitya, belonging to Arran, must be the modern Aras (the classic Araxes). The Aras was named Vanguhi, like the Oxus, but distinguished from it by the addition Daitya, which made it 'the Vanguhi of the Law' (the Vanguhi by which Zarathushtra r[eceived his revelation — text truncated].
results.translation:
Sixteen perfect lands created by Ahura Mazda, and as many plagues created by Angra Mainyu.

1. Ahura Mazda spoke unto Spitama [i.e., Spitamide] Zarathushtra, saying: I have made every land dear to its people, even though it had no charms whatever in it; for had I not made every land dear to its people, even though it had no charms whatever in it, then the whole living world would have invaded the Airyana Vaeja [the holy Iranian homeland].

[Translator's note 1: Or 'Spitamide.' Zarathushtra was descended from Spitama at the fifth generation.]
[Note 2: 'Everyone fancies that the land where he was born and has been brought up is the best and fairest land that I have created' (Commentary).]
[Note 3: The Greater Bundahish states: 'It is said in the Sacred Book: had I not created the Genius of the native place, all mankind would have gone to Eran-Vej, on account of its pleasantness.' — On Airyanem Vaeja or Eran-Vej, see the following note.]

2. The first of the good lands and countries which I, Ahura Mazda, created, was the Airyana Vaeja [Iranian seed-land], watered by the Vanguhi Daitya [the Lawful Good River]. Thereupon came Angra Mainyu [the Destructive Spirit], who is all death, and he counter-created the serpent in the river and Winter — a work of the Daevas [evil spirits].

[Note 4: In the Vendidad Sada, clause 2 is composed of Avestan quotations in the Commentary illustrating the alternating process of creation: 'First, Ahura Mazda would create a land of such a kind that its dwellers might like it, and there could be nothing more delightful. Then he who is all death would bring against it a counter-creation.']
[Note 5: Airyanem Vaeja — Iran-Vej — is the holy land of Zoroastrianism: Zarathushtra was born there and founded his religion there (Bundahish 20.32; 32.3); the first animal couple appeared there (Bundahish 14.4; Zadspram 9.8). From its name, meaning 'the Iranian seed,' it appears to have been regarded as the original seat of the Iranian race. It has generally been supposed to lie in Eastern Iran, like the provinces enumerated after it in this Fargard, principally because of the name of its river, the Vanguhi Daitya, which in Sassanian times (as 'Veh') was the name of the Oxus. However, the Bundahish explicitly states that Iran-Vej 'borders upon Adarbajan' (29.12); Adarbaijan [Azerbaijan] is bounded by the Caspian Sea to the east, by the Rangha provinces to the west, by Media proper to the south, and by Arran to the north. The Rangha provinces are excluded from consideration, since they are mentioned at the end of the Fargard (verse 20); and the climatic conditions of Iran-Vej — notably its prolonged winter — likewise exclude Media and point instead to Arran, where summer lasts barely two months (cf. § 4, note 6). The name itself is consistent with this identification, since the country known as Arran appears to have been called Ariania by the Greeks (Stephanus of Byzantium), which brings it close to Airyanem.]
[Note 6: The Vanguhi Daitya, belonging to the region of Arran, must then be identified with the modern Aras River (the classical Araxes). The Aras bore the name Vanguhi, like the Oxus, but was distinguished from it by the epithet Daitya, making it 'the Vanguhi of the Law' — that is, the Vanguhi beside which Zarathushtra [received his revelation — text truncated].]

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.notes: The supreme deity of Zoroastrianism, creator of the sixteen good lands.

entities_mentioned.name: Spitama Zarathushtra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster, the founder of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called Spitamide; descended from Spitama at the fifth generation. Recipient of Ahura Mazda's revelation.

entities_mentioned.name: Airyana Vaeja (Airyanem Vaeja; Iran-Vej; Eran-Vej)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly the region of Arran (modern Azerbaijan/Armenia border area), identified with the Araxes River valley; alternatively associated historically with the Oxus region
entities_mentioned.notes: The holy primordial land of Zoroastrianism, birthplace of Zarathushtra. Scholarly debate places it either in Eastern Iran (near the Oxus) or, per the Bundahish, bordering Adarbajan, suggesting Arran. The Greek name 'Ariania' (Stephanus Byz.) supports the Arran identification.

entities_mentioned.name: Angra Mainyu
entities_mentioned.notes: The Destructive Spirit in Zoroastrianism, described as 'all death'; counter-creator of evil forces opposing Ahura Mazda's good creations.

entities_mentioned.name: Vanguhi Daitya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aras River (classical Araxes); alternatively identified in Sassanian tradition with the Oxus (Amu Darya)
entities_mentioned.notes: The sacred river of Airyana Vaeja. If Iran-Vej borders Adarbajan/Arran, the Vanguhi Daitya is the Aras/Araxes. In Sassanian times, 'Veh' designated the Oxus.

entities_mentioned.name: Daevas
entities_mentioned.notes: Evil spirits or demons in Zoroastrian cosmology; Winter is described as their work.

entities_mentioned.name: Winter (counter-creation)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a plague counter-created by Angra Mainyu against the first land, Airyana Vaeja; associated with the long, harsh winters of the Arran/Caucasus region.

entities_mentioned.name: Serpent in the river (counter-creation)
entities_mentioned.notes: A demonic or pestilential creature counter-created by Angra Mainyu in the Vanguhi Daitya river as a plague against the first good land.

entities_mentioned.name: Arran
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Historical region corresponding roughly to modern Azerbaijan and parts of Armenia, between the Araxes and Kura rivers
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified by the editor with Airyanem Vaeja on the basis of the Bundahish, climate, and Greek nomenclature (Ariania per Stephanus Byz.).

entities_mentioned.name: Adarbajan (Adarbaijan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Azerbaijan (historical Atropatene/Media Atropatene)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the Bundahish (29.12) as bordering Iran-Vej; used to geographically locate Airyana Vaeja.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly edition of Vendidad (Videvdad) Fargard 1, combining the primary Avestan text in translation with extensive editorial footnotes. The text as supplied is truncated at note 6 mid-sentence ('Zarathushtra r—'). No plant, drug, or medical preparation is described in this passage; the content is cosmological and geographical, concerning the Zoroastrian doctrine of the sixteen good lands and their demonic counter-creations. The segment is therefore not relevant to Materia Medica extraction. The HTML entity '—' has been silently rendered as an em-dash (—), and '§' as the section sign (§), per OCR cleanup rules.

results.title_inferred: Myths of Yima (Jamshed): Fargard 2 of the Vendidad — Yima's Reign and the Expansion of the Earth
results.original_text: Myths of Yima [Jamshed] I. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Who was the first mortal, before myself, Zarathushtra, with whom thou, Ahura Mazda, didst converse, whom thou didst teach the Religion of Ahura, the Religion of Zarathushtra? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: The fair Yima, the good shepherd, O holy Zarathushtra! he was the first mortal, before thee, Zarathushtra, with whom I, Ahura Mazda, did converse, whom I taught the Religion of Ahura, the Religion of Zarathushtra. 3. Unto him, O Zarathushtra, I, Ahura Mazda, spake, saying: 'Well, fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, be thou the preacher and the bearer of my Religion!' And the fair Yima, O Zarathushtra, replied unto me, saying: 'I was not born, I was not taught to be the preacher and the bearer of thy Religion.' 4. Then I, Ahura Mazda, said thus unto him, O Zarathushtra: 'Since thou dost not consent to be the preacher and the bearer of my Religion, then make thou my world increase, make my world grow: consent thou to nourish, to rule, and to watch over my world.' 5. And the fair Yima replied unto me, O Zarathushtra, saying: 'Yes! I will make thy world increase, I will make thy world grow. Yes! I will nourish, and rule, and watch over thy world. There shall be, while I am king, neither cold wind nor hot wind, neither disease nor death.' 6. Then I, Ahura Mazda, brought two implements unto him: a golden seal and a poniard inlaid with gold. Behold, here Yima bears the royal sway! 7. [Obscure.] 8. Thus, under the sway of Yima, three hundred winters passed away, and the earth was replenished with flocks and herds, with men and dogs and birds and with red blazing fires, and there was room no more for flocks, herds, and men. 9. Then I warned the fair Yima, saying: 'O fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, the earth has become full of flocks and herds, of men and dogs and birds and of red blazing fires, and there is room no more for flocks, herds, and men.' 10. Then Yima stepped forward, in light, southwards, on the way of the sun, and (afterwards) he pressed the earth with the golden seal, and bored it with the poniard, speaking thus: 'O Spenta Armaiti, kindly open asunder and stretch thyself afar, to bear flocks and herds and men.'
results.translation:
Myths of Yima [Jamshed], Part I.

§1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Who was the first mortal, before myself, Zarathushtra, with whom thou, Ahura Mazda, didst converse [lit. 'on the Religion,' per the commentary], and whom thou didst teach the Religion of Ahura — the Religion of Zarathushtra?'

§2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The fair Yima [Jamshed], the good shepherd [the commentary explains: 'his being a good shepherd means that he held in good condition herds of men and herds of animals'], O holy Zarathushtra! He was the first mortal, before thee, Zarathushtra, with whom I, Ahura Mazda, did converse, and whom I taught the Religion of Ahura — the Religion of Zarathushtra.'

§3. Unto him, O Zarathushtra, I, Ahura Mazda, spoke, saying: 'Well, fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, be thou the preacher and the bearer of my Religion!' And the fair Yima, O Zarathushtra, replied unto me, saying: 'I was not born, I was not taught to be the preacher and the bearer of thy Religion.'

§4. Then I, Ahura Mazda, said thus unto him, O Zarathushtra: 'Since thou dost not consent to be the preacher and the bearer of my Religion, then make thou my world increase, make my world grow: consent thou to nourish, to rule, and to watch over my world.'

§5. And the fair Yima replied unto me, O Zarathushtra, saying: 'Yes! I will make thy world increase, I will make thy world grow. Yes! I will nourish, and rule, and watch over thy world. There shall be, while I am king, neither cold wind nor hot wind, neither disease nor death.'

§6. Then I, Ahura Mazda, brought two implements unto him: a golden seal [signet ring] and a poniard [dagger] inlaid with gold [these served as the symbol and instrument of sovereignty; the commentary notes: 'He reigned supreme by the strength of the ring and of the poniard' (Asp.); compare also Firdausi's account of Faridoon investing Iraj 'with the sword and the seal, the ring and the crown']. Behold, here Yima bears the royal sway!

§7. [ILLEGIBLE: passage marked obscure in source; content unclear or lost]

§8. Thus, under the sway of Yima, three hundred winters passed away, and the earth was replenished with flocks and herds, with men and dogs and birds and with red blazing fires, and there was room no more for flocks, herds, and men.

§9. Then I warned the fair Yima, saying: 'O fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, the earth has become full of flocks and herds, of men and dogs and birds and of red blazing fires, and there is room no more for flocks, herds, and men.'

§10. Then Yima stepped forward, in light, southwards, on the way of the sun, and afterwards he pressed the earth with the golden seal, and bored it with the poniard, speaking thus: 'O Spenta Armaiti [the divine personification of holy devotion/earth], [kindly open asunder and stretch thyself afar, to bear flocks and herds and men — text breaks off].'

entities_mentioned.name: Yima (Jamshed / Jamshid)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yima Xšaēta — legendary first king of Zoroastrian mythology; cognate with Vedic Yama; identified in Persian tradition as Jamshid
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as 'the good shepherd,' son of Vivanghat. He is presented as a primordial ruler who eliminated disease, death, cold, and heat during his reign. Parallel figures appear in Indo-Iranian mythology broadly.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as 'most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world.' The divine interlocutor of Zarathushtra and the grantor of sovereignty to Yima.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra (Zoroaster)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Presents questions to Ahura Mazda; framing device of the Vendidad text.

entities_mentioned.name: Vivanghat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vivaŋhat — mythological father of Yima in Avestan tradition; cognate with Vedic Vivasvant, father of Yama
entities_mentioned.notes: Named only as Yima's father; no further description in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: Spenta Armaiti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spənta Ārmaiti — Zoroastrian Amesha Spenta (Holy Immortal) personifying holy devotion and the earth
entities_mentioned.notes: Invoked by Yima when he presses the earth with the golden seal and poniard, commanding the earth to expand and accommodate more life.

entities_mentioned.name: Golden seal (signet ring)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ritual/royal implement given by Ahura Mazda to Yima as a symbol of sovereignty. Commentary (Asp.) states: 'He reigned supreme by the strength of the ring and of the poniard.' Paralleled in Firdausi's Shahnameh.

entities_mentioned.name: Poniard inlaid with gold
entities_mentioned.notes: Second implement of royal investiture given to Yima; used also physically to bore into the earth and cause it to expand.

entities_mentioned.name: Faridoon (Fereydun)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Frēdōn / Thraētaona — legendary Iranian hero-king of the Shahnameh tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced in a footnote comparing the investiture of Yima with Faridoon's bestowal of 'the sword and the seal, the ring and the crown' upon Iraj, as described by Firdausi.

entities_mentioned.name: Iraj
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iraj — son of Faridoon in Persian legendary tradition (Shahnameh)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only in the comparative footnote regarding royal investiture.

results.translator_notes:
1. This segment is a scholarly translation (with editorial footnotes incorporated as numbered annotations) of Fargard 2 of the Vendidad (Videvdat), a Zoroastrian Avestan text, not a medical or botanical source. No plants, drugs, or medical preparations are described; the Materia Medica extraction fields are therefore not applicable to this passage.
2. The text as supplied breaks off mid-sentence at §10 ('O Spenta Armaiti, kin—'); the full sentence, reconstructable from standard editions of the Vendidad, would read: 'O Spenta Armaiti, kindly open asunder and stretch thyself afar, to bear flocks and herds and men.' This completion has been supplied in brackets in the translation.
3. §7 is explicitly marked 'Obscure' in the source and has no recoverable content in this segment; marked accordingly.
4. Footnote 3 in the source notes that Vd2.6 consists of 'unconnected Avesta quotations' inserted by a commentator, not original text — this is preserved in the translation as a contextual note.
5. The bracketed '[Jamshed]' after 'Yima' in the source is an editorial gloss identifying the Avestan Yima with the Persian/Shahnameh figure Jamshid; retained in translation.
6. OCR error 'not hot wind' corrected to 'nor hot wind' in §5 (unambiguous from context).

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 3: The Earth — Places of Joy and Grief
results.original_text:
The Earth

I. 1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the first place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place whereon one of the faithful steps forward, O Spitama Zarathushtra! with the log in his hand, the Baresma in his hand, the milk in his hand, the mortar in his hand, lifting up his voice in good accord with religion, and beseeching Mithra, the lord of the rolling country-side, and Rama Hvastra.'

Notes:
1. 'The Genius of the Earth' (Comm.)
2. The wood for the fire altar.
3. The Baresma (now called barsom) is a bundle of sacred twigs which the priest holds in his hand while reciting the prayers. (See Vd19.18 seq. and notes.)
4. The so-called jîv or jîvâm, one of the elements of the Haoma sacrifice.
5. The Havana [hawan] or mortar used in crushing the Haoma or Hom.
6. Mithra, the Persian Apollo, sometimes like him identified with the Sun, is invoked here as making the earth fertile. 'Why do not you worship the Sun? King Yazdgard asked the Christians. Is he not the god who lights up with his rays all the world, and through whose warmth the food of men and cattle grows ripe?' (Elisaeus.)
7. The god that gives food its savour: he is an acolyte to Mithra.

2, 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the second place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place whereon one of the faithful erects a house with a priest within, with cattle, with a wife, with children, and good herds within; and wherein afterwards the cattle continue to thrive, virtue to thrive, fodder to thrive, the dog to thrive, the wife to thrive, the child to thrive, the fire to thrive, and every blessing of life to thrive.'

8. With the domestic chaplain (the Panthaki).
9. By the performance of worship.

4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the third place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful sows most corn, grass, and fruit, O Spitama Zarathushtra! where he waters ground that is dry, or drains ground that is too wet.'

10. Under the Achaemanian kings countrymen who brought water to places naturally dry received the usufruct of the ground for five generations (Polybius, X, 28). But for those underground canals (called qanats), which bring water from the mountains all through the Iranian desert, Persia would starve.

5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the fourth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where there is most increase of flocks and herds.'

6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the fifth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where flocks and herds yield most dung.'

II. 7. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the first place where the Earth feels sorest grief?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the neck of Arezura, whe[re...]'
results.translation:
THE EARTH

Part I.

§1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the first place where the Earth [understood as the Genius of the Earth] feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful steps forward, O Spitama Zarathushtra! bearing the sacred log [fuel for the fire altar] in his hand, the Baresma [barsom — a bundle of sacred twigs held during prayer] in his hand, the milk [jîv or jîvâm, an element of the Haoma sacrifice] in his hand, and the mortar [Havana/hawan, used to crush the Haoma plant] in his hand — raising his voice in proper accordance with the religion, and calling upon Mithra [the Persian deity associated with the Sun and fertility of the earth], lord of the broad pasture-lands, and upon Rama Hvastra [the deity who imparts savour to food, attendant to Mithra].'

§§2–3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the second place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful builds a house with a priest [domestic chaplain, the Panthaki] within it, with cattle, with a wife, with children, and with good herds; and where thereafter the cattle continue to thrive, virtue [maintained through the performance of worship] to thrive, fodder to thrive, the dog to thrive, the wife to thrive, the child to thrive, the fire to thrive, and every blessing of life to thrive.'

§4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the third place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful sows the most corn, grass, and fruit, O Spitama Zarathushtra! — where he brings water to dry ground or drains ground that is overly wet.' [Note: Under the Achaemenid kings, peasants who brought water to naturally arid land received the usufruct of that land for five generations (Polybius X.28). The underground canals known as qanats, which carry water from the mountains across the Iranian desert, remain essential to Persian agriculture.]

§5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the fourth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where there is the greatest increase of flocks and herds.'

§6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the fifth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where flocks and herds yield the most dung [used to fertilize the soil].'

Part II.

§7. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the first place where the Earth feels the deepest grief?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the neck of Arezura [a demonic locality, likely a mountain pass associated with evil spirits], where [the text continues beyond this excerpt]...'

entities_mentioned.name: Earth (Genius of the Earth)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Personified earth deity in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: The Avestan text treats the Earth as a sentient spiritual being capable of happiness and grief. Commentators gloss this as 'the Genius of the Earth.'

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: The Wise Lord, creator of the material world, speaker in this catechetical dialogue.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra (Spitama Zarathushtra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster, prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Addressed directly by Ahura Mazda; the 'Spitama' epithet refers to his clan lineage.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma (Barsom)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bundle of sacred twigs (originally from the haoma/ephedra plant or tamarisk) held during Zoroastrian prayer
entities_mentioned.notes: A key liturgical implement; in later practice made of metal rods. Referenced at Vd19.18.

entities_mentioned.name: Haoma (Hom)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra spp. or possibly Peganum harmala; a sacred psychoactive plant used in Zoroastrian and Vedic ritual
entities_mentioned.notes: The jîv or jîvâm (milk mixed with haoma juice) is one element of the Haoma sacrifice. The Havana/hawan mortar is used to crush the plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk (jîv / jîvâm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual dairy element mixed with pressed haoma juice
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the prescribed elements held by the officiating priest during the Haoma sacrifice liturgy.

entities_mentioned.name: Havana (hawan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stone or metal mortar used to crush the haoma plant
entities_mentioned.notes: A specific liturgical implement; crushing haoma in the mortar is a central act of the yasna (sacrifice).

entities_mentioned.name: Mithra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iranian solar and contractual deity; cognate with Roman Mithras and Vedic Mitra
entities_mentioned.notes: Described here as 'lord of the rolling country-side' and invoked for earth's fertility. Identified in footnote with the Persian Apollo and associated with the Sun. King Yazdgard's question to Christians (via Elisaeus) is cited as illustrative.

entities_mentioned.name: Rama Hvastra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minor Zoroastrian yazata (divine being) associated with pleasant pastures and the savour of food
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as an acolyte of Mithra who imparts flavour/savour to food. 'Hvastra' relates to good pasture.

entities_mentioned.name: Panthaki
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian domestic or parish priest
entities_mentioned.notes: The household chaplain whose presence in the home is counted among the blessings that cause the Earth to rejoice.

entities_mentioned.name: Qanats
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Underground irrigation canals (qanat system) of the Iranian plateau
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the translator's note to §4 as the practical correlate of the Zoroastrian religious injunction to bring water to dry ground.

entities_mentioned.name: Arezura
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological demonic locality in Zoroastrian cosmology; possibly associated with a real mountain pass in Central Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the place of the Earth's greatest grief; associated with evil and demonic forces. The text is cut off before the full description. Possibly Mount Arezura mentioned in other Avestan texts as a gathering place of demons.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a catechetical passage from the Vendidad (Videvdad), Fargard 3, structured as a dialogue between Ahura Mazda and Zarathushtra. The text as supplied is a 19th-century scholarly English translation (likely Darmesteter's, from the Sacred Books of the East series) rather than a raw non-English source; it has been reproduced here with light cleanup of HTML entities (e.g., 'î' rendered as 'î'). The passage is not a medical or botanical text per se, but it contains significant references to ritual pharmacobotany: the Haoma plant (likely Ephedra spp.) is explicitly mentioned as a sacrificial substance, and its preparation — crushing in the hawan mortar and mixing with milk (jîvâm) — is described. The dung of flocks and herds is listed among the Earth's blessings, consistent with its use as agricultural fertilizer. The injunction to irrigate dry land and drain wet land has agronomic and public-health implications. The text is truncated at the beginning of Part II, §7 (the Earth's sorrows). No genuinely illegible passages; the truncation at the end is a segment boundary issue, not an OCR failure.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 4: Contracts, Their Types, and Penalties for Breach
results.original_text: Contracts and offenses I. Notes: 1. He that does not restore a loan to the man who lent it, steals the thing and robs the man. This he doeth every day, every night, as long as he keep in his house his neighbour's property, as though it were his own. 1. He is a thief when he takes a view not to restore; he is a robber when, being asked to restore, he answers, 'I will not' (Comm.) 2. Every moment that he holds it unlawfully, he steals it anew. 'The basest thing with Persians is to lie; the next to it is to be in debt, for this reason among many others, that he who is so, must needs sink to lying at last' (Herod. I, 139). The debtor in question is of course the debtor of bad faith, 'he who says to a man, Give me this, I will restore it to thee at the proper time, and he says to himself: I will not restore it' (Comm.) Ia. 2. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How many in number are thy contracts, O Ahura Mazda? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are six in number, O holy Zarathushtra. The first is the word-contract; the second is the hand-contract; the third is the contract to the amount of a sheep; the fourth is the contract to the amount of an ox; the fifth is the contract to the amount of a man [person]; the sixth is the contract to the amount of a field, a field in good land, a fruitful one, in good bearing.' 3. At first view it seems as if the classification were twofold, the contracts being defined in the first two clauses by their mode of being entered into, and in the last four by their amount. Yet it appears from the following clauses that even the word-contract and the hand-contract are indicative of a certain amount, which, however, the commentators did not, or were unable to, determine. 4. The word-contract may be a contract of which the object are words: the contract of jādangōi (ukhdho-vachah), by which one offers to speak and intervene for some one's benefit, or the contract between master and pupil (for teaching the sacred texts). 5. The contract for hiring labour (?). 6. 'Viz. to the amount of 3 istīrs [in weight],' (Comm.) An istīr (stathr) is as much as 4 dirhems (drachmae). 7. 'To the amount of 12 istīrs (=48 dirhems),' (Comm.) 8. 'To the amount of 500 dirhems.' The exact translation would be rather, 'The contract to the amount of a human being' (promise of marriage). 9. 'Upwards of 500 istīrs.' 10. A sort of gloss added to define more accurately the value of the object, and to indicate that it is greater than that of the preceding one. 3. The word-contract is fulfilled by words of mouth. It is cancelled by the hand-contract; he shall give as damages the amount of the hand-contract. 4. The hand-contract is cancelled by the sheep-contract; he shall give as damages the amount of the sheep-contract. The sheep-contract is cancelled by the ox-contract; he shall give as damages the amount of the ox-contract. The ox-contract is cancelled by the man-contract [text truncated].
results.translation:
Contracts and Offenses — Part I.

Note 1: He who does not restore a loan to the man who lent it steals the thing and robs the man. He does this every day and every night, for as long as he keeps his neighbour's property in his house as though it were his own.

[Commentator's gloss:] He is a thief at the moment he decides not to restore it; he becomes a robber when, being asked to restore it, he answers, 'I will not.'

Note 2: Every moment he holds the property unlawfully, he steals it anew. 'The basest thing among Persians is to lie; the next basest is to be in debt, for this reason among many others: that one who is in debt must in the end resort to lying' (Herodotus I, 139). The debtor in question is, of course, the debtor acting in bad faith — 'he who says to a man, Give me this, I will restore it to you at the proper time, while saying to himself: I will not restore it' (Commentator).

Section Ia, Clause 2:
O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How many contracts are there, O Ahura Mazda?

Ahura Mazda answered: There are six in number, O holy Zarathushtra.
The first is the word-contract [ukhdho-vachah].
The second is the hand-contract.
The third is the contract to the value of a sheep.
The fourth is the contract to the value of an ox.
The fifth is the contract to the value of a man [person].
The sixth is the contract to the value of a field — a field in good land, a fruitful one, in good bearing.

Note 3: At first glance the classification appears twofold: the first two contracts are defined by the manner in which they are concluded, while the last four are defined by their monetary value. Yet it emerges from subsequent clauses that even the word-contract and the hand-contract imply a certain monetary amount, which the commentators either did not know or were unable to determine.

Note 4: The word-contract may be a contract whose subject matter consists of words — specifically the contract of jādangōi [ukhdho-vachah], by which one undertakes to speak and to intercede on someone's behalf, or the contract between master and pupil for the teaching of sacred texts.

Note 5: [The hand-contract may be] the contract for hiring labour (?).

Note 6: [The sheep-contract is] 'namely, to the value of 3 istīrs [by weight]' (Commentator). One istīr [stathr] equals 4 dirhems [drachmae].

Note 7: [The ox-contract is] 'to the value of 12 istīrs (= 48 dirhems)' (Commentator).

Note 8: [The man-contract is] 'to the value of 500 dirhems.' A more precise translation would be 'the contract to the value of a human being,' understood as a promise of marriage.

Note 9: [The field-contract is] 'upwards of 500 istīrs.'

Note 10: This is a kind of gloss appended to define the value of the field more precisely and to indicate that it exceeds that of the preceding contract.

Clause 3: The word-contract is fulfilled by spoken words. If it is violated, it is superseded by the hand-contract; the offender shall pay damages equal to the value of the hand-contract.

Clause 4: If the hand-contract is violated, it is superseded by the sheep-contract; the offender shall pay damages equal to the value of the sheep-contract. If the sheep-contract is violated, it is superseded by the ox-contract; the offender shall pay damages equal to the value of the ox-contract. If the ox-contract is violated, it is superseded by the man-contract [text truncated].

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: The creator god in Zoroastrian theology, here portrayed as the lawgiver answering Zarathushtra's questions about contracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster, founder-prophet of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Addressed as 'holy Zarathushtra'; recipient of divine legal instruction in the dialogue format of the Vendidad.

entities_mentioned.name: jādangōi (ukhdho-vachah)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan term for a verbal intercession contract
entities_mentioned.notes: A specific type of word-contract in Zoroastrian law by which one undertakes to speak or intervene on another's behalf; also interpreted as a teaching contract between master and pupil.

entities_mentioned.name: istīr (stathr)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A unit of weight/currency equivalent to 4 dirhems (drachmae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Persian/Avestan monetary and weight unit. Equivalent to the Greek stater. Used here to define the monetary thresholds of the six contract types.

entities_mentioned.name: dirhem
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dirham; a silver coin and unit of weight derived from the Greek drachma
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the commentator's glosses to express the monetary value of contracts (e.g., sheep-contract = 12 istīrs = 48 dirhems; man-contract = 500 dirhems).

entities_mentioned.name: Vendidad
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Videvdad; an Avestan legal and ritual text of the Zoroastrian canon
entities_mentioned.notes: The source text; Fargard 4 deals specifically with contract law and the penalties for breach of contract.

entities_mentioned.name: Herodotus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Herodotus of Halicarnassus, Greek historian (c. 484–425 BCE)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as 'Herod. I, 139' to corroborate the Persian cultural attitude toward debt and lying.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly edition (likely Darmesteter's Sacred Books of the East translation of the Avesta) rather than a raw primary source; it interleaves translated Avestan text with extensive footnotes and Pahlavi commentator glosses. The source text is not a medical or botanical text and contains no materia medica content; it is a legal-theological document concerning contract law in Zoroastrian tradition. results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 5: Purity Laws Concerning Corpse-Defilement by Animals, Water, and Fire
results.original_text: Purity laws I. Notes: 1. There dies a man in the depths of the vale: a bird takes flight from the top of the mountain down into the depths of the vale, and it feeds on the corpse of the dead man there: then, up it flies from the depths of the vale to the top of the mountain: it flies to some one of the trees there, of the hard-wooded or the soft-wooded, and upon that tree it vomits and deposits dung. 2. Now, lo! here is a man coming up from the depths of the vale to the top of the mountain; he comes to the tree whereon the bird is sitting; from that tree he intends to take wood for the fire. He fells the tree, he hews the tree, he splits it into logs, and then he lights it in the fire, the son of Ahura Mazda. What is the penalty he shall pay? 1. For defiling the fire by bringing dead matter into it (see Vd7.25 seq.) contrarily to the rule, 'Put ye only proper and well-examined fuel (in the fire).' For the purification of unclean wood, see Vd7.28 seq. 3. Ahura Mazda answered: 'There is no sin upon a man for any Nasu that has been brought by dogs, by birds, by wolves, by winds, or by flies. 4. 'For were there sin upon a man for any Nasu that might have been brought by dogs, by birds, by wolves, by winds, or by flies, how soon all this material world of mine would be only one Peshotanu, bent on the destruction of righteousness, and whose soul will cry and wail! so numberless are the beings that die upon the face of the earth.' 2. 'People guilty of death [i.e. a mortal sin]' (Comm.) Cf. Yasna 53.9b. 3. After their death, 'When the soul, crying and beaten off, is driven far away from Paradise' (Comm.) This is imitated from the Gathas (Yasna 46.11c; 51.13b; cf. Vd13.8-9). 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Here is a man watering a corn-field. The water streams down the field; it streams again; it streams a third time; and the fourth time, a dog, a fox, or a wolf carries some Nasu into the bed of the stream: what is the penalty that the man shall pay? 4. For defiling the earth and the water: 'If a man wants to irrigate a field, he must first look after the water-channel, whether there is dead matter in it or not. If the water, unknown to him, comes upon a corpse, there is no sin upon him. If he has not looked after the rivulet and the stream, he is unclean' (Saddar 75). 6. [Repeat st. 3.] 7. [Repeat st. 4.] IIa. 8. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Does water kill? Ahura Mazda answered: 'Water kills no man: Asto-vidhotu binds him, and, thus bound, Vayu carries him off; and the flood takes him up, the flood takes him down, the flood throws him ashore; then birds feed upon him. When he goes away, it is by the will of Fate he goes.' 5. Water and fire belong to the holy part of the world, and come from God; how then is it that they kill? 'Let a Gueber light a sacred fire for a hundred years, if he once fall into it, he shall be burnt.' Even the Mobeds, if we may trust Elisaeus, [ILLEGIBLE: text ends mid-sentence]
results.translation:
Purity Laws, Part I.

Section 1. A man dies in the depths of a valley. A bird descends from the top of a mountain into the valley and feeds upon the corpse of the dead man there. It then flies back up from the valley to the mountaintop and alights upon one of the trees there, whether hardwood or softwood, and upon that tree it vomits and deposits its dung.

Section 2. Now, a man comes up from the valley to the mountaintop. He approaches the tree upon which the bird had perched; from that tree he intends to take wood for the sacred fire. He fells the tree, hews it, splits it into logs, and kindles the fire — that fire which is the son of Ahura Mazda. What penalty shall he incur?
[Footnote 1: For defiling the fire by introducing dead matter (Nasu) into it, contrary to the rule: 'Put only proper and carefully examined fuel into the fire.' See Vd. 7.25ff. for the purification of unclean wood: Vd. 7.28ff.]

Section 3. Ahura Mazda answered: 'There is no sin upon a man for any Nasu [corpse-defilement] that has been carried by dogs, birds, wolves, winds, or flies.'

Section 4. 'For if sin were to fall upon a man for any Nasu carried by dogs, birds, wolves, winds, or flies, how quickly would all this material world of mine become nothing but a host of Peshotanu — those bent on the destruction of righteousness, whose souls shall cry and wail after death — so numberless are the creatures that die upon the face of the earth.'
[Footnote 2: 'Peshotanu' means 'people guilty of a mortal sin' according to the Commentary. Cf. Yasna 53.9b.]
[Footnote 3: The crying and wailing occurs after death: 'When the soul, crying and beaten off, is driven far away from Paradise' (Commentary). This passage imitates the Gathas (Yasna 46.11c; 51.13b; cf. Vd. 13.8–9).]

Section 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! A man irrigates a cornfield. The water flows down the field once, a second time, a third time; and on the fourth occasion, a dog, a fox, or a wolf carries some Nasu [corpse-matter] into the bed of the stream. What penalty shall the man incur?
[Footnote 4: For defiling the earth and water. 'If a man wishes to irrigate a field, he must first examine the water-channel to see whether there is dead matter in it or not. If the water reaches a corpse without his knowledge, there is no sin upon him. If he has not inspected the rivulet and the stream, he is unclean' (Saddar 75).]

Section 6. [Repeat Section 3.]

Section 7. [Repeat Section 4.]

Part IIa.

Section 8. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Does water kill? Ahura Mazda answered: 'Water kills no man. Asto-vidhotu [the demon of death] binds him, and, thus bound, Vayu [the divinity of wind/air] carries him off; the flood takes him downstream, the flood carries him up, the flood casts him ashore; then birds feed upon him. When he departs this life, it is by the will of Fate that he goes.'
[Footnote 5: Water and fire belong to the sacred portion of creation and proceed from God; how then can they kill? 'Let a Gueber [Zoroastrian] tend a sacred fire for a hundred years — if he once falls into it, he will be burned.' Even the Mobeds [Zoroastrian priests], if we may trust Elisaeus — [ILLEGIBLE: passage ends abruptly mid-sentence]]

entities_mentioned.name: Nasu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corpse-defilement or death-pollution (ritual concept)
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan technical term for the demonic force of pollution associated with a corpse or dead matter. Central to Zoroastrian purity law.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: The Wise Lord; divine speaker throughout the Vendidad.

entities_mentioned.name: Peshotanu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Category of mortal sinner in Zoroastrian law
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan term; the Commentary glosses it as 'people guilty of death [mortal sin].' Cf. Yasna 53.9b.

entities_mentioned.name: Asto-vidhotu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan demon of death who binds the dying
entities_mentioned.notes: Also spelled Asto-widhatu; the demon who seizes the soul at the moment of death.

entities_mentioned.name: Vayu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan deity/spirit of wind and air; ambivalent figure associated with both life and death
entities_mentioned.notes: In this context Vayu acts as the agent who carries off the soul after Asto-vidhotu has bound it.

entities_mentioned.name: Saddar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saddar Bundehesh or Saddar Nasr, a Zoroastrian religious text in Persian
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited here as Saddar 75 regarding rules of irrigation and corpse-defilement.

entities_mentioned.name: Gueber
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gabr; a term (often used by outsiders) for a Zoroastrian
entities_mentioned.notes: From Persian 'gabr'; used in this footnote in a comparative remark about the sacred fire.

entities_mentioned.name: Mobed

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 6: Purity Laws Concerning Death Pollution of Ground
results.original_text:
Purity laws I. 1. How long shall the piece of ground be fallow whereon dogs or men have died? Ahura Mazda answered: 'A year long shall the piece of ground be fallow whereon dogs or men have died, O holy Zarathustra!

2. 'A year long shall no worshipper of Mazda sow or water that piece of ground whereon dogs or men have died; he may sow as he likes the rest of the ground; he may water it as he likes.' [Cf. Vd7.45 seq.]

3. 'If within the year they shall sow or water the piece of ground whereon dogs or men have died, they are guilty of the sin of "burying the dead" towards the water, towards the earth, and towards the plants.' ['To the water which they pour out, to the earth which they plough, to the plants which they sow' (Comm.)]

4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda shall sow or water, within the year, the piece of ground whereon dogs or men have died, what is the penalty that they shall pay?

5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are Peshotanus: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra, two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana.' ['If they plough and sow it, one tanapuhr; if they pour water on it, one tanapuhr; if they plough, sow, and water it, two tanapuhrs' (Comm.)]

6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda want to till that piece of ground again, to water it, to sow it, and to plough it, what shall they do? [Even when a year's space is past, the ground is not free ipso facto.]

7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall look on the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may be there.'

8. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If they shall not look on the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may be there, what is the penalty that they shall pay?

9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are Peshotanus: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra, two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

II. 10. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw on the ground a bone of a dead dog, or of a dead man, as large as the top joint of the little finger, and if grease or marrow flow from it on to the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

11. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, thirty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

12. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw on the ground a bone of a dead dog, or of a dead man, as large as the top joint of the fore-finger, and if grease or marrow flow from it on to the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

13. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, fifty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

14. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw on the ground a bone of a dead dog, or of a dead man, as large as the top joint of the middle finger, and if grease or marrow flow from it on to the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

15. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Seventy stripes with the Aspahe-astra...'
results.translation:
Purity Laws, Section I.

1. [Zarathustra asks:] How long shall a piece of ground remain fallow upon which dogs or men have died? Ahura Mazda answered: 'O holy Zarathustra, a piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died shall remain fallow for a full year.

2. 'For a full year, no worshipper of Mazda shall sow or irrigate that piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died; the remainder of the ground he may sow and irrigate as he pleases.' [Cross-reference: Vd7.45 seq.]

3. 'If within that year they shall sow or irrigate the piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died, they are guilty of the sin of "burying the dead" against the water, against the earth, and against the plants.' [Commentary explains: 'Against the water which they pour out, against the earth which they plough, against the plants which they sow.']

4. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda shall sow or irrigate, within the year, a piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died, what penalty shall they pay?

5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are [guilty of the sin of] Peshotanu [a mortal sin]: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra [horse-goad], two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana [discipline-rod of Sraosha].' [Commentary: 'If they plough and sow it — one tanapuhr [mortal sin]; if they pour water on it — one tanapuhr; if they plough, sow, and water it — two tanapuhrs.']

6. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda wish to till that piece of ground once more — to irrigate it, to sow it, and to plough it — what shall they do? [Note: Even after a year has elapsed, the ground is not automatically free of pollution.]

7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall examine the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may remain there.'

8. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If they shall fail to examine the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may be there, what penalty shall they pay?

9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are Peshotanus: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra, two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

Section II.

10. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw onto the ground a bone of a dead dog or of a dead man — a bone no larger than the topmost joint of the little finger — and if grease or marrow flows from it onto the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

11. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, thirty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

12. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw onto the ground a bone of a dead dog or of a dead man — a bone no larger than the topmost joint of the forefinger — and if grease or marrow flows from it onto the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

13. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, fifty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

14. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw onto the ground a bone of a dead dog or of a dead man — a bone no larger than the topmost joint of the middle finger — and if grease or marrow flows from it onto the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

15. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Seventy stripes with the Aspahe-astra...' [Text ends here; passage continues beyond the provided excerpt.]

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.notes: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism, the 'Maker of the material world,' who delivers the purity law pronouncements in dialogue with Zarathustra.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathustra
entities_mentioned.notes: The Zoroastrian prophet, addressed as 'holy Zarathustra'; the interlocutor posing questions to Ahura Mazda.

entities_mentioned.name: Peshotanu
entities_mentioned.notes: A category of mortal or capital sin in Avestan law; one who commits a Peshotanu offense is subject to severe corporal punishment. Equivalent in gravity to a 'tanapuhr' (body-forfeiting) sin.

entities_mentioned.name: Aspahe-astra
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'horse-goad'; a specific instrument used for administering corporal punishment under Zoroastrian law.

entities_mentioned.name: Sraosho-charana
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'the rod/discipline of Sraosha [the angel of obedience]'; a second instrument used for corporal punishment in Zoroastrian penal law.

entities_mentioned.name: Tanapuhr
entities_mentioned.notes: A 'body-forfeiting' sin in Zoroastrian law; the gravest category of sin, referenced in the commentary. Discussed further in the Introduction, section V, paragraph 19 of the source edition.

results.translator_notes: This segment is not a materia medica or pharmacological text; it is a ritual purity legal code (the Vendidad, Fargard 6) from the Avestan Zoroastrian scripture. It concerns pollution taboos related to human and animal corpses and the ritual defilement of earth, water, and plants. No plant, drug, or medical preparation is described in the medical sense, though the passage is relevant to ancient concepts of contamination and public health (avoidance of soil and water near sites of death, inspection of ground for biological remains before re-cultivation). The source text contains a minor OCR error ('he fallow' for 'be fallow') corrected silently. The text is cut off mid-sentence at paragraph 15; the full penalty for a middle-finger-sized bone is not provided in this excerpt. Footnote numbering in the original was inconsistent (footnote 2 appears to be used twice); this has been preserved as found. The commentary references are from the Pahlavi commentary tradition.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 7: Purity Laws Concerning Death Pollution and the Druj Nasu
results.original_text: Purity laws I. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! When a man dies, at what moment does the Druj Nasu rush upon him?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Directly after death, as soon as the soul has left the body, O Spitama Zarathushtra! the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon him, from the regions of the north, in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail sticking out, droning without end, and like unto the foulest Khrafstras. 1. Hell lies in the north; see Vd2.10 n.; Vd3.7 n.; Vd19.1; Hadokht Nask, section 3.25; Bundahishn 15.19. 2. Khrafstra is a general denomination for noxious animals. [3. 'On him she stays until the dog has seen the corpse or eaten it up, or until the flesh-eating birds have taken flight towards it. When the dog has seen it or eaten it up, or when the flesh-eating birds have taken flight towards it, then the Druj Nasu rushes away to the regions of the north in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail sticking out, droning without end, and like unto the foulest Khrafstras.'] 3. Until the Sag-did has been performed (see Vd8.16 seq.) 4. The Sag-did may be performed by birds of prey as well as by dogs. The dog smites the Nasu when it brings its muzzle near to the dead, the bird (mountain hawk, sparrow (?), or eagle) when its shadow passes over the body (Comm. ad §2; cf. §29). §3 is from the Vendidad Sada. 4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If the man has been killed by a dog, or by a wolf, or by witchcraft, or by the artifices of hatred, or by falling down a precipice, or by the law, or by calumny, or by the noose, how long after death does the Druj Nasu come and rush upon the dead? 5.
