'By poison' (Comm.) 6. Literally, 'by men;' that is to say, put to death by the community according to law (Comm.) 7. If he has been condemned unjustly. 8. If he has strangled himself. 5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'At the next watch after death, the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon the dead, from the regions of the north, in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail sticking out, droning without end, and like unto the foulest Khrafstras.' 9. The day is divided into five watches or ratu. If the man dies a natural death, the Druj comes directly; if the death be violent and unlooked for, the Druj comes later (as the corruption does not set in so quickly). II. 6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If there be a number of men resting in the same place, on the same carpet, on the same pillows, be there two men near one another, or five, or fifty, or a hundred, close by one another; and of those people one happens to die; how many of them does the Druj Nasu envelope with corruption, infection, and pollution? 10. §§6-9 = Vd5.27-30. 7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'If the dead one be a priest, the Druj Nasu rushes forth, O Spitama Zarathushtra! she goes as far as the eleventh and [TEXT BREAKS OFF]'
results.translation:
Purity Laws, Part I.

§1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! When a man dies, at what moment does the Druj Nasu [the Corpse Demoness] rush upon him?'

§2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Directly after death, as soon as the soul has left the body, O Spitama Zarathushtra! the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon him from the regions of the north — for Hell lies in the north [cf. Vendidad 2.10, 3.7, 19.1; Hadokht Nask 3.25; Bundahishn 15.19] — in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail protruding, droning without end, and resembling the foulest of Khrafstras [Avestan: noxious or harmful animals, a general term for creatures considered impure or demonic].

§3. [From the Vendidad Sada:] Upon him she remains until a dog has seen the corpse or consumed it, or until flesh-eating birds have taken flight toward it. When the dog has seen it or eaten it, or when the flesh-eating birds have flown toward it, then the Druj Nasu retreats to the regions of the north in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail protruding, droning without end, and resembling the foulest of Khrafstras. [Note: This refers to the ritual known as Sag-did, 'dog-sight' (see Vendidad 8.16 and following). The Sag-did may be performed by birds of prey as well as by dogs. The dog dispels the Nasu by bringing its muzzle near the dead; a bird — whether mountain hawk, sparrow (?), or eagle — accomplishes the same when its shadow passes over the body (Commentary ad §2; cf. §29).]

§4. [Zarathushtra asked:] 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man has been killed by a dog, or by a wolf, or by witchcraft, or by the artifices of hatred [i.e., by poison, according to the Commentary], or by falling from a precipice, or by the law [i.e., put to death by the community according to law, literally "by men," per Commentary], or by calumny [i.e., if he has been unjustly condemned], or by the noose [i.e., if he has strangled himself] — how long after death does the Druj Nasu come and rush upon the dead?'

§5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'At the next watch after death [Note: The day is divided into five watches, or ratu. If a man dies a natural death, the Druj comes immediately; if the death is violent and unexpected, the Druj comes later, as physical corruption does not set in so quickly in such cases], the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon the dead from the regions of the north, in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail protruding, droning without end, and resembling the foulest of Khrafstras.'

Part II [cf. Vendidad 5.27–30].

§6. [Zarathushtra asked:] 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a number of men are resting in the same place, on the same carpet, on the same pillows — whether two men near one another, or five, or fifty, or a hundred, lying close together — and one of those men happens to die, how many of them does the Druj Nasu envelop with corruption, infection, and pollution?'

§7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'If the dead one be a priest [athravan], the Druj Nasu rushes forth, O Spitama Zarathushtra! and goes as far as the eleventh and [TEXT BREAKS OFF — segment ends mid-sentence].'

entities_mentioned.name: Druj Nasu
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan demoness of corpse pollution and putrefaction. Not a physical entity but a theological/ritual concept central to Zoroastrian purity law. Her arrival upon a corpse signifies the onset of ritual contamination.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.notes: The supreme deity in Zoroastrianism, here depicted in dialogue with the prophet Zarathushtra.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra (Spitama Zarathushtra)
entities_mentioned.notes: The prophet of Zoroastrianism; Spitama is his clan name.

entities_mentioned.name: Khrafstra
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan term for noxious or harmful animals considered impure or demonic in Zoroastrian cosmology. Used here comparatively to describe the form of the Druj Nasu.

entities_mentioned.name: Sag-did
entities_mentioned.notes: Zoroastrian ritual of 'dog-sight' (from Avestan/Pahlavi: sag = dog, did = sight). A dog or bird of prey is made to look upon or approach the corpse to drive away the Druj Nasu. Described further in Vendidad 8.16 et seq.

entities_mentioned.name: Ratu
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan term for a division of the day; the day is divided into five watches or ratu. Used here to specify the timing of the Druj Nasu's arrival in cases of violent death.

entities_mentioned.name: Vendidad
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Videvdat — a part of the Avesta (Zoroastrian scripture) dealing primarily with purity laws
entities_mentioned.notes: The source text itself; cross-references to other Fargards (chapters) are made throughout.

entities_mentioned.name: Bundahishn
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A Middle Persian Zoroastrian cosmological and encyclopedic text
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited in footnote 1 at section 15.19 in relation to the location of Hell in the north.

entities_mentioned.name: Hadokht Nask
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A lost Avestan Nask (book), partially preserved; deals with the fate of the soul after death
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited at section 3.25 in relation to the northern location of Hell.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly edition with embedded footnotes/commentary rather than a pure primary text. The translation preserves the dialogue format and integrates footnotes parenthetically for clarity. §3 is flagged in the source as deriving from the Vendidad Sada (a version of the Vendidad without commentary). The segment breaks off mid-sentence at §7, indicating truncation of the source extract. No plants, drugs, or medical preparations are described; this text concerns Zoroastrian ritual purity law relating to corpse pollution. The 'artifices of hatred' gloss as 'poison' in the Commentary is notable for its medico-legal implications. OCR entity '§' has been silently corrected to '§' throughout.

results.title_inferred: Funerary Purification Rites, Corpse Disposal, and Sacred Distances (Vendidad, Fargard 9)
results.original_text:
Funerals and purification, unlawful sex

Notes:
1. If a dog or a man die under a hut of wood or a hut of felt, what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?
1. A movable shelter, by contradistinction to a fixed abode, something like the oba of the Tartars, one of those huts made of boards or felt and called thâruma by the Arabs, which served as pavilions for princes as well as tents for nomads.
2. That sort of abode, having only one room, can have no chamber for the dead (Vd5.10).

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall search for a Dakhma, they shall look for a Dakhma all around. If they find it easier to remove the dead, they shall take out the dead, they shall let the house stand, and shall perfume it with Urvasna or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other sweet-smelling plant.

3. If there is a Dakhma in the proximity, they remove the corpse at once. If there is no Dakhma or the season prevents its access, they purify the hut first.
4. 'So, when a dog or a man dies, the first thing to do is to take the corpse out (from the house), and to purify the house, inside and outside, with perfumes burnt on the fire' (Comm.) See Vd11.4.
Urvâsna is the râsan plant, a sort of garlic; Vohû-gaona, Vohû-kereti, and Hadhâ-naêpata are respectively (according to Frâmjî) benzoin, aloe, and pomegranate.

3. 'If they find it easier to remove the house, they shall take away the house, they shall let the dead lie on the spot, and shall perfume the house with Urvasna, or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other sweet-smelling plant.'

II
4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If in the house of a worshipper of Mazda a dog or a man happens to die, and it is raining, or snowing, or blowing, or it is dark, or the day is at its end, when flocks and men lose their way, what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

5. 'No corpse must be taken to the Dakhma when rain is falling, or threatening. If one is overtaken by rain on the way, if there be a place to lay it down, they shall lay it down; if there be none, they must go on and take it to the Dakhma, they must not retrace their steps.... When arrived at the Dakhma, if they find it full of water, they may nevertheless lay down the corpse' (Comm.)
6. If it is the season of rain or snow. See Vd5.10 seq.

5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The place in that house whereof the ground is the cleanest and the driest, and the least passed through by flocks and herds, by the fire of Ahura Mazda, by the consecrated bundles of Baresma, and by the faithful;'

6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful?

7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty paces from the fire; thirty paces from the water; thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma; three paces from the faithful;—

8. 'On that place they shall dig a grave.
results.translation:
FUNERALS AND PURIFICATION (Vendidad, Fargard 9)

[Section I]

Question 1: If a dog or a man dies inside a wooden hut or a felt hut [i.e., a movable, single-room portable shelter — akin to the 'oba' of the Tartars, or the 'thâruma' tent used by Arabs, serving as both a prince's pavilion and a nomad's dwelling; such a structure, having only one room, cannot contain a separate chamber for the dead (cf. Vd. 5.10)] — what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

Answer (Ahura Mazda): They shall seek out a Dakhma [Tower of Silence, Zoroastrian funerary exposure structure]; they shall search for a Dakhma in all directions. If they find it easier to remove the dead body, they shall take out the corpse, leave the house standing, and fumigate [purify] it with Urvasna, or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other fragrant [sweet-smelling] plant.

[Commentator's note:] If a Dakhma is nearby, the corpse is to be removed immediately. If no Dakhma exists or the season prevents access to one, the hut itself is purified first. "When a dog or a man dies, the first duty is to take the corpse out of the house and to purify the house, inside and outside, with perfumes burnt upon the fire." (Commentary; see also Vd. 11.4.)

[Botanical identifications per Frâmjî:] Urvâsna = the râsan plant, a species of garlic [Allium sativum or related Allium sp.]; Vohû-gaona = benzoin [Styrax benzoin or related Styrax sp.]; Vohû-kereti = aloe [Aloe vera or related Aloe sp.]; Hadhâ-naêpata = pomegranate [Punica granatum].

Alternate ruling: If they find it easier to remove the house [i.e., to dismantle or relocate the portable shelter] rather than the body, they shall take the house away, leave the corpse in place, and fumigate the house with Urvasna, or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other fragrant plant.

[Section II]

Question 4: O Creator of the material world, Most Holy One! If in the dwelling of a worshipper of Mazda a dog or a man happens to die, and at that time it is raining, or snowing, or stormy [blowing hard], or the night is dark, or the day is drawing to its close — the hour when both flocks and men lose their way — what then shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

[Commentator's note:] No corpse may be carried to the Dakhma while rain is falling or threatening. If those transporting the corpse are overtaken by rain on the road: if there is a suitable place to set the body down, they shall set it down; if there is no such place, they must proceed and deliver it to the Dakhma — they must not turn back. Upon arriving at the Dakhma, even if it is found to be filled with water, they may nonetheless lay down the corpse. [If it is the season of rain or snow, see Vd. 5.10 seq.]

Answer (Ahura Mazda): They shall choose the spot within that house where the ground is the cleanest and the driest, and where it is least frequented by flocks and herds, by the sacred fire of Ahura Mazda, by the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred ritual plant-bundles], and by the faithful [Zoroastrian believers].

Question 6: O Creator of the material world, Most Holy One! How far [must the temporary resting place of the corpse be] from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful?

Answer (Ahura Mazda): Thirty paces from the fire; thirty paces from the water; thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma; three paces from the faithful.

Further ruling (8): On that chosen spot they shall dig a grave [for temporary placement of the body, pending transfer to the Dakhma].

entities_mentioned.name: Urvasna / Urvâsna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium sativum (garlic) or a closely related Allium species; described as 'the râsan plant, a sort of garlic'
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a fumigant/purifying aromatic burnt on fire to cleanse a space where a corpse has been present. Identified as 'râsan,' a type of garlic, per the commentator Frâmjî.

entities_mentioned.name: Vohu-gaona / Vohû-gaona
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Styrax benzoin or related Styrax sp. (benzoin resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as benzoin by Frâmjî. Used as a fumigant for ritual purification after contact with a corpse. Identification is traditional and not independently verified.

entities_mentioned.name: Vohu-kereti / Vohû-kereti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or a related Aloe species (aloe)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as aloe by Frâmjî. Used as a fumigant for ritual purification. Identification is traditional.

entities_mentioned.name: Hadha-naepata / Hadhâ-naêpata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Punica granatum (pomegranate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as pomegranate by Frâmjî. Used as a fumigant for ritual purification. Identification is traditional.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma (Barsom)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sp. or Tamarix sp. (sacred ritual plant bundles used in Zoroastrian liturgy); possibly also Hom/Haoma plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Consecrated bundles of plant twigs used in Zoroastrian ritual. The corpse must be kept at least thirty paces away from Baresma bundles. Not used medicinally in this passage but listed as a ritual-purity reference point.

entities_mentioned.name: Dakhma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tower of Silence (Zoroastrian funerary structure for corpse exposure)
entities_mentioned.notes: The designated place for disposal of the dead in Zoroastrian law. Corpses must be transported here; purification rites apply when this is not immediately possible.

results.translator_notes: This passage is a scholarly annotated edition of the Avestan Vendidad (Videvdat), Fargard 9, combining the primary text with embedded commentarial notes (marked 'Comm.') and footnotes. The source text is not purely primary scripture but an annotated translation (likely Darmesteter's Sacred Books of the East edition). Several HTML entities appear in the original (â, î, û, ê) and have been silently resolved to proper diacritical characters in the cleaned original text. The botanical identifications (garlic, benzoin, aloe, pomegranate) are attributed to the Parsi scholar Frâmjî and are traditional rather than philologically secure; confidence is accordingly rated low for those identifications. The section heading 'unlawful sex' appears in the original label but the translated segment itself does not address that topic — it may refer to content in a different sub-section of Fargard 9 not included here. The term 'Dakhma' is left untranslated in the text per standard scholarly convention, with a parenthetical gloss supplied in translation.

results.title_inferred: The Nine Nights' Barashnum: Ritual Purification Ceremony (Vendidad, Fargard 11)
results.original_text: The Nine Nights' Barashnum. 1a. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! To whom shall they apply here below, who want to cleanse their body defiled by the dead?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'To a pious man, O Spitama Zarathushtra! who knows how to speak, who speaks truth, who has learned the Holy Word [manthra], who is pious, and knows best the rites of cleansing according to the law of Mazda. That man shall fell the trees off the surface of the ground on a space of nine Vibazus square; 3. in that part of the ground where there is least water and where there are fewest trees, the part which is the cleanest and driest, and the least passed through by sheep and oxen, and by the fire of Ahura Mazda, by the consecrated bundles of Baresma, and by the faithful.' 4. How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful? 5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful. 6. Then thou shalt dig a hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. 7. Thou shalt dig a second hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a third hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fourth hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fifth hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a sixth hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come.' 8. How far from one another? 'One pace.' How much is the pace? 'As much as three feet.' 9. 'Then thou shalt dig three holes more, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come.' How far from the former six? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those (three) paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 10. 'Then thou shalt draw a furrow all around with a metal knife.' How far from the holes? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those (three) paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 11. 'Then thou shalt draw twelve furrows; three of which thou shalt draw to surround and divide [from the rest] (the [remainder of text cut off]).'
results.translation: The Nine Nights' Barashnum [ritual purification]. Section 1a, with commentary notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! To whom shall those apply here below who wish to cleanse their body defiled by contact with the dead?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'To a pious man [i.e., a priest], O Spitama Zarathushtra — one who knows how to speak, who speaks truth, who has learned the Holy Word [manthra, the sacred liturgical formula], who is pious, and who knows best the rites of cleansing according to the law of Mazda. That man shall clear the trees from the surface of the ground over a space of nine Vibazus [nine ells, approximately; cf. Vd7.34] square. 3. This shall be in that part of the ground where there is the least water and the fewest trees — the part which is cleanest and driest, and least frequented by sheep and oxen, and [kept distant] from the fire of Ahura Mazda, from the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred twigs used in ritual], and from the faithful.' 4. How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful? 5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful.' 6. 'Then thou shalt dig a hole [intended to receive liquid draining from the body being purified] — two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. [Note: In summer, the air and earth being dry, the hole may be shallower, since it is certain to remain empty enough to contain that liquid.] 7. Thou shalt dig a second hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a third hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fourth hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fifth hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a sixth hole [these six holes are to contain gomez, i.e., consecrated cattle urine; they must be dug from north to south, per the Commentary], two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come.' 8. How far from one another? 'One pace.' How much is a pace? 'As much as three feet.' 9. 'Then thou shalt dig three more holes [to contain water], two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come.' How far from the previous six? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those three paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 10. 'Then thou shalt draw a furrow all around with a metal knife.' How far from the holes? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those three paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 11. 'Then thou shalt draw twelve furrows; three of which thou shalt draw to surround and divide [from the rest] (the [text continues beyond this segment]).'

entities_mentioned.name: Barashnum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian nine-night purification ritual (Bareshnum i no shab)
entities_mentioned.notes: A major Zoroastrian ritual of purification for persons defiled by contact with the dead (nasu). Involves staged washing with gomez (consecrated cattle urine) and water in a specially prepared ritual space.

entities_mentioned.name: Gomez
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Consecrated cattle urine used as a purifying agent in Zoroastrian ritual
entities_mentioned.notes: Used medicinally and ritually as a cleansing and purifying substance throughout Zoroastrian tradition. Contained in the first six holes dug during the Barashnum ritual.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma (Baresma bundles)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Barsom — sacred twigs or rods used in Zoroastrian liturgy
entities_mentioned.notes: Consecrated bundles of twigs (traditionally from the pomegranate or date palm) held during prayers; here used as a ritual boundary marker whose proximity must be maintained at thirty paces from the purification site.

entities_mentioned.name: Manthra (Holy Word)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan liturgical formula; cognate with Sanskrit 'mantra'
entities_mentioned.notes: Sacred verbal formula believed to have purificatory and protective power; knowledge of it is a prerequisite for the officiating priest.

entities_mentioned.name: Vibazu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan unit of linear measurement, possibly equivalent to an ell (approx. 45–50 cm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Precise modern equivalent uncertain; the text glosses it tentatively as 'ells' and cross-references Vd7.34.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: The divine source of the purification instructions relayed to Zarathushtra (Zoroaster).

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra (Spitama Zarathushtra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster, prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Recipient of divine revelation in the Vendidad's question-and-answer format.

results.translator_notes: This segment is already in English (translated from the Avestan Vendidad, Fargard 11), likely reflecting James Darmesteter's 19th-century translation with editorial notes interpolated into the main text. The translation here preserves the original English rendering while clarifying the structure and integrating the footnotes inline for scholarly legibility. The segment is cut off mid-sentence at clause 11; the full text of that clause is not present in the supplied source. The ritual described — the Barashnum — is a medico-religious purification protocol: the 'defilement by the dead' (Avestan: nasu) was understood as both spiritual and physical contamination, and the prescribed procedure (site preparation, hole-digging for drainage of washing fluids, ritual boundaries drawn with a metal knife, spatial distances from fire/water/baresma/people) constitutes a systematic quarantine and cleansing procedure with notable parallels to hygienic isolation practices.

results.title_inferred: Formulaic recitations for ritual purification against corpse-defilement (Vendidad, Fargard 12)
results.original_text: Formulas recited during the process of cleansing. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I fight against that Druj who from the dead rushes upon the living? How shall I fight against that Druj who from the dead defiles the living?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Say aloud those words in the Gathas that are to be said twice [Bish-âmrûta]. Say aloud those words in the Gathas that are to be said thrice [Thrish-âmrûta]. Say aloud those words in the Gathas that are to be said four times [Chathrush-âmrûta].' 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those words in the Gathas that are to be said twice? 4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the words in the Gathas that are to be said twice, and thou shalt twice say them aloud: ahya yasa ... urvanem (Y28.2). humatenam ... mahi (Y35.2), ashahya aad saire ... ahubya (Y35.8), yatha tu i ... ahura (Y39.4), humaim thwa ... hudaustema (Y41.3), thwoi staotaraska ... ahura (Y41.5). usta ahmai ... manangho (Y43.1), spenta mainyu ... ahuro (Y47.1), vohu khshathrem ... vareshane (Y51.1), vahista istis ... skyaothanaka (Y53.1).' [The Bish-âmrûta are the opening stanzas of the five Gathas and five stanzas in the Yasna Haptanghaiti.] 5. 'And after thou hast twice said those Bis-amrutas, thou shalt say aloud these victorious, most healing words: "I drive away Angra Mainyu from this house, from this borough, from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness. 6. I drive away the Nasu, I drive away direct defilement, I drive away indirect defilement, from this house, from this borough, from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness."' 7. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those words in the Gathas that are to be said thrice? 8. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the words in the Gathas that are to be said thrice, and thou shalt thrice say them aloud: ashem vohu ... (Y27.14), ye sevisto ... paiti (Y33.11), hukhshathrotemai ... vahistai (Y35.5), duzvarenais ... vahyo (Y53.9).' 9. 'After thou hast thrice said those Thris-amrutas, thou shalt say aloud these victorious, most healing words: "I drive away Indra, I drive away Sauru, I drive away the daeva Naunghaithya, from this...[text breaks off]"'
results.translation:
Formulas recited during the process of ritual cleansing.

1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I fight against that Druj [Demon of Corruption] who from the dead rushes upon the living? How shall I fight against that Druj who from the dead defiles the living?'

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Recite aloud those passages of the Gathas [sacred hymns] that are to be said twice [called the Bish-âmrûta, "twice-uttered"]. Recite aloud those passages of the Gathas that are to be said thrice [the Thrish-âmrûta, "thrice-uttered"]. Recite aloud those passages of the Gathas that are to be said four times [the Chathrush-âmrûta, "four-times-uttered"].'

3. 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those passages of the Gathas that are to be said twice?'

4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the passages of the Gathas to be said twice, and thou shalt recite them aloud twice: ahya yasa ... urvanem (Yasna 28.2); humatenam ... mahi (Y35.2), ashahya aad saire ... ahubya (Y35.8), yatha tu i ... ahura (Y39.4), humaim thwa ... hudaustema (Y41.3), thwoi staotaraska ... ahura (Y41.5); usta ahmai ... manangho (Y43.1), spenta mainyu ... ahuro (Y47.1), vohu khshathrem ... vareshane (Y51.1), vahista istis ... skyaothanaka (Y53.1).' [Note: The Bish-âmrûta are the opening stanzas of each of the five Gathas together with five stanzas of the Yasna Haptanghaiti.]

5. 'And after thou hast twice recited those Bis-amrutas, thou shalt proclaim aloud these victorious, supremely healing words: "I drive away Angra Mainyu [the Destructive Spirit, chief of demons] from this house, from this borough [village], from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness.

6. I drive away the Nasu [the demon of corpse-defilement], I drive away direct defilement, I drive away indirect defilement, from this house, from this borough, from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness."'

7. 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those passages of the Gathas that are to be said thrice?'

8. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the passages of the Gathas to be said thrice, and thou shalt recite them aloud three times: ashem vohu ... (Y27.14), ye sevisto ... paiti (Y33.11), hukhshathrotemai ... vahistai (Y35.5), duzvarenais ... vahyo (Y53.9).'

9. 'After thou hast thrice recited those Thris-amrutas, thou shalt proclaim aloud these victorious, supremely healing words: "I drive away Indra [the daeva], I drive away Sauru [the daeva], I drive away the daeva Naunghaithya, from this... [text breaks off]"'

entities_mentioned.name: Druj
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan demonic principle of deception and corruption; here specifically the demon of corpse-pollution
entities_mentioned.notes: The Druj in this context is the spirit of ritual impurity that transfers from the dead to the living. Closely related to, and often identified with, the Nasu.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster, the prophet-founder of Zoroastrianism

entities_mentioned.name: Angra Mainyu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ahriman; the chief destructive/evil spirit in Zoroastrian cosmology, described here as the Daeva of Daevas
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly glossed in the source notes as 'the chief demon, the Daeva of the Daevas.'

entities_mentioned.name: Nasu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The corpse-demon (Avestan: Nasush); a fly-like demon believed to rush into a corpse at the moment of death and transmit ritual pollution to the living
entities_mentioned.notes: Source note glosses this as 'the very demon with whom one has to do in the present case,' i.e., the immediate agent of corpse-defilement.

entities_mentioned.name: Bish-âmrûta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual term for Gathic stanzas recited twice during purification
entities_mentioned.notes: From Avestan/Pahlavi meaning 'twice-uttered.' Specifically the opening stanzas of the five Gathas and five stanzas of the Yasna Haptanghaiti.

entities_mentioned.name: Thrish-âmrûta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual term for Gathic stanzas recited three times during purification
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'thrice-uttered.'

entities_mentioned.name: Chathrush-âmrûta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual term for Gathic stanzas recited four times during purification
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'four-times-uttered.'

entities_mentioned.name: Indra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: In Zoroastrianism, a daeva (demon) of apostasy; distinct from the Vedic deity Indra
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed here as one of the daevas to be ritually expelled during purification.

entities_mentioned.name: Sauru
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan daeva corresponding to misrule or anarchy; sometimes associated with the demon Shaurva
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed alongside Indra and Naunghaithya as daevas to be expelled.

entities_mentioned.name: Naunghaithya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan daeva of discontent or ingratitude; cognate with Vedic Nâsatya
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three daevas named in the expulsion formula of section 9.

entities_mentioned.name: Yasna Haptanghaiti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan liturgical text; the 'Worship in Seven Chapters' (Yasna 35–41)
entities_mentioned.notes: Five stanzas from this text are included among the Bish-âmrûta.

entities_mentioned.name: Gathas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The oldest sacred hymns of the Avesta, attributed to Zarathushtra himself
entities_mentioned.notes: The Bish-âmrûta, Thrish-âmrûta, and Chathrush-âmrûta are all drawn from these hymns.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from the Vendidad (Videvdat), Fargard 12, a Zoroastrian priestly law text dealing primarily with ritual purity and purification from corpse-defilement. The segment describes a dialogue between Zarathushtra and Ahura Mazda in which specific Gathic stanzas are prescribed as verbal formulae for exorcising demonic pollution (druj/nasu) contracted through contact with the dead. The 'preparation method' in medical/ritual terms is entirely verbal and liturgical: the recitation of specific sacred stanzas a prescribed number of times (twice, three times, or four times), followed by standardized expulsion formulae naming specific daevas. The HTML entity 'â' represents â (a with circumflex) and 'û' represents û (u with circumflex); these have been silently corrected in the cleaned text. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence in section 9, indicating the source text was truncated. This text is not a materia medica source in the pharmacological sense, but represents ritual-medical practice wherein verbal/spiritual means are the sole therapeutic intervention against spiritually transmitted disease (corpse-pollution).

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 13: Special Formulas for Cleansing Persons, Animals, and Natural Elements
results.original_text: Special formulas for cleansing several objects. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! most beneficent spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? how the fire? how the water? how the earth? how the cow? how the tree? how the faithful man and the faithful woman? how the stars? how the moon? how the sun? how the boundless light? how all good things, made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thou shalt chant the cleansing words, and the house shall be clean; clean shall be the fire, clean the water, clean the earth, clean the cow, clean the tree, clean the faithful man and the faithful woman, clean the stars, clean the moon, clean the sun, clean the boundless light, clean all good things, made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle. 3. [So thou shalt say these victorious, most healing words]; thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya five times: "The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," &c. 'The Ahuna-Vairya preserves the person of man: '"Yatha ahu vairyo: - The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," &c. '"Kem-na mazda: - What protector hast thou given unto me, O Mazda! while the hate of the wicked encompasses me?" &c. '"Ke verethrem-ja: - Who is the victorious who will protect thy teaching?" &c. '"Keep us from our hater, O Mazda and Armaiti Spenta!" &c. [Note 1: As in Vd. 8.19, 20.] 4. 'If thou wantest to cleanse the house, say these words aloud: "As long as the sickness lasts my great protector [is he who teaches virtue to the perverse]." [Note 2: Yasna 49.1. The allusion is not quite clear. This line was recited by the Genius of the sky at the moment when Ahriman was invading the sky (Gr. Bd.). Perhaps the small house of man is compared here with that large house, the world.] 'If thou wantest to cleanse the fire, say these words aloud: "Thy fire, first of all, do we approach with worship, O Ahura Mazda!" [Note 3: Yasna 36.1.] 5. 'If thou wantest to cleanse the water, say these words aloud: "Waters we worship, the Maekainti waters, the Hebvainti waters, the Fravazah waters." [Note 4: Yasna 38.3.] 'If thou wantest to cleanse the earth, say these words aloud: "This earth we worship, this earth with the women, this earth which bears us and those women who are thine, O Ahura!" [Note 5: Yasna 38.1. 'Who are thine,' that is, 'who are thy wives.'] 6. 'If thou wantest to cleanse the cow, say these words aloud: "The best of all works we will fulfil while we order both the learned and the unlearned, both masters and servants to secure for the cattle a good resting-place and fodder." [Note 6: Yasna 35.4. 'Let those excellent deeds be done for the behoof of cattle, that is to say, let stables be made, and water...'] 'If thou wantest to cleanse the trees, say these words aloud: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up."
results.translation: Special Formulas for Cleansing Several Objects. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! Most beneficent spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How the fire? How the water? How the earth? How the cow? How the tree? How the faithful man and the faithful woman? How the stars? How the moon? How the sun? How the boundless light? How all good things made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thou shalt chant the cleansing words, and the house shall be clean; clean shall be the fire, clean the water, clean the earth, clean the cow, clean the tree, clean the faithful man and the faithful woman, clean the stars, clean the moon, clean the sun, clean the boundless light, clean all good things made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle. 3. [So thou shalt say these victorious, most healing words]; thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya five times: "The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," etc. The Ahuna-Vairya preserves the person of man: "Yatha ahu vairyo: The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," etc. "Kem-na mazda: What protector hast thou given unto me, O Mazda, while the hate of the wicked encompasses me?" etc. "Ke verethrem-ja: Who is the victorious one who will protect thy teaching?" etc. "Keep us from our hater, O Mazda and Armaiti Spenta!" etc. [As in Vendidad 8.19–20.] 4. If thou wouldst cleanse the house, say these words aloud: "As long as the sickness lasts, my great protector is he who teaches virtue to the perverse." [Yasna 49.1. The allusion is not entirely clear. This line was recited by the Genius of the sky at the moment when Ahriman was invading the sky (Greater Bundahishn). Perhaps the small house of man is here compared to that great house, the world.] If thou wouldst cleanse the fire, say these words aloud: "Thy fire, first of all, do we approach with worship, O Ahura Mazda!" [Yasna 36.1.] 5. If thou wouldst cleanse the water, say these words aloud: "Waters we worship — the Maekainti waters, the Hebvainti waters, the Fravazah waters." [Yasna 38.3.] If thou wouldst cleanse the earth, say these words aloud: "This earth we worship — this earth with the women; this earth which bears us and those women who are thine, O Ahura!" [Yasna 38.1. 'Who are thine' means 'who are thy wives.'] 6. If thou wouldst cleanse the cow, say these words aloud: "The best of all works we will fulfil while we direct both the learned and the unlearned, both masters and servants, to secure for the cattle a good resting-place and fodder." [Yasna 35.4. Note: Let those excellent deeds be done for the benefit of cattle — that is, let stables be built and water provided.] If thou wouldst cleanse the trees, say these words aloud: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up."

entities_mentioned.name: Ahuna-Vairya (Yatha ahu vairyo)
entities_mentioned.notes: The most sacred Zoroastrian prayer formula, described here as a healing and cleansing recitation to be chanted five times. Transliterated Avestan: 'Yatha ahu vairyo.' Cited as preserving 'the person of man.'

entities_mentioned.name: Kem-na mazda
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan prayer formula from the Zoroastrian liturgy, referenced as a protective invocation against enemies.

entities_mentioned.name: Ke verethrem-ja
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan prayer formula invoking the victorious protector of Ahura Mazda's teaching.

entities_mentioned.name: Armaiti Spenta
entities_mentioned.notes: Holy Armaiti, one of the Amesha Spentas (Beneficent Immortals) in Zoroastrianism, associated with devotion and the earth. Invoked alongside Ahura Mazda as a protector.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahriman
entities_mentioned.notes: The Zoroastrian principle of evil and destruction; referenced in the commentary as the adversary invading the sky, cited from the Greater Bundahishn (Gr. Bd.).

entities_mentioned.name: Maekainti waters
entities_mentioned.notes: Named sacred waters invoked in the Yasna 38.3 purification formula for water; geographic or mythological identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Hebvainti waters
entities_mentioned.notes: Named sacred waters invoked in the Yasna 38.3 purification formula for water; geographic or mythological identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Fravazah waters
entities_mentioned.notes: Named sacred waters invoked in the Yasna 38.3 purification formula for water; geographic or mythological identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: cow (gāv)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bos taurus
entities_mentioned.notes: The domestic cow held sacred in Zoroastrian tradition. Specific Yasna formula (35.4) prescribed for its ritual purification, with explicit mention of providing stables and fodder.

entities_mentioned.name: plants/trees
entities_mentioned.notes: Trees and plants are listed among the objects requiring ritual purification; a specific recitation from the Yasna is prescribed for their cleansing.

entities_mentioned.name: fire
entities_mentioned.notes: Sacred fire in Zoroastrianism; purified by recitation of Yasna 36.1 ('Thy fire, first of all, do we approach with worship, O Ahura Mazda!').

results.translator_notes: This segment is a liturgical-ritual text from the Vendidad (Videvdat), Fargard 13, a key Zoroastrian purity code. The text is not a pharmacopoeia or medical treatise in the conventional sense, but it does present ritual purification formulas ('cleansing words') for a broad array of objects including living beings, animals, natural elements, and cosmological entities. The 'healing' aspect (para. 3 refers to 'most healing words') is ritual-verbal rather than pharmaceutical. No plant preparations, drug compounds, or physical medical interventions are described. Extraction fields for medical entities are therefore largely inapplicable to this segment. Source references (Yasna chapters and verses, Greater Bundahishn) are preserved from the original scholarly edition's footnotes and integrated into the cleaned text in brackets. The segment appears to be cut off mid-sentence at the end ('let stables be made, and water a[...])'; this has been flagged in the original_text as incomplete.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 14: The Upaman — Mourning Periods and Purification Rites After Death
results.original_text: The Upaman, how long it lasts for different relatives. 1. If one's father or mother dies, how long shall they stay [in mourning], the son for his father, the daughter for her mother? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall stay thirty days for the righteous, sixty days for the sinners.' Notes: 1. How long if the dead person died in a state of holiness (a dahma)? How long if in the state of a Peshotanu [mortal sin]? 2. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be clean again? Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times, you shall wash your clothes three times, you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire, you shall bind the bundles of Baresma, you shall bring libations to the good waters; then the house shall be clean, and then the waters may enter, then the fire may enter, and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!' 2. This refers probably to the sacrifice that is offered on each of the three days that follow the death of a Zoroastrian for the salvation of his soul. 3. All the other objects over which the Amesha-Spentas preside (such as the cow, the metals, &c.) 3. If one's son or daughter dies, how long shall they stay, the father for his son, the mother for her daughter? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall stay thirty days for the righteous, sixty days for the sinners.' 4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be clean again? Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times, you shall wash your clothes three times, you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire, you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma, you shall bring libations to the good waters; then the house shall be clean, and then the waters may enter, then the fire may enter, and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!' 5. If one's brother or sister dies, how long shall they stay, the brother for his brother, the sister for her sister? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall stay thirty days for the righteous, sixty days for the sinners.' 6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be clean again? Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times, you shall wash your clothes three times, you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire, you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma, you shall bring libations to the good waters; then the house shall be clean, and then the waters may enter, then the fire may enter, and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!' 7. If the master of the house dies, or if the mistress of the house dies, how long shall they stay? How long for the righteo[us? — text truncated]
results.translation:
The Upaman [mourning period], and how long it lasts for different relatives.

§1. If one's father or mother dies, how long shall they [the survivors] remain [in mourning] — the son for his father, the daughter for her mother? How long [if the deceased was] righteous? How long [if the deceased was] a sinner?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall remain thirty days for the righteous, and sixty days for the sinners.'

[Annotator's note 1: 'How long if the dead person died in a state of holiness (a dahma [state of ritual purity])? How long if in the state of a Peshotanu [one guilty of mortal sin]?']

§2. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be made clean again?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times; you shall wash your clothes three times; you shall chant the Gathas [sacred hymns] three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire; you shall bind the bundles of Baresma [sacred barsom twigs]; you shall bring libations to the good waters. Then the house shall be clean; then the waters may enter; then the fire may enter; and then the Amesha-Spentas [Holy Immortals] may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!'

[Annotator's note 2: This refers probably to the sacrifice offered on each of the three days following the death of a Zoroastrian, for the salvation of his soul.]

[Annotator's note 3: 'All the other objects over which the Amesha-Spentas preside (such as the cow, the metals, etc.).']

§3. If one's son or daughter dies, how long shall they remain — the father for his son, the mother for her daughter? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall remain thirty days for the righteous, and sixty days for the sinners.'

§4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be made clean again?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times; you shall wash your clothes three times; you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire; you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma; you shall bring libations to the good waters. Then the house shall be clean; then the waters may enter; then the fire may enter; and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!'

§5. If one's brother or sister dies, how long shall they remain — the brother for his brother, the sister for her sister? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall remain thirty days for the righteous, and sixty days for the sinners.'

§6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be made clean again?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times; you shall wash your clothes three times; you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire; you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma; you shall bring libations to the good waters. Then the house shall be clean; then the waters may enter; then the fire may enter; and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!'

§7. If the master of the house dies, or if the mistress of the house dies, how long shall they remain? How long for the righteo[us? — text truncated]

entities_mentioned.name: Upaman
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian mourning period (ritual impurity period following a death in the household)
entities_mentioned.notes: An Avestan term referring to the prescribed period of mourning and ritual pollution observed by relatives of the deceased. Duration varies by the moral status of the deceased and the degree of kinship.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 'Maker of the material world' and 'thou Holy One' in this passage. The divine interlocutor responding to Zarathushtra's questions.

entities_mentioned.name: Gathas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sacred hymns composed by Zarathushtra; oldest texts of the Avesta
entities_mentioned.notes: Chanting the Gathas three times is prescribed as part of the purification rite for a polluted household.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma (Baresma bundles / barsom)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Barsom — bundles of sacred twigs (traditionally from the pomegranate, tamarisk, or date palm) used in Zoroastrian ritual
entities_mentioned.notes: Binding the Baresma is a required ritual act in the household purification ceremony described here. Modern barsom is often made of metal wires.

entities_mentioned.name: Amesha-Spentas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Holy Immortals — six (or seven) divine emanations or archangels in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Their re-entry into the purified house signals the completion of cleansing. The annotator notes they preside over specific material creations (e.g., cattle, metals).

entities_mentioned.name: Spitama Zarathushtra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster (Zarathustra) — the prophet of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: The questioner in this dialogic text; 'Spitama' is his clan or family name.

entities_mentioned.name: Dahma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: State of ritual holiness or purity in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the annotator's note to describe a deceased person who died in a state of sanctity, as opposed to one who died in a state of Peshotanu (mortal sin).

entities_mentioned.name: Peshotanu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian legal/theological category: one guilty of mortal or grievous sin
entities_mentioned.notes: Annotator gloss in the source text defines it as 'mortal sin.' The mourning period for a Peshotanu is sixty days, double that for the righteous.

entities_mentioned.name: Fire (Ahura Mazda's Fire)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sacred fire maintained in Zoroastrian ritual; the atar or sacred flame
entities_mentioned.notes: Sacrifice is offered to the sacred fire as part of the household purification rite.

entities_mentioned.name: Good waters (libations to)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual libations offered to water as a sacred element in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Water is one of the sacred creations presided over by an Amesha-Spenta. Libations are poured as part of the purification sequence.

results.translator_notes:
1. This segment is a translated English-language scholarly edition of Vendidad (Videvdad) Fargard 14, not itself a non-English source text; the source appears to be James Darmesteter's 19th-century translation of the Avesta (Sacred Books of the East series), with his own footnotes embedded inline. The 'translation' provided here is therefore a re-presentation and clarification of that existing English text with structural annotation. 
2. The text is truncated at §7 mid-sentence ('How long for the righteo[us]') — this is an artefact of the source segment boundary, not a lacuna in the original. 
3. The annotator's note '[mortal sin -JHP]' uses initials 'JHP' which do not correspond to Darmesteter; this may indicate a subsequent editor or annotator of the edition used. 
4. This segment is of limited direct relevance to Materia Medica in the pharmacological sense, but is highly relevant to ritual purification practices — specifically, bodily washing, textile washing, fire sacrifice, use of sacred plant bundles (Baresma/barsom), and water libations as therapeutic/purificatory responses to ritual pollution (death contamination). The Baresma bundle is the principal botanical entity described.

results.title_inferred: The Hedgehog and Tortoise in Zoroastrian Law: Sacred and Accursed Creatures (Vendidad, Fargard 15)
results.original_text: The dog. Ia. Notes: 1. Which is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The dog with the prickly back, with the long and thin muzzle, the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka; this is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu] that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit.' 1. The hedgehog, according to the Bund. 19.28, is created in opposition to the ant that carries off grain, as it says that the hedgehog, every time that it voids urine into an ant's nest, will destroy a thousand ants (Bund. 19.28; cf. Saddar 57). When the Arabs conquered Saistan, the inhabitants submitted on the condition that hedgehogs should not be killed nor hunted for, as they got rid of the vipers which swarm in that country. Every house had its hedgehog (Yaqout, Dictionnaire de la Perse, p. 303). Plutarch counts the hedgehog amongst the animals sacred to the Magi (Quaestiones Conviviales, IV, 5, 2). 2. Dusaka is the popular name of the hedgehog (Pers. zuza). It is not without importance which name is given to a being: 'When called by its high name, it is powerful' (Comm.); cf. § 6, and Vd18.15. 3. 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra! shall kill the dog with the prickly back, with the long and thin muzzle, the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka, kills his own soul for nine generations, nor shall he find a way over the Chinwad bridge, unless he has, while alive, atoned for his sin.' 3. The bridge leading to Paradise; see Vd19.30. 4. Cf. § 54. Framji translates: 'He cannot atone for it in his life even by performing a sacrifice to Sraosha' (cf. Vd9.56, text and note). 4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man kill the dog with the prickly back, with the long and thin muzzle, the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka, what is the penalty that he shall pay? Ahura Mazda answered: 'A thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra, a thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana.' Ib. 5. Which is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu]? 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The daeva Zairimyangura, which evil-speaking people call the Zairimyaka, this is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu].' 5. The tortoise (Framji and Rivayats). 6. 'When not so called it is less strong' (Comm.) Zairimyaka is a lucky name, and means, as it seems, who lives in verdure; Zairimyangura seems to mean 'the verdure-devourer.' 7. 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra! shall kill the daeva Zairimya[ngura]...'
results.translation: The Dog [hedgehog]. Section Ia. Notes: 1. [Zarathushtra asks:] Which is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu] that, from midnight until sunrise, goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The dog with the prickly back and the long, thin muzzle — the dog Vanghapara [i.e., the hedgehog], which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka — this is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit that from midnight until sunrise goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit.' [Translator's note 1:] The hedgehog. According to the Bundahishn 19.28, the hedgehog was created in opposition to the ant that carries off grain; that text states that the hedgehog, every time it voids urine into an ant's nest, will destroy a thousand ants (Bund. 19.28; cf. Saddar 57). When the Arabs conquered Saistan [Sistan], the inhabitants submitted on the condition that hedgehogs should not be killed or hunted, since these animals rid the country of the vipers that swarm there. Every household kept its own hedgehog (Yaqout, Dictionnaire de la Perse, p. 303). Plutarch numbers the hedgehog among the animals sacred to the Magi (Quaestiones Conviviales, IV, 5, 2). [Translator's note 2:] 'Duzaka' [also spelled Dusaka] is the common popular name of the hedgehog (Persian: zuza). The name given to a being is not without consequence: 'When called by its high name, it is powerful' (Commentary); cf. § 6 and Vd18.15. 3. [The text continues:] 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra, shall kill the dog with the prickly back and the long, thin muzzle — the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka — kills his own soul for nine generations, and shall find no passage over the Chinwad bridge [the bridge leading to Paradise; see Vd19.30], unless he has made atonement for his sin during his lifetime.' [Translator's note 4:] Cf. § 54. Framji translates: 'He cannot atone for it in his lifetime even by performing a sacrifice to Sraosha' (cf. Vd9.56, text and note). 4. [Zarathushtra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man kills the dog with the prickly back and the long, thin muzzle — the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka — what penalty shall he pay? Ahura Mazda answered: 'A thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra [horse-whip], and a thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana [the discipline of Sraosha, i.e., the religious scourge].' Section Ib. 5. [Zarathushtra asks:] Which is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that, from midnight until sunrise, goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu]? 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The daeva [demon] Zairimyangura — which evil-speaking people call the Zairimyaka — this is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that from midnight until sunrise goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit.' [Translator's note 5:] The tortoise (identified as such by Framji and in the Rivayats). [Translator's note 6:] 'When not called by that name, it is less powerful' (Commentary). Zairimyaka is an auspicious name meaning, apparently, 'one who dwells in verdure'; Zairimyangura appears to mean 'the verdure-devourer.' 7. [The text continues:] 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra, shall kill the daeva Zairimya[ngura]...' [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: Vanghapara / Duzaka (Dusaka)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: European hedgehog (Erinaceus europaeus) or related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'dog with the prickly back and long, thin muzzle' in the sacred text. Vanghapara is its ritual high name; Duzaka/Dusaka is the common popular name (Persian: zuza). Sacred to the Magi according to Plutarch. Venerated in Sistan as a destroyer of vipers and ants. Killing this animal incurs severe spiritual and physical penalties.

entities_mentioned.name: Zairimyangura / Zairimyaka
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tortoise (likely Testudo species, common to the Iranian plateau)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as a tortoise by Framji and the Rivayats. Classified as a daeva (demon creature) of the Evil Spirit. Zairimyaka is described as a 'lucky' popular name meaning 'one who lives in verdure'; Zairimyangura is the ritual high name meaning 'verdure-devourer.' The text implies killing this creature is meritorious.

entities_mentioned.name: Aspahe-astra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Horse-whip (ritual scourge)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of two instruments of corporal punishment prescribed for the killing of the hedgehog; literally 'horse-weapon/whip.'

entities_mentioned.name: Sraosho-charana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Religious discipline scourge associated with the yazata Sraosha
entities_mentioned.notes: The second instrument of punishment prescribed; associated with the deity Sraosha (divine Obedience/Discipline).

entities_mentioned.name: Chinwad bridge
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian eschatological Bridge of the Separator (Cinvat Peretu)
entities_mentioned.notes: The bridge traversed by the soul after death leading to Paradise. Referenced in Vd19.30. Killing the hedgehog bars the soul from crossing it unless atonement is made in life.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly annotated translation of Vendidad (Videvdat) Fargard 15, incorporating both the primary Avestan legal text and extensive secondary commentary drawn from the Bundahishn, Saddar, Plutarch, Yaqout, and the Rivayats. The bracketed expansions [Spenta Mainyu] and [JHP] appear to be editorial interpolations by a prior translator (likely James Darmesteter or a subsequent editor using his framework). The text breaks off mid-sentence at §7, suggesting the source excerpt is incomplete. No direct materia medica preparation methods are described; the hedgehog's relevance to this archive is its documented use as a biological pest-control agent (against vipers and ants) in historical Sistan, and the Bundahishn's attribution of a medicinal/destructive urinary property (destruction of ant nests). The tortoise is framed as a pest/evil creature, not a medicinal one.

results.title_inferred: Atonement for the killing of a water-dog (otter): penalties and ritual obligations
results.original_text: Atoning for the murder of a water-dog. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! He who smites one of those water-dogs that are born one from a thousand dogs and a thousand she-dogs, so that he gives up the ghost and the soul parts from the body, what is the penalty that he shall pay?' Notes: 1. See preceding Fargard § 51. 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'He shall pay ten thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra, ten thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana. He shall godly and piously bring unto the fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of hard, well dried, well examined wood, to redeem his own soul. 2. He shall pay 50 tanapuhrs (= 15,000 istirs = 60,000 dirhems). If he can afford it, he will alone in the manner stated in the Avesta; if he cannot afford it, it will be sufficient to perform a complete Izashne [Yasna] (sacrifice).' (Comm.) 3. To the altar of the Warharan fire. 4. It is forbidden to take any ill-smelling thing to the fire and to kindle it thereon; it is forbidden to kindle green wood, and even though the wood were hard and dry, one must examine it three times, lest there may be any hair or any unclean matter upon it. (Gr. Riv.) Although the pious Arda Viraf had always taken the utmost care never to put on the fire any wood but such as was seven years old, yet, when he entered Paradise, Atar, the genius of fire, showed him reproachfully a large tank full of the water which that wood had exuded (see Arda Viraf 10). 3. 'He shall godly and piously bring unto the fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of soft wood, of Urvasna, Vohu-gaona, Vohu-kereti, Hadha-naepata, or any sweet-scented plant, to redeem his own soul.' 5. See above, Vd8.2, note 4. 4. 'He shall godly and piously tie ten thousand bundles of Baresma, to redeem his own soul. He shall offer up to the Good Waters ten thousand Zaothra libations with the Haoma and the milk, cleanly prepared and well strained, cleanly prepared and well strained by a pious man, and mixed with the roots of the tree known as Hadha-naepata, to redeem his own soul. 5. He shall kill ten thousand snakes of those that go upon the belly. He shall kill ten thousand Kahrpus, who are snakes with the shape of a dog. He shall kill ten thousand tortoises. He shall kill ten thousand land-frogs; he shall kill ten thousand water-frogs. He shall kill ten thousand corn-carrying ants; he shall kill ten thousand ants of the small, venomous mischievous kind.' 6. 'Mar banak snakes: they are dog-like, because they sit on their hindparts.' (Comm.) The cat (gurba = Kahrpu) seems to be the animal intended. In a paraphrase of this passage in a Parsi Rivayat, the cat is numbered amongst the Khrafstras which it is enjoined to kill to redeem a sin (India Office Library, VIII, 13); cf. G. du Chinozi, p. 462: 'Les animaux que les Gaures ont en horreur sont les serpents, les couleuvres, les lezars, et aut[res].'
results.translation: Atonement for the killing of a water-dog [otter]. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! He who strikes and kills one of those water-dogs that are born but one in a thousand [ordinary] dogs and a thousand she-dogs, so that it gives up the ghost and the soul departs from the body — what penalty shall he pay?' [Note 1: See the preceding Fargard, §51.] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'He shall receive ten thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra [horse-whip], ten thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana [the instrument of penance associated with the angel Sraosha]. He shall, in a godly and pious manner, bring unto the sacred fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of hard, well-dried, carefully examined wood, to redeem his own soul. [Note 2:] He shall pay fifty tanapuhrs [a major unit of religious penalty] (equivalent to 15,000 istirs, or 60,000 dirhems). If he has the means, he shall perform the atonement alone in the manner prescribed in the Avesta; if he lacks the means, it shall suffice to perform a complete Izashne [Yasna] (sacrifice).' (Commentary.) [Note 3:] This offering is made to the altar of the Warharan [Bahram] fire. [Note 4:] It is forbidden to bring any ill-smelling substance to the sacred fire or to kindle it thereon; it is forbidden to kindle green wood; and even if the wood is hard and dry, it must be examined three times lest any hair or unclean matter adhere to it. (Greater Rivayat.) Although the pious Arda Viraf had always taken the greatest care never to place upon the fire any wood less than seven years old, yet when he entered Paradise, Atar — the divine genius of fire — showed him reproachfully a great tank full of the moisture that had exuded from that wood (see Arda Viraf, chapter 10). 3. 'He shall, in a godly and pious manner, bring unto the fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of soft aromatic wood — of Urvasna [a sweet-scented shrub], Vohu-gaona [lit. 'of good colour'], Vohu-kereti [lit. 'of good cutting'], Hadha-naepata [a plant of the Ephedra or related genus], or any other sweet-scented plant — to redeem his own soul.' [Note 5: See above, Vendidad 8.2, note 4.] 4. 'He shall, in a godly and pious manner, tie ten thousand bundles of Baresma [barsom, the sacred bundle of twigs used in ritual], to redeem his own soul. He shall offer up to the Good Waters ten thousand Zaothra [libation] offerings composed of Haoma [the sacred plant beverage] and milk, cleanly prepared and well strained by a pious man, and mixed with the roots of the plant known as Hadha-naepata, to redeem his own soul. 5. He shall kill ten thousand snakes of those that go upon the belly. He shall kill ten thousand Kahrpus — creatures described as snakes having the form of a dog. He shall kill ten thousand tortoises. He shall kill ten thousand land-frogs; he shall kill ten thousand water-frogs. He shall kill ten thousand grain-carrying ants; he shall kill ten thousand ants of the small, venomous, mischievous kind.' [Note 6:] 'Mar banak snakes: they are called dog-like because they sit up on their haunches.' (Commentary.) The cat (gurba = Kahrpu) seems to be the animal actually intended here. In a paraphrase of this passage in a Parsi Rivayat, the cat is listed among the Khrafstras [noxious creatures] that one is enjoined to kill in order to atone for a sin (India Office Library, MS VIII, 13); cf. G. du Chinozi, p. 462: 'The animals which the Zoroastrians [Guebres] hold in abhorrence are serpents, adders, lizards, and oth[ers].'

entities_mentioned.name: water-dog
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Avestan 'water-dog' (udra) is consistently identified by scholars as the otter, a sacred and protected animal in Zoroastrian law; killing one incurs severe ritual and financial penalties.

entities_mentioned.name: Aspahe-astra
entities_mentioned.notes: A ritual instrument of corporal punishment, literally 'horse-whip' in Avestan; used as a unit of penance.

entities_mentioned.name: Sraosho-charana
entities_mentioned.notes: Another instrument of ritual penance associated with the angel Sraosha (divine Obedience); used alongside the Aspahe-astra in penological contexts in the Vendidad.

entities_mentioned.name: Urvasna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Artemisia or a fragrant shrub; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: A sweet-scented plant used in sacred fire offerings; precise botanical identity debated.

entities_mentioned.name: Vohu-gaona
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan plant name meaning 'of good colour'; botanical identity not established with confidence.

entities_mentioned.name: Vohu-kereti
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan plant name meaning 'of good cutting'; botanical identity not established.

entities_mentioned.name: Hadha-naepata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ephedra (Ephedra sp.) or a related plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in both fire offerings and Zaothra libations mixed with Haoma and milk; several scholars associate it with Ephedra or Haplophyllum; roots specifically used in preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Haoma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ephedra sinica, Sarcostemma sp., or Peganum harmala
entities_mentioned.notes: The sacred ritual plant and pressed beverage of the Avestan/Zoroastrian tradition, cognate with Vedic Soma. Botanical identity remains debated; most current scholarship favors Ephedra.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Barsom — bundles of twigs (historically tamarisk or pomegranate; later metal rods)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ritual bundle of sacred twigs held by priests during Yasna ceremony; tying bundles was itself an act of piety and atonement.

entities_mentioned.name: Kahrpu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Domestic cat (Felis catus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described in the text as a 'snake with the shape of a dog'; the commentary and parallel Parsi Rivayat sources suggest the cat is the intended creature. Cats were classified as Khrafstras (noxious creatures) in some Zoroastrian legal texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Mar banak
entities_mentioned.notes: A snake-type described in the Pahlavi commentary as dog-like due to its posture; possibly a large bodied snake that rears up.

entities_mentioned.name: Atar
entities_mentioned.notes: The Zoroastrian divine genius (yazata) of fire; personification of sacred fire.

entities_mentioned.name: Izashne / Yasna
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal Zoroastrian liturgical sacrifice/ceremony; performing a complete Yasna is offered as an alternative atonement for those who cannot afford the full financial penalty.

results.translator_notes: This segment is from Fargard 16 of the Vendidad (Videvdat), a legal-ritual text of the Zoroastrian Avesta. The source text is an English scholarly translation (likely Darmesteter's, late 19th century) with embedded Pahlavi commentary and Rivayat citations. The HTML entity '§' has been silently corrected to '§'; 'â' and 'â' corrected to 'â' in 'Mâr bânâk'; the truncated final word 'aut' is clearly 'autres' (French: 'others') and has been completed in the translation. The passage treats the otter-killing as a grave religious offence requiring elaborate ritual, financial, and symbolic atonement. The enumeration of creatures to be killed (snakes, tortoises, frogs, ants) reflects Zoroastrian Khrafstra doctrine, in which killing noxious creatures earns religious merit offsetting sin. The embedded French citation from 'G. du Chinozi' refers to a European account of Zoroastrian practice. The note on Arda Viraf and seven-year-old wood is from the Arda Viraf Namag, a Pahlavi visionary text. Currency equivalences (tanapuhr, istir, dirhem) reflect Sasanian-era monetary reckoning preserved in Pahlavi exegesis.

results.title_inferred: Five Sins Rendering a Man Peshotanu: Dogs, Women, and Sexual Transgressions (Vendidad, Fargard 17)
results.original_text: Regarding certain sins and obligations. I. Notes: 1. How many are the sins that men commit and that, being committed and not confessed, nor atoned for, make their committer a Peshotanu? [Note 1: That is to say: he shall receive two hundred strokes with the Aspahe-ashtra or the Sraosho-charana; or pay three hundred istirs.] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'There are five such sins, O holy Zarathushtra! It is the first of these sins that men commit when a man teaches one of the faithful another faith, another law, a lower doctrine, and he leads him astray with a full knowledge and conscience of the sin: the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 2: The Commentary has, 'that is, a creed that is not ours.'] 3. 'It is the second of these sins when a man gives bones too hard or food too hot to a shepherd's dog or to a house-dog; 4. 'If the bones stick in the dog's teeth or stop in his throat; or if the food too hot burn his mouth or his tongue, he may come to grief thereby; if he come to grief thereby, the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 3: He who gives too hot food to a dog so as to burn his throat is margarzan (guilty of death); he who gives bones to a dog so as to tear his throat is margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).] 5. 'It is the third of these sins when a man smites a bitch big with young or affrights her by running after her, or shouting or clapping with the hands; 6. 'If the bitch fall into a hole, or a well, or a precipice, or a river, or a canal, she may come to grief thereby; if she come to grief thereby, the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 4: If a bitch is big with young [pregnant] and a man shouts or throws stones at her, so that the whelps come to mischief and die, he is margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).] 7. 'It is the fourth of these sins when a man has intercourse with a woman who has the whites or sees the blood, the man that has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 5: i.e. during menstruation. See Vd16.14 seq.] 8. 'It is the fifth of these sins when a man has intercourse with a woman quick with child, whether the milk has already come to her breasts or has not yet come: she may come to grief thereby; if she come to grief thereby, the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 6: When she has been pregnant for four months and ten days, as it is then that the child is formed and a soul is added to its body (Anquetil II, 563). Note 7: Or better: 'if the child die.' 'If a man come to his wife [during her pregnancy] so that she is injured and bring forth a still-born child, he is margarzan' (Old Riv. 115b).] IIa. 9.
