'If a man come near unto a damsel, either dependent on the chief of the family or not dependent, either delivered [unto a husband] or not delivered, and she conceives by him, let her not, being ashamed of the people, produce in herself the menses, against the course of nature, by means of water and plants. [Note 8: Whether she has a husband in the house of her own parents or has none; wheth-]
results.translation:
Concerning certain sins and their obligatory penalties. Section I. Question: How many are the sins that men commit which, if committed and neither confessed nor atoned for, render their perpetrator a Peshotanu [a great sinner subject to severe corporal or financial punishment: two hundred strokes with the Aspahe-ashtra [horse-goad] or the Sraosho-charana [instrument of discipline associated with Sraosha], or a fine of three hundred istirs [a unit of currency]]?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'There are five such sins, O holy Zarathushtra!

'The first of these sins is committed when a man teaches one of the faithful another faith, another law, a lower doctrine, and leads him astray with full knowledge and deliberate intention [lit. 'with a full knowledge and conscience of the sin']: the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [The Commentary explains: 'that is, a creed that is not ours.']

'The second of these sins is committed when a man gives bones too hard or food too hot to a shepherd's dog or to a house-dog. If the bones stick in the dog's teeth or lodge in his throat, or if the overly hot food burns his mouth or tongue, he may come to harm thereby; if he does come to harm, the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [Commentary: He who gives a dog food so hot as to burn his throat is margarzan [guilty of a capital offense]; he who gives bones to a dog so as to tear his throat is likewise margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).]

'The third of these sins is committed when a man strikes a pregnant bitch [a female dog with young] or frightens her by chasing her, or by shouting, or by clapping his hands. If the bitch falls into a hole, a well, a precipice, a river, or a canal, she may come to harm thereby; if she does come to harm, the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [Commentary: If a pregnant bitch is shouted at or has stones thrown at her so that the whelps suffer injury and die, the offender is margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).]

'The fourth of these sins is committed when a man has sexual intercourse with a woman who has a vaginal discharge [lit. 'the whites'] or is menstruating [lit. 'sees the blood']; the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [See Vendidad 16:14 and following for elaboration.]

'The fifth of these sins is committed when a man has intercourse with a woman who is quick with child [pregnant], whether her milk has already come to her breasts or has not yet come: she may come to harm thereby; if she does come to harm [or better: if the child should die], the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [Commentary: This applies when the woman has been pregnant for four months and ten days, at which point the child is considered formed and a soul has been added to its body (Anquetil II, 563). 'If a man come to his wife during her pregnancy so that she is injured and delivers a stillborn child, he is margarzan' (Old Riv. 115b).]

Section IIa. 'If a man has relations with a young woman—whether she is under the authority of the head of her family or not, whether she has been given in marriage or not—and she conceives by him, she must not, out of shame before the people, attempt to bring on her menses contrary to nature by means of water or plants [i.e., she must not attempt to procure an abortion using herbal or hydro-therapeutic means].' [Note: Whether she has a husband in the household of her own parents or has none; wheth- (text breaks off here)]

entities_mentioned.name: Peshotanu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Legal/moral category in Zoroastrian law: a 'great sinner' subject to severe corporal or monetary punishment
entities_mentioned.notes: Defined here as one who commits any of the five enumerated sins without confession or atonement; penalty is 200 strokes with prescribed instruments or a fine of 300 istirs.

entities_mentioned.name: Aspahe-ashtra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A horse-goad or riding whip used as an instrument of corporal punishment in Zoroastrian penal law
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'horse-goad'; one of two instruments with which the Peshotanu may be struck.

entities_mentioned.name: Sraosho-charana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A ritual/penal instrument associated with Sraosha (the Zoroastrian yazata of obedience and discipline)
entities_mentioned.notes: Second of the two instruments of corporal punishment enumerated; its precise physical form is debated among scholars.

entities_mentioned.name: istir
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian unit of monetary value; possibly related to Greek stater
entities_mentioned.notes: Three hundred istirs is the monetary equivalent of the corporal penalty for a Peshotanu sin.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Speaker of the laws enumerated in this fargard.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroaster, the prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: The interlocutor addressed by Ahura Mazda throughout the Vendidad.

entities_mentioned.name: margarzan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian legal term: 'guilty of death' or one who has committed a capital offense
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied in the commentaries to particularly aggravated forms of the sins listed, e.g., burning a dog's throat with hot food, or causing the death of a pregnant bitch's whelps.

entities_mentioned.name: shepherd's dog / house-dog
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canis lupus familiaris — domestic dog in its working and household roles
entities_mentioned.notes: Dogs hold a sacred status in Zoroastrian law; harm done to them incurs severe religious and legal penalties.

entities_mentioned.name: water and plants (used to produce menses artificially)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Abortifacient or emmenagogue preparations; likely herbal decoctions and possibly ritual water treatments
entities_mentioned.notes: The text prohibits a woman impregnated outside of proper union from procuring an abortion or inducing menstruation by means of 'water and plants,' suggesting knowledge of plant-based emmenagogues. No specific plants are named in this passage. Relevant to Materia Medica Americana insofar as it documents awareness of botanical reproductive interventions in the ancient Near East.

entities_mentioned.name: the whites
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leukorrhea (vaginal discharge); possibly also conflated with menstruation in the text's legal usage
entities_mentioned.notes: The phrase 'has the whites or sees the blood' refers to two conditions — vaginal discharge and menstruation — both of which render intercourse a grave sin under Zoroastrian law.

results.translator_notes: 1. This passage is an English-language scholarly edition (not a foreign-language source text requiring translation per se), but has been cleaned, structured, and annotated per the project schema. The segment is drawn from the Vendidad (Videvdat), Fargard 17, a section of the Avesta codifying Zoroastrian religious law. 2. The source text incorporates inline footnotes (numbered 1–8 and beyond) that blend the primary text with editorial commentary by the compiler/editor (likely James Darmesteter's edition, with added notes marked '-JHP', possibly James Henry Paterson or another editor). These have been clearly demarcated in brackets in the cleaned original and translation. 3. The passage breaks off mid-sentence at Note 8, indicating the source segment is incomplete. 4. The prohibition on abortifacient use of 'water and plants' (section IIa, clause 9) is directly relevant to the Materia Medica Americana project as evidence of ancient awareness of botanical and hydrotherapeutic reproductive interventions. No specific plant names are given in this passage, but cross-reference with Vendidad 15 and 16 may yield more specific botanical data. 5. The term 'quick with child' refers to the stage of pregnancy at which fetal movement is felt; the commentary (Anquetil II, 563) specifies this as four months and ten days of gestation, aligned with ensoulment doctrine. 6. References to 'Gr. Riv. 639' and 'Old Riv. 115b' are citations to Rivayat manuscripts (Persian commentaries on Zoroastrian law); 'Anquetil II, 563' refers to Anquetil-Duperron's 18th-century French translation of the Avesta.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 18: Purity Laws Concerning Menstruation and Ritual Isolation
results.original_text: Purity laws regarding menstruation I. Notes: 1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If there be in the house of a worshipper of Mazda a woman who has the whites or sees blood, what shall the worshippers of Mazda do? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall clear the way of the wood there, both plants and trees; they shall strew dry dust on the ground; and they shall isolate a half, or a third, or a fourth, or a fifth part of the house, lest her look should fall upon the fire.' 1. The way of the Dashtanistan. 2. Lest the wood shall be touched and defiled by the woman on her way to the Dashtanistan. 3. Lest the earth shall be touched and defiled by her. Cf. Farg. 9.11. 4. Nowadays a room on the ground-floor is reserved for that use. 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful? 4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifteen paces from the fire, fifteen paces from the water, fifteen paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful.' 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from her shall he stay, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees the blood? 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Three paces from her shall he stay, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees the blood.' In what kind of vessels shall he bring her bread? In what kind of vessels shall he bring her barley-drink? 'In vessels of iron, or of lead, or of any common metal.' 5. The food is held out to her from a distance in a metal spoon. 5b. Dar: brass. -JHP 6. Earthen vessels, when defiled, cannot be made clean; but metal vessels can. See Farg. 7.73 seq. 7. How much bread shall he bring to her? How much barley-drink shall he bring? 'Two danares of dry bread, and one danare of liquor, lest she should get too weak. 'If a child has just touched her, they shall first wash his hands and then his body. 7. A danare is, according to Anquetil, as much as four tolas; a tola is from 105 to 175 grains. 8. 'Soshyos says: For three nights cooked meat is not allowed to her, lest the issue shall grow stronger. 9. A child whom she suckles. The meaning is, Even a child, if he has touched her, must undergo the rites of cleansing. The general rule is given in the Commentary: 'Whoever has touched a Dashtan woman must wash his body and his clothes with gomez and water.' The ceremony in question is the simple Ghosel, not the Barashnum, since the woman herself performs the former only (see below, § 11 seq.) II. 8. 'If she still see blood after three nights have passed, she shall sit in the place of infirmity until four nights have passed. 'If she still see blood after four nights have passed, she shall sit in the place of infirmity until five nights have passed. 9. 'If she still see blood after five nights have passed, she shall sit in the place of infirmity until six nights have passed.'
results.translation:
Purity Laws Concerning Menstruation, Part I.

[Question] 1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If there be in the house of a worshipper of Mazda a woman who has the whites [leucorrhoea] or sees blood [menstrual bleeding], what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

[Answer] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall clear the way of the wood there — both plants and trees — they shall strew dry dust upon the ground, and they shall isolate a half, or a third, or a fourth, or a fifth part of the house, lest her gaze should fall upon the fire.'
[Note 1: 'The way of the Dashtanistan' — i.e., the prescribed path to the place of menstrual seclusion.]
[Note 2: Lest the wood be touched and defiled by the woman on her way to the Dashtanistan.]
[Note 3: Lest the earth be touched and defiled by her. Cf. Fargard 9.11.]
[Note 4: In modern practice, a ground-floor room is reserved for this purpose.]

[Question] 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred ritual twigs]? How far from the faithful?

[Answer] 4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifteen paces from the fire, fifteen paces from the water, fifteen paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful.'

[Question] 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from her shall he remain, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees blood?

[Answer] 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Three paces from her shall he remain, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees the blood.' In what kind of vessels shall he bring her bread? In what kind of vessels shall he bring her barley-drink? 'In vessels of iron, or of lead, or of any common metal.'
[Note 5: The food is extended to her from a distance using a metal spoon.]
[Note 5b: Dar [Dārī/Middle Persian gloss]: brass. — J.H.P.]
[Note 6: Earthen vessels, once defiled, cannot be purified; metal vessels, however, can be. See Fargard 7.73 and following.]

[Question] 7. How much bread shall he bring to her? How much barley-drink shall he bring?

[Answer] 'Two danares [a unit of weight] of dry bread and one danare of liquor, lest she become too weak.'
'If a child has just touched her, they shall first wash his hands and then his entire body.'
[Note 7: A danare is, according to Anquetil, equivalent to approximately four tolas; a tola equals between 105 and 175 grains in weight.]
[Note 8: Soshyos [a Zoroastrian commentator] says: For three nights, cooked meat is not permitted to her, lest the [menstrual] discharge grow stronger.]
[Note 9: The child referred to is one whom she nurses. The meaning is: even a suckling infant who has touched her must undergo purification rites. The general rule stated in the Commentary is: 'Whoever has touched a Dashtan [menstruating] woman must wash his body and his clothes with gomez [ritual cow urine] and water.' The purification ceremony in question is the simple Ghosel [ritual washing], not the Barashnum [higher-order purification], since the woman herself undergoes only the former (see below, Section 11 and following).]

Part II.

8. 'If she still sees blood after three nights have passed, she shall remain in the place of infirmity [seclusion chamber] until four nights have passed.
'If she still sees blood after four nights have passed, she shall remain in the place of infirmity until five nights have passed.

9. 'If she still sees blood after five nights have passed, she shall remain in the place of infirmity until six nights have passed.'

entities_mentioned.name: Dashtanistan
entities_mentioned.notes: The designated place of seclusion for a menstruating woman in Zoroastrian practice; a specific room or area in the household. Not a plant or drug — a ritual/spatial term.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma (Baresma bundles)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra species or Tamarix species (disputed); ritual bundle of twigs used in Zoroastrian ceremony
entities_mentioned.notes: Sacred ritual twigs used in Zoroastrian liturgy; identity debated among scholars. Not a medicinal substance in this context, but a ritual purity marker.

entities_mentioned.name: barley-drink
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Barley (Hordeum vulgare) decoction or fermented barley beverage
entities_mentioned.notes: Given to the secluded woman as sustenance during menstrual isolation. Preparation implied is liquid, likely a simple water-based barley infusion or light brew.

entities_mentioned.name: gomez
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual cow urine used in Zoroastrian purification rites
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a purifying agent for washing the body and clothes of those who have come into contact with a menstruating woman. A standard component of Zoroastrian purification ritual (Nirang-din).

entities_mentioned.name: cooked meat
entities_mentioned.notes: Prohibited to the menstruating woman for the first three nights according to the commentator Soshyos, on the grounds that it would intensify the menstrual flow. This represents a dietary restriction, not a remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: dry bread
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed ration for the secluded woman: two danares of dry bread per serving. Condition treated: maintenance of strength during menstrual seclusion.

entities_mentioned.name: iron / lead / common metal vessels
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed material for vessels used to bring food and drink to the menstruating woman. Metal vessels are preferred because they can be ritually purified, unlike earthen vessels.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a translation of Avestan religious law (Vendidad/Videvdat, Fargard 18) concerning ritual purity during menstruation, embedded with scholarly annotations by the translator (likely James Darmesteter, standard 19th-century edition). The note '5b. Dar: brass. -JHP' appears to be an editorial insertion by a later hand (initials J.H.P., identity uncertain). The original text uses 'the whites' (leucorrhoea) and 'sees blood' (menstruation) as distinct conditions, both triggering the same purity restrictions. The danare weight unit is contextually a dietary measure. The text is not a medical source per se, but records dietary, spatial, and hygienic prescriptions that have ethnomedical significance. The prohibition on cooked meat attributed to 'Soshyos' reflects a humoral-adjacent theory that rich food exacerbates hemorrhagic conditions. The purification ritual using gomez (cow urine) is directly relevant to Materia Medica Americana as a comparative cross-cultural medicinal/ritual substance. The HTML entity 'â' in 'Dashtânistân' was silently resolved to 'a' with circumflex in cleanup.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 19: Ritual Disposal of Hair and Nail Clippings
results.original_text: Hair and nails. I. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the most deadly deed whereby a man offers up a sacrifice to the Daevas?' 1. Any offense to religion is considered an offering to the Daevas, whose strength is thereby increased. See Yt5.95. 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is when a man here below, combing his hair or shaving it off, or paring off his nails, drops them in a hole or in a crack. 2. Without performing the requisite ceremonies. 3. Doubtful. 3. 'Then by this transgression of the rites, Daevas are produced in the earth; by this transgression of the rites, those Khrafstras are produced in the earth which men call lice, and which eat up the corn in the corn-field and the clothes in the wardrobe. 4. 'Therefore, thou, O Zarathushtra! whenever here below thou shalt comb thy hair or shave it off, or pare off thy nails, thou shalt take them away ten paces from the faithful, twenty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, fifty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma. 5. 'Then thou shalt dig a hole, a disti deep if the earth be hard, a vitasti deep if it be soft; thou shalt take the hair down there and thou shalt say aloud these victorious words: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up." 4. A dishti = ten fingers. A vitasti = twelve fingers. 5. [at ah'yâi ashâ mazdå urvarå vaxshat.] See above, Vd11.6; the choice of this line was determined by the presence of the word plants in it: man was considered a microcosm, and every element in him had its counterpart in nature; the skin is like the sky, the flesh is like the earth, the bones are like the mountains, the veins are like the rivers, the blood in the body is like the water in the sea, the hair is like the plants, the more hairy parts are like the forests (Gr. Bund.) Cf. Rig-veda X, 16,3; Ilias VII, 99; Empedocles, fr. 378; Epicharmus ap. Plut. Consol. ad Apoll. 15; Edda, Grimnismal 40. 6. 'Thereupon thou shalt draw three furrows with a knife of metal around the hole, or six furrows or nine, and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya three times, or six, or nine. II. 7. 'For the nails, thou shalt dig a hole, out of the house, as deep as the top joint of the little finger; thou shalt take the nails down there and thou shalt say aloud these victorious words: "The things that the pure proclaim through Asha and Vohu-mano." 6. [ashâ vohû mananghâ yâ sruyê parê magaonô.] Yasna 33.7; understood (with a play upon the word sruyê 'is heard,' and 'nails of both hands') as: 'O Asha, with Vohu-mano, the nails of the pure [are for you].' 8. 'Then thou shalt draw three furrows with a knife of metal around the hole, or six furrows or nine, and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya three times, or six, or nine. 9. 'And then: "O Asho-zushta bird! these nails I [...]"
results.translation:
On Hair and Nails. Part I.

Footnotes as integrated commentary:

1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! What is the most deadly deed by which a man offers sacrifice to the Daevas [demonic beings]?' [Footnote 1: Any offense against religion is considered an offering to the Daevas, whose power is thereby increased. See Yasht 5.95.]

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is when a man here below, combing his hair or shaving it off, or paring his nails, lets them fall into a hole or into a crack [in the ground]. [Footnote 2: That is, without performing the requisite ritual ceremonies. Footnote 3: This detail is of doubtful interpretation.]

3. 'By this transgression of the rites, Daevas are generated in the earth; by this transgression of the rites, those Khrafstras [noxious creatures] are generated in the earth which men call lice, and which devour the grain in the cornfield and the garments in the wardrobe.

4. 'Therefore, O Zarathushtra! whenever here below thou dost comb thy hair or shave it off, or pare thy nails, thou shalt carry them away ten paces from the faithful [believers], twenty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, fifty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred ritual twigs].

5. 'Then thou shalt dig a hole — a dishti [ten finger-breadths] deep if the earth be hard, a vitasti [twelve finger-breadths] deep if it be soft; thou shalt place the hair therein and shalt say aloud these victorious words: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up." [Footnote 4: A dishti equals ten fingers' breadth; a vitasti equals twelve fingers' breadth. Footnote 5: The Avestan text reads: at ah'yâi ashâ mazdå urvarå vaxshat. See above, Vendidad 11.6. This verse was chosen because of the word "plants" contained in it: man was understood as a microcosm, each element of his body having a counterpart in nature — the skin corresponds to the sky, the flesh to the earth, the bones to the mountains, the veins to the rivers, the blood to the water of the sea, the hair to the plants, and the more densely haired parts to the forests. (Cf. Greater Bundahishn.) Compare also: Rig-Veda X.16.3; Iliad VII.99; Empedocles, fragment 378; Epicharmus as quoted by Plutarch, Consolatio ad Apollonium 15; Eddic poem Grímnismál, stanza 40.]

6. 'Thereupon thou shalt draw three furrows with a metal knife around the hole, or six furrows, or nine; and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya prayer three times, or six, or nine.

Part II.

7. 'For the nails, thou shalt dig a hole outside the house, as deep as the topmost joint of the little finger; thou shalt place the nails therein and shalt say aloud these victorious words: "The things that the pure proclaim through Asha [Righteousness] and Vohu-mano [Good Mind]." [Footnote 6: Avestan text: ashâ vohû mananghâ yâ sruyê parê magaonô. Yasna 33.7. This is understood — with a pun on the word sruyê, meaning both "is heard" and "nails of both hands" — as: "O Asha, together with Vohu-mano, the nails of the pure [are for you]."]

8. 'Then thou shalt draw three furrows with a metal knife around the hole, or six furrows, or nine; and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya prayer three times, or six, or nine.

9. 'And then thou shalt say: "O Asho-zushta bird [the bird sacred to righteousness]! these nails I [give to thee...]" [Text breaks off here.]

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.notes: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism; the divine interlocutor in this Vendidad passage.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra (Zoroaster)
entities_mentioned.notes: The prophet of Zoroastrianism; questioner in this catechetical dialogue.

entities_mentioned.name: Daevas
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonic beings in Zoroastrian cosmology; improperly disposed hair and nails are said to strengthen them.

entities_mentioned.name: Khrafstras
entities_mentioned.notes: Noxious or harmful creatures in Zoroastrian belief; in this context identified with lice (Pediculus spp.) that destroy grain and clothing.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma (Barsom)
entities_mentioned.notes: Consecrated bundles of ritual twigs used in Zoroastrian liturgy; hair clippings must be kept fifty paces away from them.

entities_mentioned.name: Asho-zushta bird
entities_mentioned.notes: A sacred bird associated with righteousness (Asha) in Zoroastrian ritual; recipient of the nail clippings in the accompanying prayer. Possibly related to the Avestan concept of the Saena bird or another sacred avian figure.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahuna-Vairya
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal sacred prayer formula of Zoroastrianism (also known as the Yatha Ahu Vairyo); to be chanted three, six, or nine times as part of the disposal ritual.

entities_mentioned.name: Asha
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan concept of cosmic truth, righteousness, and order; one of the Amesha Spentas (Holy Immortals).

entities_mentioned.name: Vohu-mano
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan concept of Good Mind or Good Thought; one of the Amesha Spentas.

results.translator_notes: This passage is not a medical or materia medica text in the pharmacological sense, but it is of direct relevance to Materia Medica Americana as a primary source for ritual hygiene practices. It prescribes specific procedures for the disposal of cut hair and nail clippings to prevent ritual pollution and the generation of noxious creatures (Khrafstras/lice). The measurements dishti (ten finger-breadths) and vitasti (twelve finger-breadths) are indigenous units of depth. The microcosm-macrocosm parallel drawn in footnote 5 — identifying hair with plants — is significant for the history of sympathetic medicine and cosmological anatomy across cultures. The text breaks off mid-sentence at section 9; the source appears to be an incomplete extraction. The footnotes in the source are editorial/scholarly annotations embedded within the primary text; they have been preserved and clearly labeled as such in the translation. OCR artifacts corrected include: 'å' and 'â' diacritics in Avestan transliterations standardized silently.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 20: The True Athravan and the Causes of Death
results.original_text: 1. 'There is many a one, O holy Zarathushtra!' said Ahura Mazda, 'Who wears a wrong Paitidana, and who has not girded his loins with the Religion; When such a man says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 2. 'He holds a wrong Khrafstraghna in his hand and he has not girded his loins with the Religion; when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 3. 'He holds a wrong twig in his hand and he has not girded his loins with the Religion; when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 4. 'He wields a wrong Ashtra mairya and he has not girded his loins with the Religion; when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 5. 'He who sleeps on throughout the night, neither performing the Yasna nor chanting the hymns, worshipping neither by word nor by deed, neither learning nor teaching, with a longing for (everlasting) life, he lies when he says, "I am an Athravan," do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 6. 'Him thou shalt call an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra! who throughout the night sits up and demands of the holy Wisdom, which makes man free from anxiety, and wide of heart, and easy of conscience at the head of the Chinwad bridge, and which makes him reach that world, that holy world, that excellent world of Paradise. 7. (Therefore) demand of me, thou upright one! of me, who am the Maker, the most beneficent of all beings, the best knowing, the most pleased in answering what is asked of me; demand of me, that thou mayst be the better, that thou mayst be the happier.' 8. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! What is it that brings in the unseen power of Death?' 9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the man that teaches a wrong Religion; it is the man who continues for three springs without wearing the sacred girdle, without chanting the Gathas, without worshipping.'
results.translation:
§1. 'There are many, O holy Zarathushtra,' said Ahura Mazda, 'who wear an incorrect Paitidana [the ritual mouth-veil worn by priests during sacred rites] and who have not girded their loins with the Religion [i.e., have not bound themselves to the Law as one binds on the Kusti, the sacred girdle]; when such a man says, "I am an Athravan [a Zoroastrian priest of the fire-tending class]," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§2. 'He who holds an incorrect Khrafstraghna [the ritual instrument used to strike noxious creatures] in his hand, and who has not girded his loins with the Religion — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§3. 'He who holds an incorrect twig [i.e., the Baresma/barsom bundle, or the urvaram sacred branch, used in ritual] in his hand, and who has not girded his loins with the Religion — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§4. 'He who wields an incorrect Ashtra mairya [the priestly rod, also called Aspahe-ashtra, with which the priest acting as Sraosho-varez chastises the guilty] and who has not girded his loins with the Religion — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§5. 'He who sleeps throughout the night, performing neither the Yasna [the principal act of Zoroastrian worship] nor chanting the hymns, worshipping neither by word nor by deed, neither learning nor teaching, yet longing for everlasting life — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§6. 'Him shalt thou call an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra, who sits up throughout the night and seeks of the holy Wisdom — that Wisdom which frees man from anxiety, enlarges his heart, and eases his conscience when he stands at the head of the Chinwad bridge [the bridge of judgment crossed by souls after death], and which brings him to that world, that holy world, that excellent world of Paradise.'

§7. 'Therefore seek of me, thou upright one — of me, who am the Maker, the most beneficent of all beings, the best knowing, the most pleased to answer what is asked of me. Seek of me, so that thou mayst be the better, so that thou mayst be the happier.'

§8. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! What is it that summons the unseen power of Death?'

§9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the man who teaches a false Religion. It is the man who for three springs [i.e., three years] continues without wearing the sacred girdle [the Kusti], without chanting the Gathas [the sacred hymns of Zarathushtra], without worship.'

entities_mentioned.name: Paitidana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual mouth-veil (padam) worn by Zoroastrian priests during sacred rites to prevent pollution of the sacred fire or ritual objects by breath
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-referenced to Vd14.8, note 19. Still worn by Zoroastrian priests today.

entities_mentioned.name: Athravan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian priest of the fire-tending priestly class (Athornan/Athravans)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term literally relates to fire (atar); the segment is largely concerned with defining who genuinely merits this title.

entities_mentioned.name: Kusti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sacred woven girdle (koshti/kusti) worn by Zoroastrians after initiation (Naojote ceremony)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'girding of the loins with the Religion' is interpreted throughout as a metaphor for binding oneself to the Law, as one literally binds on the Kusti. Referred to at Yasna 9.26.

entities_mentioned.name: Khrafstraghna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual implement (possibly a club or striker) used to kill noxious creatures (khrafstra) in Zoroastrian practice
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-referenced to Vd14.8, note 20.

entities_mentioned.name: Baresma / Barsom
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bundle of sacred twigs or metal rods held by officiating priests during Zoroastrian rituals
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called urvaram; cross-referenced to Vd3.1, note 3 and Vd14.8, note 23.

entities_mentioned.name: Ashtra mairya (Aspahe-ashtra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Priestly rod or whip used by the Sraosho-varez (priestly officer of discipline) to chastise the guilty
entities_mentioned.notes: The editor's note identifies it specifically as the instrument of the Sraosho-varez function.

entities_mentioned.name: Yasna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The principal Zoroastrian liturgical ceremony; also the name of the primary Avestan liturgical text

entities_mentioned.name: Gathas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The ancient hymns composed by the prophet Zarathushtra (Zoroaster), forming the oldest stratum of the Avesta

entities_mentioned.name: Chinwad bridge
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Bridge of the Separator (Chinvat Peretu) in Zoroastrian eschatology, crossed by souls after death; it widens for the righteous and narrows to a razor's edge for the wicked
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-referenced to Vd19.30.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The supreme deity of Zoroastrianism, the 'Wise Lord'

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The prophet Zoroaster, founder of the Zoroastrian religion

entities_mentioned.name: Sraosho-varez
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A priestly officer in Zoroastrian practice tasked with enforcing religious discipline
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the editorial note to §4 as the functionary who wields the Ashtra.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a scholastic-religious text, not a medical or pharmacological source; it contains no materia medica content in the strict sense. The 'unseen power of Death' introduced at §8–9 may be of indirect relevance to Zoroastrian concepts of ritual pollution and spiritual hygiene as preconditions for physical and eschatological wellbeing. The source text is an English translation (by James Darmesteter, Sacred Books of the East series) with editorial annotations, some of which are marked [JHP] indicating a secondary annotator. The segment is incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence at §9. Footnote numbering in the source resets or is non-sequential; cross-references are to Vendidad chapters and verses. No OCR errors were detected that could not be silently resolved.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 21: Angra Mainyu's Attack on Zarathushtra and His Repulse
results.original_text:
I. 1. From the region of the north, from the regions of the north, forth rushed Angra Mainyu, the deadly, the Daeva of the Daevas. And thus spake the evil-doer Angra Mainyu, the deadly: 'Druj, rush down and kill him,' O holy Zarathushtra! The Druj came rushing along, the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death.

Notes: 1. From hell; cf. II, 10n; III, 7n; Hadokht Nask 25; Bundahishn 15.19. 2. 'The fiend of fiends,' the arch-fiend. 3. Buiti is identified by the Greater Bundahishn with the Bût, the idol, worshipped by Budasp (a corruption of Bodhisativa). Buiti [Buddha] would be therefore a personification of Buddhism, which was flourishing in Eastern Iran in the two centuries before and after Christ. Buidhi (Vd11.9) may be another and more correct pronunciation of Bodhi. 4. Idolatry (cf. note 3) being the death of the soul.

2. Zarathushtra chanted aloud the Ahuna-Vairya: 'The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness. The gifts of Vohu-mano to the deeds done in this world for Mazda. He who relieves the poor makes Ahura king.' He offered the sacrifice to the good waters of the good Daitya! He recited the profession of the worshippers of Mazda! The Druj dismayed, rushed away, the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death.

Notes: 5. [i.e. Ahunwar], a prayer in frequent use, and considered of great efficacy. It was by reciting it that Ohrmazd in his first conflict with Ahriman drove him back to hell (Bund 1). 6. The river in Eranwej; see Vd1.3. 7. The Fravarane (Y11.16).

3. And the Druj said unto Angra Mainyu: 'Thou, tormenter, Angra Mainyu! I see no way to kill Spitama Zarathushtra, so great is the glory of the holy Zarathushtra.' Zarathushtra saw (all this) within his soul: 'The wicked, the evil-doing Daevas (thought he) take counsel together for my death.'

Ia. 4. Up started Zarathushtra, forward went Zarathushtra, unabated by Akem-mano, by the hardness of his malignant riddles; he went swinging stones in his hand, stones as big as a house, which he obtained from the Maker, Ahura Mazda, he the holy Zarathushtra. 'Whereat on this wide, round earth, whose ends lie afar, whereat dost thou swing (those stones), thou who standest by the upper bank of the river Dareja, in the mansion of Pourushaspa?'

Notes: 8. See Vd10.10n. 9. This is a fragment of an old legend in which Zarathushtra and Angra Mainyu played respectively the parts of Oedipus and the Sphinx. Cf. Yt5.81, where the same legend is told in nearly the same terms of the sorcerer Ahktya and Yavisht i Friyan. [See also the Pahlavi Text Yavisht i Friyan.] 10. The Commentary has, 'Some say, those stones are the Ahunwar.' If one keeps in mind how much the Moslem legend of Ibrahim owes to the legend of Zarathushtra, one may easily admit that this passage in our text is the origin of the story of how Iblis tempted Ibrahim, and was pelted away, whence he was named 'the stoned One' (ar-ragîmû). 11. 'The Daraja is the chief of the rivers, bec[ause...]'
results.translation:
Part I. Section 1. From the region of the north, from the regions of the north [i.e., from hell], forth rushed Angra Mainyu [Ahriman], the deadly one, the Daeva [demon] of Daevas — the arch-fiend. And thus spoke the evil-doer Angra Mainyu, the deadly: 'Druj [Lie-demon], rush down and kill him' — O holy Zarathushtra! The Druj came rushing along: the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death [i.e., the demon of idolatry, identified with the idol Bût and, by extension, Buddhism — a personification of the spiritual death caused by idolatry].

Section 2. Zarathushtra chanted aloud the Ahuna-Vairya [Ahunwar prayer]: 'The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness. The gifts of Vohu-mano [Good Mind] to the deeds done in this world for Mazda. He who relieves the poor makes Ahura king.' He offered sacrifice to the good waters of the good Daitya [river of Eranwej]! He recited the profession of faith of the worshippers of Mazda [the Fravarane]! The Druj, dismayed, rushed away — the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death.

Section 3. And the Druj said unto Angra Mainyu: 'Thou, tormenter, Angra Mainyu! I see no way to kill Spitama Zarathushtra, so great is the glory of the holy Zarathushtra.' Zarathushtra perceived all this within his soul: 'The wicked, the evil-doing Daevas,' thought he, 'take counsel together for my death.'

Part Ia. Section 4. Up started Zarathushtra, forward went Zarathushtra, undaunted by Akem-mano [Evil Mind] and by the harshness of his malignant riddles [an allusion to the Oedipus-and-Sphinx type legend]; he went swinging stones in his hand — stones as big as a house — which he had obtained from the Maker, Ahura Mazda, he the holy Zarathushtra. [Angra Mainyu asked:] 'Whereat on this wide, round earth, whose ends lie afar, whereat dost thou swing those stones, thou who standest by the upper bank of the river Dareja, in the mansion of Pourushaspa [Zarathushtra's father]?'

[Translator's notes embedded in the source text indicate: the Ahunwar prayer was of supreme apotropaic efficacy in Zoroastrian tradition; the demon Buiti is equated with Buddhist idolatry flourishing in Eastern Iran around the turn of the Common Era; the legend of stone-throwing parallels the Islamic legend of Ibrahim pelting Iblis, suggesting textual influence; and the episode structurally echoes the riddle-contest of Oedipus and the Sphinx, as well as the Avestan tale of Yavisht i Friyan and the sorcerer Ahktya.]

entities_mentioned.name: Angra Mainyu (Ahriman)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The supreme evil spirit in Zoroastrian dualistic theology
entities_mentioned.notes: Described here as 'Daeva of the Daevas,' i.e., the arch-fiend or chief demon.

entities_mentioned.name: Druj
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan concept of the Lie or Deception; a class of female demon
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here both as a general term for the demonic principle of falsehood and as a specific entity sent to kill Zarathushtra.

entities_mentioned.name: Buiti (Buidhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Identified in Greater Bundahishn with the idol Bût; likely a demonization of the Buddha or Buddhist practice
entities_mentioned.notes: Buiti is glossed as a personification of Buddhism (from 'Bodhi' or 'Bodhisattva'), representing spiritual death through idolatry. Buddhism was active in Eastern Iran c. 200 BCE–200 CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahuna-Vairya (Ahunwar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Yatha Ahu Vairyo prayer; the most sacred formula in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Recitation of this prayer is portrayed as the primary apotropaic weapon against demonic attack. Used by Ohrmazd to repel Ahriman in Bundahishn 1.

entities_mentioned.name: Vohu-mano
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan: 'Good Mind'; one of the Amesha Spentas (holy immortals) in Zoroastrianism
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in the Ahunwar prayer as the divine principle rewarding righteous deeds.

entities_mentioned.name: Daitya (river)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A sacred river in Eranwej (Airyana Vaeja); possibly identified with the Araxes or Oxus
entities_mentioned.notes: Sacrifice is offered to its waters; see Vd1.3 for the mythological geography of Eranwej.

entities_mentioned.name: Fravarane
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Zoroastrian profession of faith (Y11.16); equivalent to a creedal declaration
entities_mentioned.notes: Recited by Zarathushtra as a second act of ritual defense against the demon.

entities_mentioned.name: Akem-mano
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan: 'Evil Mind'; the demonic counterpart to Vohu-mano
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as the source of malignant riddles posed to Zarathushtra; see Vd10.10n.

entities_mentioned.name: Dareja (river)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: An Avestan river; possibly in the Eranwej region; full identification uncertain due to truncated source text
entities_mentioned.notes: Text breaks off mid-note: 'The Daraja is the chief of the rivers, bec[ause...]'

entities_mentioned.name: Pourushaspa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Father of Zarathushtra in Zoroastrian tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: His mansion on the upper bank of the Dareja is the setting for this episode.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra (Zoroaster)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism; historical figure estimated c. 1500–600 BCE depending on scholarly tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Central hero of this passage; defeats demonic attack through prayer and stone-throwing, paralleling the Oedipus/Sphinx and Ibrahim/Iblis myth structures.

entities_mentioned.name: Budasp
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corruption of 'Bodhisattva'; a figure in Zoroastrian polemical literature representing Buddhism
entities_mentioned.notes: Named in the Bundahishn as the worshipper of the idol Bût (Buddha image).

results.translator_notes: This segment is a theological-mythological narrative from Vendidad (Videvdat) Fargard 21, part of the Avesta (Zoroastrian sacred canon). It is not a medical or materia medica text; no plants, drugs, or medical preparations are described. The segment is notable for: (1) the identification of the demon Buiti with Buddhism, reflecting Zoroastrian polemics against Buddhist influence in Eastern Iran; (2) a structural parallel drawn by the annotator between Zarathushtra's stone-throwing and the Islamic legend of Ibrahim pelting Iblis at Mecca (the stoning ritual at Hajj), suggesting cross-traditional mythological borrowing; (3) an Oedipus-Sphinx riddle-contest parallel in the Zarathushtra-Angra Mainyu confrontation. The source text contains HTML entity encoding (e.g., 'û' for û, 'î' for î) which has been silently corrected. The final note (note 11) is truncated mid-sentence in the source and is marked accordingly. No materia medica extraction is applicable for this segment.

results.title_inferred: Thrita, the First Healer: Origin of Medicine and Healing Plants in the Vendidad
results.original_text: Thrita, the first healer. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Who was he who first of the healers, of the wise, the happy, the wealthy, the glorious, the strong, the Paradhatas, drove back sickness to sickness, drove back death to death; and first turned away the point of the sword and the fire of fever from the bodies of mortals?' [Footnote 2: Those who knew how to take care of their own bodies, like Isfandyar: some say that no sword could wound him (Comm.)] [Footnote 3: The Paradhata or Peshdad, the kings of the first Iranian dynasty.] [Footnote 4: That is to say, who kept sickness in bonds, who kept death in bonds (Comm.)] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thrita it was who first of the healers, of the wise, the happy, the wealthy, the glorious, the strong, the Paradhatas, drove back sickness to sickness, drove back death to death, and first turned away the point of the sword and the fire of fever from the bodies of mortals. 3. He asked for a source of remedies; he obtained it from Khshathra-Vairya, to withstand sickness and to withstand death; to withstand pain and to withstand fever; to withstand Sarana and to withstand Sarastya [headache and cold fever]; to withstand Azana and to withstand Azahva; to withstand Kurugha and to withstand Azivaka; to withstand Duruka and to withstand Astairya; to withstand the evil eye, rottenness, and infection which Angra Mainyu had created against the bodies of mortals. [Footnote 5: As Khshathra-Vairya presides over metals, it was a knife he received, 'of which the point and the base were set in gold.' He was therefore the first who healed with the knife, as well as the first who healed with herbs. As for the healing with the holy word, see §5 and seq.] 4. And I Ahura Mazda brought down the healing plants that, by many hundreds, by many thousands, by many myriads, grow up all around the one Gaokerena. [Footnote 7: There are two Haomas: one is the yellow or golden Haoma, which is the earthly Haoma, and which, when prepared for the sacrifice, is the king of healing plants; the other is the white Haoma or Gaokerena, which grows up in the middle of the sea Vouru-Kasha, where it is surrounded by the ten thousand healing plants, created by Ohrmazd in order to oppose so many diseases that had been created by Ahriman (Bundahishn 9; see Vd22.2). A frog goes swimming around the Gaokerena to gnaw it down: but two Kar Mahi (Vd19.42) keep watch and circle around the tree, so that the head of one of them is continually towards the frog (Bund. 18).] 5. All this do we achieve; all this do we order; all these prayers do we utter, for the benefit of the bodies of mortals. [Footnote 8: We do all that is necessary for healing; we give, as Dastobar (Dastur), the necessary prescriptions; we recite the needed prayers. — This section is a transition to the spell-medicine.] 6. To withstand sickness and to withstand death; to withstand pain and to withstand fever; to withstand Sar[ana and to withstand Sarastya...] [TEXT ENDS INCOMPLETE]
results.translation:
Thrita, the First Healer. 1. Zarathushtra [Zoroaster] put this question to Ahura Mazda: 'Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Creator of the material world, O Holy One! Who was the first among the healers — among the wise, the prosperous, the wealthy, the glorious, the mighty, the Paradhatas [kings of the first Iranian dynasty, the Peshdadians] — who drove back sickness upon sickness itself, who drove back death upon death itself, and who first turned away the edge of the sword and the burning heat of fever from the bodies of mortal men?' [Commentator's note: those who knew how to preserve their own bodies, like Isfandyar, of whom some say no sword could wound him. The Paradhata, or Peshdad, were the kings of the first Iranian dynasty. The meaning is: who kept sickness in bonds, who kept death in bonds.]

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It was Thrita who was first among the healers — among the wise, the prosperous, the wealthy, the glorious, the mighty, the Paradhatas — who drove back sickness upon sickness itself, who drove back death upon death itself, and who first turned away the edge of the sword and the burning heat of fever from the bodies of mortal men.

3. 'He petitioned for a source of remedies, and he received it from Khshathra-Vairya [the Aməša Spənta presiding over metals], to resist sickness and to resist death; to resist pain and to resist fever; to resist Sarana and to resist Sarastya [headache and cold fever]; to resist Azana and to resist Azahva; to resist Kurugha and to resist Azivaka; to resist Duruka and to resist Astairya; to resist the evil eye, putrefaction, and contagion which Angra Mainyu [Ahriman, the evil spirit] had fashioned against the bodies of mortal men.' [Commentator's note: Because Khshathra-Vairya presides over metals, what Thrita received was a knife, 'of which the point and the base were set in gold.' He was thus the first to heal with the knife, as well as the first to heal with herbs; for healing by the holy word, see §5 and following.]

4. 'And I, Ahura Mazda, caused the healing plants to descend — those that grow up in their hundreds, their thousands, their myriads, all about the one Gaokerena [the sacred white Haoma tree].' [Commentator's note: There are two Haomas — the yellow or golden Haoma, which is the earthly Haoma and, when prepared for sacrifice, is the king of all healing plants; and the white Haoma, or Gaokerena, which grows in the midst of the sea Vouru-Kasha, surrounded by ten thousand healing plants created by Ohrmazd to counter the diseases created by Ahriman (Bundahishn 9; cf. Vd. 22.2). A frog swims about the Gaokerena attempting to gnaw it down, but two Kar Mahi fish (cf. Vd. 19.42) keep watch and circle the tree so that one of them always faces the frog (Bundahishn 18).]

5. 'All this we accomplish; all this we ordain; all these prayers we recite, for the benefit of the bodies of mortal men.' [Commentator's note: We perform all that is required for healing; we issue, in the capacity of Dastobar [Dastur, priestly authority], the necessary prescriptions; we recite the requisite prayers. — This section marks a transition to medicine by incantation.]

6. 'To resist sickness and to resist death; to resist pain and to resist fever; to resist Sara[na and to resist Sarastya...]' [TEXT ENDS INCOMPLETE]

entities_mentioned.name: Thrita
entities_mentioned.notes: Mythological first healer of the Avestan tradition, associated with the Paradhata (Peshdadian) dynasty; credited with introducing both surgical (knife) and herbal medicine. Possibly related to the Vedic figure Trita.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.notes: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism, described here as the source of healing plants and the one who brought them down to earth around the Gaokerena.

entities_mentioned.name: Zarathushtra (Zoroaster)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prophet and interlocutor in the Vendidad dialogue framework.

entities_mentioned.name: Khshathra-Vairya
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the Amesha Spentas (Bounteous Immortals) in Zoroastrianism, presiding over metals and desirable dominion. Source of the healing knife given to Thrita.

entities_mentioned.name: Angra Mainyu (Ahriman)
entities_mentioned.notes: The evil spirit of Zoroastrianism, credited with creating diseases, the evil eye, rottenness, and infection against mortals.

entities_mentioned.name: Gaokerena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: White Haoma; possibly Ephedra species or a mythological sacred plant
entities_mentioned.notes: The sacred white Haoma tree growing in the sea Vouru-Kasha, surrounded by ten thousand healing plants. Distinct from the earthly golden/yellow Haoma. The plant identity of Haoma is debated; Ephedra and Peganum harmala have been proposed.

entities_mentioned.name: Haoma (golden/yellow)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ephedra sinica, Peganum harmala, or Sarcostemma acidum
entities_mentioned.notes: The earthly Haoma, described as the king of healing plants when prepared for sacrifice. Corresponds to the Vedic Soma. Its precise botanical identity remains contested in scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: Sarana / Sarastya
entities_mentioned.notes: Named disease entities in the Avestan text; the commentator glosses these as 'headache and cold fever.'

entities_mentioned.name: Azana / Azahva
entities_mentioned.notes: Named disease entities in the Avestan demonological disease catalogue; specific identification unknown.

entities_mentioned.name: Kurugha / Azivaka
entities_mentioned.notes: Named disease entities in the Avestan demonological disease catalogue; specific identification unknown.

entities_mentioned.name: Duruka / Astairya
entities_mentioned.notes: Named disease entities in the Avestan demonological disease catalogue; specific identification unknown.

entities_mentioned.name: Kar Mahi
entities_mentioned.notes: Mythological fish described in Vendidad 19.42 and Bundahishn 18 as guarding the Gaokerena tree against a destructive frog.

entities_mentioned.name: Isfandyar
entities_mentioned.notes: Legendary Iranian hero mentioned in the commentary as an example of one whose body could not be wounded by a sword; invoked to illustrate the class of 'those who knew how to take care of their own bodies.'

entities_mentioned.name: Paradhata (Peshdad)
entities_mentioned.notes: The legendary first dynasty of Iranian kings in Zoroastrian tradition. Thrita is numbered among them.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly edition of Vendidad (Videvdat) Fargard 22 in English, already partially translated in the source, with embedded commentary footnotes. The source text is not a foreign-language original but an existing English translation (likely Darmesteter's Sacred Books of the East rendering) with interpolated scholarly notes. The translation above has been regularized, footnotes integrated contextually, and the incomplete final line noted. The text describes three modes of healing recognized in the Avestan tradition: healing by the knife (surgery), healing by herbs (pharmacopoeia), and healing by the holy word (incantation/prayer) — a tripartite medical system explicitly referenced in Vendidad 7.44. The disease names (Sarana, Sarastya, Azana, etc.) are Avestan demonological terms for specific ailments; their precise clinical correlates are largely unidentified in modern scholarship beyond the commentator's gloss on 'headache and cold fever' for Sarana/Sarastya. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence in §6, indicating the source text is truncated.

results.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 23: Invocations for Rain, Healing, and Purification
results.original_text: I. Notes: 1. Hail, bounteous bull! Hail to thee, beneficent bull! Hail to thee, who makest increase! Hail to thee, who makest growth! Hail to thee, who dost bestow his part upon the righteous faithful, and wilt bestow it on the faithful yet unborn! Hail to thee, whom the Jahi kills, and the ungodly Ashemaogha, and the wicked tyrant. 1. The primeval bull who was created by Ohrmazd and killed by Ahriman with the help of the Jahi. -- Clause 1 is to be recited when one meets an ox or any kind of cattle, Gr. Riv. 386. 2. Possibly, 'who dost kill the Jahi (by means of gomez). 3. His daily food. 4. The wicked kills animals, out of mere cruelty, beyond his needs (Yasna 29.1; Y32.12, 14; Y48.7). II. 2. 'Come, come on, O clouds, from up above, down on the earth, by thousands of drops, by myriads of drops:' thus say, O holy Zarathushtra! 'to destroy sickness, to destroy death, to destroy the sickness that kills, to destroy death that kills, to destroy Gadha and Apagadha. 5. Cf. Bund. 3.3, 6, 4. 6. Names of diseases. 3. 'If death come after noon, may healing come at eve! If death come at eve, may healing come at night! If death come at night, may healing come at dawn! And showers shower down new water, new earth, new plants, new healing powers, and new healing. IIIa. 4. 'As the sea Vouru-kasha is the gathering place of the waters, rising up and going down, up the aerial way and down the earth, down the earth and up the aerial way: thus rise up and roll along! thou in whose rising and growing Ahura Mazda made the aerial way. 7. Waters and light are believed to flow from the same spring and in the same bed: 'As the light comes in through Alburz (Hara Berezaiti) and goes out through Alburz, so water also comes out through Alburz and goes away through Alburz' (Bund. 20.4). Every day the sun, moon, and stars rise up from Alburz, and every day all the waters on the earth come back together to the sea Vouru-kasha, and there collected come down again to the earth from the peaks of Alburz (Gr. Riv. 43'). As light comes from three different sources (the sun, the moon, and the stars), the waters are invoked three times, first in company with the sun, then with the moon, lastly with the stars, as if there should be three different movements of the rain connected with the three movements of light. 8. Waters come down from the sky to the earth and rise back from the earth to the sky (see Vd5.15 seq.) 5. 'Up! rise up and roll along! thou swift-horsed Sun, above Hara Berezaiti, and produce light for the world (and mayst thou [O man!] rise up there, if thou art to abide in Garo-nmanem), along the path made by Mazda, along the way made by the gods, the watery way they opened. 9. 'If thou art a righteous man' (Comm.) 10. The translation of this clause is doubtful. 6. 'And the Holy Word [manthra spenta] shall keep away the evil: Of thee [O child!] I will cleanse the birth and growth; of thee [O woman!] I will make the body and the
results.translation:
Section I, Verse 1: Hail, O bounteous bull! Hail to thee, O beneficent bull! Hail to thee, who causest increase! Hail to thee, who causest growth! Hail to thee, who dost bestow his portion upon the righteous faithful, and shalt bestow it upon the faithful yet unborn! Hail to thee, whom the Jahi [demoness of licentiousness] slays, and the ungodly Ashemaogha [heretic], and the wicked tyrant. [Scholarly note: This refers to the primeval bull created by Ohrmazd (Ahura Mazda) and slain by Ahriman with the assistance of the Jahi. This clause is to be recited when one encounters an ox or any cattle (cf. Greater Rivayat, p. 386). A secondary interpretation suggests the bull 'slays the Jahi by means of gomez [ox-urine].' The wicked kills animals out of mere cruelty, beyond his needs (cf. Yasna 29.1; 32.12, 14; 48.7).]

Section II, Verse 2: 'Come, come forth, O clouds, from on high, down upon the earth, in thousands of drops, in myriads of drops' — thus speak, O holy Zarathushtra! — 'to destroy sickness, to destroy death, to destroy the lethal sickness, to destroy lethal death, to destroy Gadha and Apagadha [named diseases].' [Scholarly note: Cf. Bundahishn 3.3, 6, 4. Gadha and Apagadha are names of specific diseases.]

Verse 3: 'If death come after midday, may healing come by evening! If death come by evening, may healing come by night! If death come by night, may healing come at dawn! And may showers rain down new water, new earth, new plants, new healing powers, and new healing.'

Section IIIa, Verse 4: 'As the sea Vouru-kasha is the gathering place of the waters, rising up and going down — up the aerial way and down to the earth, down to the earth and up the aerial way — so rise up and roll along! Thou [O water], in whose rising and increase Ahura Mazda fashioned the aerial way.' [Scholarly note: Waters and light are believed to flow from the same source and along the same channel. As the light enters through Alburz (Hara Berezaiti) and exits through Alburz, so too does water issue from Alburz and return thither (cf. Bundahishn 20.4). Each day the sun, moon, and stars ascend from Alburz, and all terrestrial waters return to the sea Vouru-kasha, thence descending again from the peaks of Alburz (cf. Greater Rivayat, p. 43'). The waters are invoked three times, corresponding to the three sources of light — sun, moon, and stars — as if three movements of rain correspond to three movements of light. Waters descend from sky to earth and ascend from earth back to sky (cf. Vendidad 5.15 seq.).]

Verse 5: 'Up! Rise up and roll along, O swift-horsed Sun, above Hara Berezaiti, and bring forth light for the world — (and mayest thou, O man, rise up there, if thou art destined to dwell in Garo-nmanem [the House of Song, i.e., Paradise]) — along the path fashioned by Mazda, along the way fashioned by the gods, the watery way which they opened.' [Scholarly note: 'If thou art a righteous man' (Commentary). The translation of the parenthetical clause is uncertain.]

Verse 6: 'And the Holy Word [manthra spenta, the sacred utterance] shall repel evil. Of thee, O child, I will purify the birth and growth; of thee, O woman, I will restore the body and the —' [Text breaks off; remainder of verse not present in this segment.]

entities_mentioned.name: Bounteous Bull / Primeval Bull
entities_mentioned.notes: Mythological creature of Zoroastrian cosmology, created by Ahura Mazda and slain by Ahriman; its body or products (including gomez, ox-urine) are associated with purification and healing.

entities_mentioned.name: Gomez
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ox or bovine urine (used ritually in Zoroastrian purification rites)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a means by which the bull 'kills the Jahi' (demoness of pollution); gomez is a primary purifying agent in Zoroastrian ritual medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Gadha
entities_mentioned.notes: Named disease in Avestan text; exact medical identity unknown. Treated by invocation of rain and the Holy Word.

entities_mentioned.name: Apagadha
entities_mentioned.notes: Named disease in Avestan text, paired with Gadha; exact medical identity unknown. Treated by invocation of rain and the Holy Word.

entities_mentioned.name: Manthra Spenta (Holy Word)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sacred Avestan liturgical formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a ritual/spiritual remedy that repels evil, purifies birth, and restores the body; a form of verbal/incantatory medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Rain / Clouds
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rainwater as therapeutic agent
entities_mentioned.notes: Invoked to destroy sickness and death, including named diseases Gadha and Apagadha; rain is conceived as carrying new healing powers (new water, new earth, new plants, new healing).

entities_mentioned.name: Sea Vouru-kasha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological cosmic sea of Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: Gathering place of all waters; central to the cosmological water-cycle described in this text.

entities_mentioned.name: Hara Berezaiti (Alburz)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mythological world-mountain; possibly associated with the Alborz mountain range in Iran
entities_mentioned.notes: The cosmic mountain through which both light and water circulate; conduit for the sun's path and the hydrological cycle.

entities_mentioned.name: Jahi
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan demoness of licentiousness and pollution; associated with killing or corrupting cattle and the righteous.

entities_mentioned.name: Ashemaogha
entities_mentioned.notes: Avestan term for a heretic or one who promotes the lie (druj); listed among those who harm the primeval bull.

entities_mentioned.name: Garo-nmanem
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zoroastrian Paradise / House of Song
entities_mentioned.notes: The heavenly abode of the righteous in Zoroastrian eschatology.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an annotated scholarly edition of Vendidad Fargard 23 (Avestan Zoroastrian text), not an original primary-language source but rather an English scholarly translation with embedded commentary (likely James Darmesteter's Sacred Books of the East edition). The text is presented here as-is, since the 'original' is already in English scholarly translation. Several passages are incomplete due to text truncation at the end of the segment (Verse 6 breaks off mid-sentence). The medical/healing content centers on: (1) invocation of rain to destroy named diseases (Gadha, Apagadha) and death generally; (2) use of gomez (ox-urine) as a purifying agent; (3) the manthra spenta (sacred word/incantation) as a healing and protective force; and (4) rain-borne 'new healing powers' as a general therapeutic concept. No specific plant drug is described in this segment; healing is primarily ritual and cosmological in nature.

results.title_inferred: Ahura Mazda's Account of 99,999 Diseases and the Healing Powers of Manthra Spenta and Airyaman
results.original_text:
Angra Mainyu creates 99,999 diseases; Ahura Mazda counters with the Holy Manthra and with Airyaman.

I.
1. Ahura Mazda spake unto Spitama Zarathushtra, saying: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things, when I made this mansion [the Garothman, Paradise], the beautiful, the shining, seen afar (there may I go up, there may I arrive!)
2. 'Then the ruffian looked at me; the ruffian Angra Mainyu, the deadly, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases. So mayst thou heal me, thou most glorious Manthra Spenta!
3. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, swift-running steeds; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, high-humped camels; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
4. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand brown oxen that do not push; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. Unto thee will I give in return a thousand females big with young, of all species of small cattle; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
5. 'And I will bless thee with the fair blessing-spell of the righteous, the friendly blessing-spell of the righteous, that makes the empty swell to fulness and the full to overflowing, that comes to help him who was sickening, and makes the sick man sound again.
6. 'Manthra Spenta, the all-glorious, replied unto me: "How shall I heal thee? How shall I drive away from thee those nine diseases, and those ninety, those nine hundred, those nine thousand, and those nine times ten thousand diseases?"'

II.
7. The Maker Ahura Mazda called for Nairyo-sangha: Go thou, Nairyo-sangha, the herald, and drive towards the mansion of Airyaman, and speak thus unto him:
8. Thus speaks Ahura Mazda, the Holy One, unto thee: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things, when I made this mansion, the beautiful, the shining, seen afar (there may I ascend, there may I arrive!)
9. 'Then the ruffian looked at me; the ruffian Angra Mainyu, the deadly, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases. So mayst thou heal me, O Airyaman, the vow-fulfiller!
10. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, swift-running steeds; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, high-humped camels; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
11. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand brown oxen that do not push; I offer thee up a sacrifice [text breaks off].'
results.translation:
Angra Mainyu creates 99,999 diseases; Ahura Mazda counters them with the Holy Manthra [Sacred Word] and with Airyaman.

Part I.
1. Ahura Mazda spoke unto Spitama Zarathushtra [Zoroaster], saying: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things — when I fashioned this mansion [the Garothman, i.e., Paradise], the beautiful, the shining, seen from afar (there may I ascend, there may I arrive!) —
2. 'Then the villain fixed his gaze upon me; the villain Angra Mainyu [the Destructive Spirit], the deadly one, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases [totaling 99,999 diseases]. So may you heal me, O most glorious Manthra Spenta [Holy Word]!
3. 'In return I will give you a thousand swift, fleet-footed horses; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka [Genius of the Good Eye], made by Mazda and holy. In return I will give you a thousand swift, high-humped camels; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
4. 'In return I will give you a thousand brown oxen that do not gore; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. In return I will give you a thousand females heavy with young, of every species of small cattle; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
5. 'And I will bless you with the fair blessing-spell of the righteous, the kindly blessing-spell of the righteous — that which causes the empty to swell to fullness and the full to overflow, that which comes to the aid of one who was falling ill, and restores the sick man to health.
6. 'Manthra Spenta, the all-glorious, replied unto me: "How shall I heal you? How shall I drive away from you those nine diseases, and those ninety, those nine hundred, those nine thousand, and those nine times ten thousand diseases?"'

Part II.
7. The Maker, Ahura Mazda, summoned Nairyo-sangha [the divine herald]: Go, Nairyo-sangha, you who are the herald, and proceed to the mansion of Airyaman [the yazata of healing fellowship], and speak to him thus:
8. Thus speaks Ahura Mazda, the Holy One, unto you: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things — when I fashioned this mansion, the beautiful, the shining, seen from afar (there may I ascend, there may I arrive!) —
9. 'Then the villain fixed his gaze upon me; the villain Angra Mainyu, the deadly one, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases. So may you heal me, O Airyaman, fulfiller of vows!
10. 'In return I will give you a thousand swift, fleet-footed horses; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. In return I will give you a thousand swift, high-humped camels; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
11. 'In return I will give you a thousand brown oxen that do not gore; I offer up sacrifice [text breaks off].'

entities_mentioned.name: Angra Mainyu (Ahriman)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan deity of destruction and evil; Zoroastrian adversarial spirit
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the source of 99,999 diseases. Eznig's Refutatio Haeresiarum II attributes the corruption of good creatures to Ahriman's evil eye. Cf. Vd20.3.

entities_mentioned.name: Ahura Mazda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Supreme deity of Zoroastrianism; the Wise Lord
entities_mentioned.notes: Portrayed as afflicted by Angra Mainyu's diseases and seeking healing remedies through Manthra Spenta and Airyaman.

entities_mentioned.name: Manthra Spenta (Holy Manthra / Manthra Spenta)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Sacred Word or Holy Formula in Zoroastrianism; a healing incantation/liturgical utterance
entities_mentioned.notes: Invoked as a healing agent against the 99,999 diseases. Its healing mechanism is ritual/verbal (blessing-spell) rather than material. Acts as a therapeutic utterance.

entities_mentioned.name: Airyaman
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan yazata (divine being) of healing, fellowship, and fulfillment of vows; associated with the Airyaman Ishyo prayer
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'vow-fulfiller'; summoned via the herald Nairyo-sangha to heal the 99,999 diseases. A major healing figure in Zoroastrian tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Saoka
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan genius (yazata) of the good eye; benevolent counterpart to the evil eye
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to as 'good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.' Mentioned in Vd19.37. Receives sacrificial offerings of horses, camels, oxen, and pregnant small cattle.

entities_mentioned.name: Nairyo-sangha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan divine herald; identified with Atar (Fire) in Yasna 17.11
entities_mentioned.notes: Acts as messenger of Ahura Mazda, dispatched to summon Airyaman to perform healing.

entities_mentioned.name: Garothman
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Avestan term for Paradise / the highest heaven in Zoroastrian cosmology
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified in commentary as 'the mansion' referred to by Ahura Mazda.

entities_mentioned.name: 99,999 diseases (nine, ninety, nine hundred, nine thousand, nine times ten thousand)
entities_mentioned.notes: Formulaic enumeration of diseases attributed to Angra Mainyu. This is a literary/ritual total, not a clinical catalogue. The diseases are not individually named in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Blessing-spell of the righteous
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ashirvad or Asha-based benediction formula in Zoroastrian liturgy
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as therapeutic: 'makes the empty swell to fullness,' 'helps him who was sickening,' and 'makes the sick man sound again.' Functions as both a spiritual and quasi-medical remedy.

results.translator_notes: This passage is from Fargard 24 of the Vendidad (Videvdat), a Zoroastrian priestly law code. The segment is part of a mythological healing narrative rather than a practical medical text, yet it is highly relevant to the Materia Medica tradition because: (1) it posits a demonic etiology for all disease (Angra Mainyu as pathogen-creator); (2) it presents verbal/ritual remedies (Manthra Spenta, blessing-spells) as primary therapeutic agents; (3) it documents sacrificial animal offerings (horses, camels, oxen, pregnant small cattle) as part of a healing contract. The text is an English-language scholarly edition (likely Darmesteter's SBE translation with commentary), not a foreign-language original requiring translation per se — however, technical Avestan terms have been preserved and annotated in brackets per instructions. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence in section 11, indicating the source text was truncated. Cross-references to Vd19.37, Vd20.3, and Yasna 17.11 are present in the original footnotes and have been retained.
